# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Process Design. List its hierarchical levels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Process Design is the systematic methodology of conceiving, analyzing, and optimizing a chemical process that transforms raw materials into valuable products while satisfying mass, energy, and economic constraints. The hierarchical levels are:*
- *Level 0: Batch vs. Continuous decision*
- *Level 1: Input–Output structure*
- *Level 2: Recycle structure*
- *Level 3: Separation sequence*
- *Level 4: Energy integration (Pinch analysis)*
- *Level 5: Equipment sizing and cost estimation*

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the list of levels)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Six-Tenths Rule in process design? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*The Six-Tenths Rule (Williams' Law) is an empirical cost-scaling relation used to estimate the purchased cost of equipment at a different size from a known reference. It is given by:*

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^{0.6}
$$

*where $C$ is the cost and $S$ is the size. It is used because it provides a quick, reasonably accurate first-cut capital cost estimate without requiring detailed engineering drawings or vendor quotes. (3 marks: 1.5 for formula + 1.5 for use)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the Degrees of Freedom (DOF) for a CSTR receiving two inlet streams and producing one outlet stream, given $N_c$ components. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define DOF:**

$$
DOF = U - E
$$

**Step 2 — Count Unknowns $U$:**

- Inlet 1 component flows: $N_c$
- Inlet 2 component flows: $N_c$
- Outlet component flows: $N_c$
- Outlet $T$: 1
- Outlet $P$: 1
- Jacket $T_j$: 1
- Conversion $X$: 1

$$
U = 3N_c + 4
$$

**Step 3 — Count Independent Equations $E$:**

- Component mass balances: $N_c$
- Energy balance on reactor: 1
- Rate law: 1
- Jacket energy balance: 1

$$
E = N_c + 3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute DOF:**

$$
DOF = (3N_c + 4) - (N_c + 3) = 2N_c + 1
$$

**Step 5 — For Binary System ($N_c = 2$):**

$$
DOF = 2(2) + 1 = 5
$$

So 5 design variables (e.g., 4 inlet flow rates + 1 conversion) must be specified. **(7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for U count, 2 for E count, 2 for final DOF)**

#### (b) A heat exchanger costs ₹ 60,00,000 for an area of 120 m². Estimate the cost for an area of 300 m² using the Six-Tenths Rule. Comment on the economy of scale. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the rule:**

$$
C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{S_2}{S_1} \right)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
C_2 = 60{,}00{,}000 \times \left( \frac{300}{120} \right)^{0.6}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the size ratio:**

$$
\frac{300}{120} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power:**

$$
(2.5)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 2.5} = e^{0.6 \times 0.9163} = e^{0.5498} = 1.733
$$

**Step 5 — Final Cost:**

$$
C_2 = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 1.733 = \text{₹ } 1{,}03{,}98{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on Economy of Scale:**
The area increased by a factor of 2.5 (150% increase), but the cost increased only by 73.3%. This non-linear scaling reflects the **economy of scale** — larger plants are more cost-effective per unit capacity due to fixed overhead, standardized designs, and bulk procurement. **(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for numerical answer, 2 for comment)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the role of AI and Machine Learning in modern process design. Discuss at least four applications with examples. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
Modern process design is no longer purely static and first-principles-based. AI/ML augments design by enabling **fast prediction, inverse design, and real-time adaptation** to disturbances.

**Step 2 — Application 1: Surrogate Modeling**
Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are trained on data from detailed first-principles simulations (e.g., CFD reactor models) to predict reactor outlet composition orders of magnitude faster. *Example:* An ANN trained on 10,000 CFD runs of a steam methane reformer can predict CH₄ conversion in milliseconds vs. hours for CFD.

**Step 3 — Application 2: Soft Sensors**
Inferential models estimate hard-to-measure variables (e.g., composition) from easy measurements (T, P, flow). *Example:* A Random Forest model predicts distillation overhead composition from tray temperatures with RMSE < 0.5 mol%.

**Step 4 — Application 3: Real-Time Optimization (RTO)**
Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents continuously adjust setpoints (reflux ratio, steam flow) to maximize Economic Potential under disturbances like feed impurity. *Example:* RL controller in an ethylene plant increased profit by 3–5% over traditional MPC.

**Step 5 — Application 4: Generative Pathway Design**
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) propose novel reaction pathways for a target product. *Example:* DeepMind's AlphaFold-inspired models for retrosynthesis propose 10× more candidate routes than human experts.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
AI/ML transforms process design from a *one-time exercise* into a *continuous, data-driven discipline*. **(7 marks: 1 for intro, 1.5 each for 4 applications)**

#### (b) A distillation column separates a binary mixture of benzene and toluene. The feed contains 50 mol% benzene, distillate is 95 mol% benzene, and bottoms is 5 mol% benzene. The relative volatility is 2.5. Using the Fenske equation, determine the minimum number of stages at total reflux. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Fenske Equation:**

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{x_B} \right)_A \cdot \left( \frac{x_B}{x_D} \right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{AB}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Components:**
- Light key A = Benzene, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$
- Heavy key B = Toluene, $x_D = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$, $x_B = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$

**Step 3 — Compute the Inner Ratio:**

$$
\left( \frac{x_D}{x_B} \right)_A = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19
$$

$$
\left( \frac{x_B}{x_D} \right)_B = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator:**

$$
\ln (19 \times 19) = \ln(361) = 5.886
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Denominator:**

$$
\ln(2.5) = 0.916
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $N_{\min}$:**

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{5.886}{0.916} = 6.43
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, the minimum number of **ideal stages at total reflux is 7**. The actual operating column will need a few more stages, typically $N = 1.5 \times N_{\min} \approx 10$. **(7 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substituting values, 2 for log computation, 2 for final answer with comment)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **DOF questions:** Students commonly forget to include the **energy balance** as a separate equation, leading to an off-by-one error. Always list mass balance + energy balance + rate law + auxiliary equations explicitly.
> 2. **Six-Tenths Rule:** Do not use linear scaling ($C \propto S$). The exponent is 0.6, not 1. Many students write $(S_2/S_1)^1$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Fenske Equation:** The two ratios inside the logarithm are inverse of each other for the light and heavy key. Mixing up $x_D$ and $x_B$ for component B is a classic trap.
> 4. **Units:** Always show units in the final answer (e.g., ₹, m², mol%, K). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **AI/ML in PECHT866:** When asked "role of AI," do not write generic definitions of AI. Tie each point to a **chemical engineering specific** example (CFD surrogate, soft sensor, RL-RTO, GNN-retrosynthesis). Generic answers fetch ≤ 60% marks on this section.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Process Design = Synthesis + Analysis + Optimization + AI/ML layer.**
- **Six Hierarchical Levels:** Batch/Continuous → I/O → Recycle → Separation → Energy → Equipment.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** $DOF = U - E$; must equal 0 for a solvable system.
- **Six-Tenths Rule:** $C_2 = C_1 (S_2/S_1)^{0.6}$ — economy of scale, not linear scaling.
- **Conversion:** $X = (F_{A0} - F_A)/F_{A0}$.
- **Selectivity vs. Yield:** $Y = S \cdot X$.
- **Recycle Ratio:** $R = F_R/F_0$ — improves raw material use, increases equipment cost.
- **Reflux Ratio:** $R_r = L/D$; optimal $R_{r,\text{opt}} = 1.2 R_{r,\min}$.
- **Fenske:** Gives $N_{\min}$ at total reflux using $\alpha_{AB}$ and split ratios.
- **Underwood–Gilliland:** Gives $R_{r,\min}$ and $N$ at any $R_r$.
- **Pinch Analysis:** $\Delta T_{\min} \approx 10$ K; recoups 30–50% utility costs.
- **AI/ML Roles in PECHT866:**
  - **ANN** = Surrogate
  - **Random Forest/XGBoost** = Property prediction / cost estimation
  - **RL** = RTO / adaptive control
  - **GNN** = Generative retrosynthesis
  - **BERT/NLP** = HAZOP automation
  - **LSTM/Transformer** = Time-series process soft sensors
- **Three Pillars of a Valid Design:** Mass balance ✓, Energy balance ✓, Economic positivity (EP > 0) ✓.
- **Standard Reference State:** $T_{ref} = 25$ °C, $P_{ref} = 101.325$ kPa, $\Delta H_f^0 = 0$ for elements.
- **Heuristics to remember:**
  - Distill in order of relative volatility (largest α first).
  - Remove the most abundant component first.
  - Avoid vacuum and high pressure if not needed.
  - Recycle reactants, purge inerts.
  - Energy integrate before specifying utilities.
- **KTU 2024 Tip:** Part A carries 2–3 mark definition/short questions; Part B is 14 marks split into (a) 7 + (b) 7. Always attempt a flowchart or block diagram for 1–2 bonus marks in (b).
- **AI/ML "Hot Words" Examiner Loves:** surrogate, soft sensor, RTO, RL agent, digital twin, generative design, physics-informed neural network (PINN), Bayesian optimization.
- **Common SI Units to memorize:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $\Delta H_{vap}^{H_2O} = 2257$ kJ/kg.
- **Don't confuse:** *Process Design* (lifecycle of plant) vs. *Process Control* (real-time dynamics of running plant). AI/ML bridges both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development and Selection: Separation Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Separation Design** is the systematic engineering procedure used to identify, evaluate, and select the most appropriate physical or chemical technique for separating a mixture into its desired product streams. The **Development and Selection** phase is the highest-level design decision in chemical process engineering, where the *type* of separation operation (e.g., distillation, extraction, membrane separation, adsorption, crystallization) is chosen before any detailed column sizing or equipment rating is performed.

According to KTU 2024 scheme learning outcomes, this topic requires the engineer to:

1. Generate a **complete list of candidate separations** based on the feed mixture's physical, chemical, and thermodynamic properties.
2. Apply **heuristics and thermodynamic feasibility** rules to eliminate infeasible options.
3. Rank remaining candidates using **economic, energy, and environmental criteria**.
4. Select the most viable option for downstream simulation and detailed design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> The selection of a separation technique is a **heuristic-driven, hierarchical, multi-criteria decision** that precedes detailed mass-transfer or stage calculations. In modern practice, AI/ML tools (property prediction models, surrogate cost models, and multi-criteria optimizers) are increasingly integrated into the screening step.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Recyclable Sorting Plant

Imagine you are the manager of a **municipal recycling facility** that receives a mixed pile of plastic bottles, aluminum cans, glass, paper, and steel scrap. Your job is to produce **five pure, sellable product streams**. Before you buy any equipment (magnets, eddy-current separators, optical sorters, flotation tanks), you must first **decide which sorting principle to use** for each material.

- **Property difference (density)** → flotation tank separates paper from plastic.
- **Property difference (magnetism)** → magnetic drum pulls out steel.
- **Property difference (electrical conductivity)** → eddy current ejects aluminum.
- **Property difference (optical reflectivity)** → NIR sensor sorts glass.

Just like the recycling plant manager chooses the sorting *principle* first, a chemical engineer chooses the **separation *mechanism*** first. The recycle stream is the **feed mixture**; the pure product streams are the **distillate and bottoms**; and the property difference is the **thermodynamic driving force** (relative volatility, distribution coefficient, solubility, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Nearly **40–60% of total plant capital and operating cost** in bulk chemical industries (refineries, petrochemicals, gas processing) is associated with separation equipment. Choosing the wrong separation method early in design can lead to cost overruns of **10× to 100×** during later stages.

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Questions of Separation Selection

When a chemical engineer faces a new mixture, three foundational questions must be answered sequentially:

| # | Foundational Question | Engineering Translation |
|---|----------------------|-------------------------|
| Q1 | **What property difference exists between the components?** | Identify volatility, solubility, size, charge, density, reactivity, etc. |
| Q2 | **Which separation operation exploits that property difference?** | Map property → operation (e.g., volatility difference → distillation). |
| Q3 | **Is the operation feasible, safe, and economical at industrial scale?** | Apply feasibility filters (thermal degradation, azeotropes, cost). |

> [!TIP]
> **AI/ML Connection:** Question Q1 is being revolutionized by **graph neural networks (GNNs)** and **transformer-based property predictors** that estimate missing thermophysical properties in seconds — replacing weeks of laboratory experimentation.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decision Framework

Separation design follows a **top-down hierarchy** (analogous to the Douglas hierarchical approach for overall process design). Each level filters out infeasible options until one or two candidates remain for detailed simulation.

### Level 1 — Property Identification
- Determine the **feed composition**, **physical state** (V/L/S), and the **key property differences** (volatility, polarity, molecular size, charge, etc.).
- Output: A ranked list of exploitable property differences.

### Level 2 — Operation Mapping
- Map each property difference to a **list of candidate separation operations** using heuristic rules.
- Example mapping:
  - **Volatility difference (boiling point > 25 °C apart)** → Distillation.
  - **Volatility difference but azeotrope present** → Extractive or Azeotropic Distillation.
  - **Solubility difference in two immiscible liquids** → Liquid–Liquid Extraction.
  - **Solubility difference with temperature sensitivity** → Crystallization.
  - **Size difference at molecular scale** → Membrane Separation (Reverse Osmosis, Nanofiltration, UF).
  - **Charge difference** → Electrodialysis, Ion Exchange.
  - **Adsorption affinity** → Adsorption (Pressure Swing, Temperature Swing).

### Level 3 — Feasibility Screening
Eliminate candidates that fail any of the following:
- **Thermal degradation** of any component at operating temperature.
- **Azeotrope formation** that prevents reaching required purity by simple distillation.
- **Corrosion incompatibility** with available construction materials.
- **Safety hazards** (flammability, toxicity, reactivity).
- **Unfavorable phase behavior** (e.g., solid formation inside a distillation column).

### Level 4 — Economic and Energy Ranking
- Estimate **Total Annualized Cost (TAC)** = (Capital Cost / Payback Period) + Operating Cost.
- Rank remaining candidates by TAC, energy duty (GJ/tonne), and **CO₂ equivalent emissions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Heuristic:** *"If a clear volatility difference exists and no azeotrope forms, distillation is the default first choice."* — This is the single most tested heuristic in KTU ESE papers on separation selection.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table lists every key equation, driving force, and screening parameter you must know for ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Symbol / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Relative Volatility** | $\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} \;=\; \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$ (ideal) | Ratio of vapor–liquid K-values; key driver for distillation. | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Separation Factor (General)** | $S_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{(x_i/x_j)_{phase\,1}}{(x_i/x_j)_{phase\,2}}$ | General thermodynamic driving force for any equilibrium-based separation. | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Distribution Coefficient** | $K_D^{(i)} \;=\; \dfrac{C_i^{extract}}{C_i^{raffinate}}$ | Used in liquid–liquid and solid–liquid extraction. | dimensionless (or L/L) |
| 4 | **Selectivity** | $\beta \;=\; \dfrac{K_D^{(i)}}{K_D^{(j)}}$ | Ratio of distribution coefficients; $\beta > 1$ means component *i* preferentially enters extract. | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Fenske Equation (Min. Stages)** | $N_{min} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\tfrac{x_D}{x_B}\right)_i \big/ \left(\tfrac{x_D}{x_B}\right)_j\right]}{\ln \alpha_{ij}}$ | Minimum number of equilibrium stages at total reflux. | stages |
| 6 | **Underwood Equation (Min. Reflux)** | $\sum \dfrac{q \cdot z_i}{\alpha_{ij} - \theta} \;=\; 1$ | Implicit equation solved for root $\theta$ to compute $R_{min}$. | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Gilliland Correlation** | $X \;\text{vs}\; Y$ chart (Eduljee, 1975 fit) | Relates $(R - R_{min})/(R + 1)$ to $(N - N_{min})/(N + 1)$ for actual stages. | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Recovery** | $\eta_i \;=\; \dfrac{F \cdot z_i - B \cdot x_{B,i}}{F \cdot z_i}$ | Fraction of component *i* recovered in the product stream. | fraction or % |
| 9 | **Energy Duty** | $Q \;=\; D \cdot \lambda_{D} \;+\; B \cdot \lambda_{B}$ (reboiler + condenser) | Thermal energy per unit time required by distillation. | kW or MW |
| 10 | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $E_{sp} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_{product}}$ | Energy required per kg of product (key sustainability metric). | kJ/kg or MJ/tonne |
| 11 | **Carnot Efficiency (Max. Theoretical)** | $\eta_{Carnot} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Upper bound for thermodynamic efficiency of any thermal separation. | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Minimum Work of Separation** | $W_{min} \;=\; -RT \sum n_i \ln(y_i / x_i)$ | Reversible work needed to separate an ideal mixture. | J or kJ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Rule:** Note the use of `\vert`-free notation (e.g., `\dfrac` with parentheses and `\ln`). Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — it will break the column rendering.

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Practice

- The **relative volatility $\alpha_{ij}$** directly tells an engineer whether distillation is worth attempting. As a rule of thumb: if $\alpha_{ij} > 1.1$, distillation is feasible; if $\alpha_{ij} < 1.05$, distillation becomes uneconomical and an alternative (extractive distillation, membrane, adsorption) should be considered.
- The **Fenske equation** gives the *theoretical lower bound* on the number of stages — useful for screening whether a target purity is even achievable.
- The **minimum work of separation** establishes the **thermodynamic floor** for energy consumption. The actual energy duty $Q$ for any real distillation column is typically **5× to 20×** this minimum (a measure of process irreversibility).

> [!TIP]
> **AI/ML Connection:** Equations like Fenske and Underwood assume ideal or near-ideal behavior. **Surrogate models** (Gaussian Process Regression, Random Forest, Neural Networks) trained on process simulator data can rapidly predict $N_{min}$ and $R_{min}$ for **non-ideal, multi-component mixtures** in milliseconds — bypassing the need to solve Underwood's implicit root-finding repeatedly.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fenske Equation (Minimum Number of Stages)

The Fenske equation is a **cornerstone result** in separation design because it answers the question: *"What is the smallest possible number of equilibrium stages needed to achieve a specified split between two key components, assuming total reflux and constant relative volatility?"*

### Step 1 — Define the Stage-by-Stage Mass Balance at Total Reflux

At **total reflux**, there is **no feed** and **no product withdrawal**. All condensed vapor is returned as reflux, and all liquid is vaporized and sent up the column. The operating lines for both rectifying and stripping sections coincide with the **45° diagonal** of the McCabe–Thiele diagram. For a binary mixture with components *i* (light) and *j* (heavy), the equilibrium relationship for any stage $k$ is:

$$y_k \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha_{ij} \, x_k}{1 + (\alpha_{ij} - 1) x_k}$$

This is the **binary VLE relation** derived from Raoult's law for an ideal system.

### Step 2 — Express the Mass Balance in Terms of $K$-Values

For an ideal solution, $K_i = P_i^{sat} / P$ and $K_j = P_j^{sat} / P$. Therefore:

$$\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} \;=\; \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$$

This is a function of temperature only (pressure is constant). For a **constant relative volatility** assumption (often valid for narrow-boiling mixtures), $\alpha_{ij}$ is treated as constant across all stages.

### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium at Successive Stages

At total reflux, the vapor leaving stage $k$ is in equilibrium with the liquid leaving the same stage. By successive substitution, the ratio of compositions between the **top product (distillate, $D$)** and the **bottom product (bottoms, $B$)** is:

$$\left(\dfrac{x_{i,D}}{x_{j,D}}\right) \;=\; \alpha_{ij}^{N_{min}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{i,B}}{x_{j,B}}\right)$$

The derivation involves writing the equilibrium relationship for each of the $N_{min}$ stages in the rectifying section and stripping section and multiplying them together; all intermediate stage compositions cancel.

### Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm to Solve for $N_{min}$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides of the Stage 3 result and solving for $N_{min}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_{i,D} / x_{j,D}}{x_{i,B} / x_{j,B}}\right] \;&=\; N_{min} \cdot \ln \alpha_{ij} \\[6pt]
N_{min} \;&=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_{i,D} / x_{j,D}}{x_{i,B} / x_{j,B}}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{ij}} \\[6pt]
N_{min} \;&=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_{i,D}}{x_{i,B}}\right) \bigg/ \left(\dfrac{x_{j,D}}{x_{j,B}}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{ij}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Fenske equation** in its final, most commonly used form.

### Step 5 — Interpretation of the Result

- $N_{min}$ is the **absolute lower bound** on the number of equilibrium stages. The actual number of stages $N$ is always greater (typically $N \approx 1.5\,N_{min}$ to $2.5\,N_{min}$).
- $N_{min}$ depends **logarithmically** on the composition ratio — meaning that each 10-fold improvement in purity adds only a fixed number of stages.
- If $\alpha_{ij} = 1$ (no volatility difference), $N_{min} \to \infty$ — confirming that distillation is impossible.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Fenske Equation

**Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (i) and toluene (j) is to be separated in a distillation column. The feed is equimolar (z_benzene = 0.5, z_toluene = 0.5). The distillate must contain 95 mol% benzene and the bottoms must contain 95 mol% toluene. The average relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene is $\alpha_{ij} = 2.47$. Calculate the **minimum number of theoretical stages** required at total reflux.

**Step A — Extract Compositions**
- $x_{i,D} = 0.95$ (benzene in distillate), $x_{j,D} = 0.05$ (toluene in distillate)
- $x_{i,B} = 0.05$ (benzene in bottoms), $x_{j,B} = 0.95$ (toluene in bottoms)

**Step B — Compute the Composition Ratios**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{x_{i,D}}{x_{i,B}}\right) \;&=\; \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19.0 \\[4pt]
\left(\dfrac{x_{j,D}}{x_{j,B}}\right) \;&=\; \dfrac{0.05}{0.95} \;=\; 0.05263
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Form the Outer Ratio**

$$
\dfrac{x_{i,D}/x_{i,B}}{x_{j,D}/x_{j,B}} \;=\; \dfrac{19.0}{0.05263} \;\approx\; 361.0
$$

**Step D — Apply the Fenske Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} \;&=\; \dfrac{\ln(361.0)}{\ln(2.47)} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{5.886}{0.9046} \\[4pt]
\;&\approx\; 6.51
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Round Up and Interpret**

Since $N_{min}$ must be a whole number of stages, we round **up to 7 stages**. This means the column must have at least **7 theoretical (equilibrium) stages** at total reflux to achieve the specified separation. The actual column will require **more stages** (typically 10–15) to operate at a finite, economically reasonable reflux ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When KTU asks for $N_{min}$, always show: (i) the composition ratios, (ii) the outer ratio, and (iii) the natural logarithms. Marks are awarded for *each* of these intermediate steps, not just the final answer.

## 3.3 Derivation of Selectivity from Distribution Coefficients

For **liquid–liquid extraction**, the separation factor (selectivity) is derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta \;&=\; \dfrac{K_D^{(i)}}{K_D^{(j)}} \;\;\text{(definition)} \\[4pt]
K_D^{(i)} \;&=\; \dfrac{x_i^{E}}{x_i^{R}}, \quad K_D^{(j)} \;=\; \dfrac{x_j^{E}}{x_j^{R}} \quad \text{(definitions)} \\[4pt]
\beta \;&=\; \dfrac{x_i^{E}/x_i^{R}}{x_j^{E}/x_j^{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{(x_i^{E}/x_j^{E})}{(x_i^{R}/x_j^{R})} \;=\; S_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

where superscripts $E$ and $R$ denote the **extract** and **raffinate** phases. Thus the **selectivity $\beta$** and the **separation factor $S_{ij}$** are mathematically identical — they simply answer the same question in different contexts. The condition for feasible extraction is $\beta > 1$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Separation Design Workflow

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end decision flow** for selecting a separation technique, including the AI/ML augmentation points that are central to the PECHT866 course.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture Input] --> B[Identify Property Differences]
    B --> C{Phase: V / L / S?}
    C --> C1[Volatility diff] --> M1[Distillation Family]
    C --> C2[Solubility diff L-L] --> M2[Liquid-Liquid Extraction]
    C --> C3[Solubility diff T-sens] --> M3[Crystallization]
    C --> C4[Size diff molecular] --> M4[Membrane Separation]
    C --> C5[Charge diff] --> M5[Electrodialysis / Ion Exchange]
    C --> C6[Adsorption affinity] --> M6[Adsorption PSA TSA]

    M1 --> F{Feasibility Screen}
    M2 --> F
    M3 --> F
    M4 --> F
    M5 --> F
    M6 --> F

    F --> F1{Azeotrope?}
    F1 -- Yes --> F1A[Consider Extractive / Azeotropic Distillation]
    F1 -- No --> F2
    F --> F2{Thermal degradation?}
    F2 -- Yes --> F2A[Shift to Low-Temp Method: Membrane / Extraction]
    F2 -- No --> G
    F2A --> G

    G[Rank by TAC Energy CO2] --> H{Single Candidate?}
    H -- No --> H1[AI/ML Multi-Criteria Optimizer]
    H1 --> H2[Pareto-Front Analysis]
    H2 --> I[Select Final Operation]
    H -- Yes --> I
    I --> J[Detailed Simulation Aspen HYSYS ProMax]
    J --> K[Final Design and Rating]

    A --> P1[AI Property Predictor GNN]
    P1 --> B
    F --> P2[Surrogate Cost Model ML]
    P2 --> G
    H1 --> P3[Bayesian Optimization Loop]
    P3 --> H1
```

### 4.2 Reading Guide for the Diagram

- **Top-Down Logic:** The flow starts at the **feed mixture** and ends at the **rated equipment** — mirroring the engineer's natural thought process.
- **Branches C1–C6:** Each branch represents a **candidate separation mechanism** keyed to one specific property difference. Real feed mixtures may require **multiple sequential separations** (a separation train).
- **Decision Diamonds F, F1, F2, H:** Each is a **feasibility filter** or **ranking gate** that eliminates or down-ranks candidates.
- **AI/ML Augmentation (P1, P2, P3):** These dashed nodes show where modern AI/ML tools enter the workflow:
  - **P1 (GNN Property Predictor):** Quickly estimates missing vapor pressures, activity coefficients, solubilities.
  - **P2 (Surrogate Cost Model):** Replaces expensive CAPEX/OPEX correlations with a trained ML regressor.
  - **P3 (Bayesian Optimization):** Iteratively explores the discrete-continuous design space (choice of operation + operating conditions) to find a Pareto-optimal solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Alignment:** The course outcomes for PECHT866 Module 1 require students to **describe the role of AI/ML in accelerating property estimation and screening** for separation design. The diagram above is the canonical answer template for any 7-mark question that asks "Explain with a flowchart the steps in separation design selection."

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Quick-Reference Separation Selection

The table below maps the **most common property differences** in chemical engineering feeds to the **default separation operation**. This is the highest-frequency content tested under this topic.

| Property Difference | Default Operation | When to Switch |
|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Volatility, $\alpha > 1.1$, no azeotrope | **Distillation** | Azeotrope, thermal degradation |
| Volatility + third component added | **Azeotropic / Extractive Distillation** | High energy cost |
| Solubility in two immiscible liquids | **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** | Solvent recovery too expensive |
| Solubility varies strongly with T | **Crystallization** | Solid handling issues |
| Molecular size, MW difference | **Membrane: NF, RO, UF** | Fouling, low flux |
| Charge / ionic nature | **Electrodialysis, Ion Exchange** | Concentration polarization |
| Surface affinity / polarity | **Adsorption (PSA, TSA)** | Regeneration cost, adsorbent life |
| Density difference (large) | **Decantation, Centrifugation** | Emulsions, small density gap |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** The property families are **VS²CA²** — **V**olatility, **S**olubility, **S**ize, **C**harge, **A**ffinity, **A**ctivity. Each letter maps to a separation family. Recall this acronym in Part A and Part B answers for extra examiner goodwill.
