## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Define the acentric factor ($\omega$) and explain its physical significance in the Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) equation of state.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **acentric factor ($\omega$)** is a **dimensionless** number introduced by Pitzer (1955) that characterises the **non-sphericity** (acentricity) of a molecule. It is defined as:

$$\omega = -\log_{10}\left( \frac{P^{sat}}{P_c} \right)_{T_r = 0.7} - 1.000$$

**Physical Significance in SRK (3 marks breakdown):**
- **(1 mark)** Definition: Quantifies deviation of vapour pressure from the simple corresponding-states principle.
- **(1 mark)** Role in SRK: Enters the $\alpha$ function via the parameter $m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^2$, which corrects the attractive term $a(T)$ for molecular shape.
- **(1 mark)** Engineering importance: Enables SRK to accurately predict vapour-liquid equilibrium for non-spherical, real molecules (e.g., n-alkanes) instead of being limited to noble gases only.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Distinguish between an Equation of State (EOS) approach and an Activity Coefficient Model (ACM) approach for VLE calculations. State one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **EOS Approach** | **ACM Approach** |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Applicability | Both vapour and liquid (unified) | Liquid phase only |
| Typical Use | High pressure, non-polar | Low–moderate pressure, polar |
| Example | Peng-Robinson (PR) | NRTL or UNIQUAC |

- **(1 mark)** Correctly identifying EOS as unified.
- **(1 mark)** Correctly identifying ACM as liquid-phase only.
- **(1 mark)** Valid example of each (e.g., PR for EOS, NRTL for ACM).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the SRK equation of state parameters $a(T)$ and $b$ in terms of critical temperature $T_c$ and critical pressure $P_c$, clearly showing the role of the acentric factor $\omega$ in the temperature dependence of $a(T)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a binary system of acetone (1) and chloroform (2), explain why the Wilson equation fails to predict Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium (LLE), and describe how the NRTL model overcomes this limitation through its non-randomness parameter $\alpha_{12}$. Use relevant mathematical expressions in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the SRK EOS and critical-point conditions.

$$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a(T)}{V(V+b)}$$

Critical point conditions:
$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad ; \quad \left( \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the conditions, introduce $Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_c}{R T_c} = \dfrac{1}{3}$, and derive:

$$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$$

$$a_c = 0.42748 \, \frac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Introduce the $\alpha$ function via the acentric factor:

$$a(T) = a_c \cdot \alpha(T_r, \omega) \quad ; \quad \alpha = \left[ 1 + m\left(1 - \sqrt{T_r}\right) \right]^2$$

$$m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude that $\omega$ makes $a(T)$ a temperature-dependent quantity, enabling accurate vapour-pressure prediction for real (non-spherical) fluids.

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the Wilson equation:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2) + x_2 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{\Lambda_{21} x_1 + x_2} \right]$$

where $\Lambda_{ij} = \dfrac{V_j}{V_i} \exp\left( -\dfrac{\lambda_{ij} - \lambda_{ii}}{RT} \right) > 0$ always.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Demonstrate that since $\Lambda_{ij} > 0$, the Wilson equation is **mathematically incapable of yielding two stable liquid roots** — it cannot describe partial miscibility (LLE). For acetone-chloroform, which shows a negative deviation from Raoult's Law (minimum-boiling azeotrope), the system is highly non-random.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Introduce the NRTL equation:

$$\ln \gamma_i = \frac{\sum_j \tau_{ji} G_{ji} x_j}{\sum_k G_{ki} x_k} + \sum_j \frac{x_j G_{ij}}{\sum_k G_{kj} x_k} \left[ \tau_{ij} - \frac{\sum_m \tau_{mj} G_{mj} x_m}{\sum_k G_{kj} x_k} \right]$$

where $G_{ij} = \exp(-\alpha_{ij} \tau_{ij})$ and $\alpha_{ij}$ is the **non-randomness parameter** (typically $0.2$ to $0.47$).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude that the $\alpha_{ij}$ term allows $G_{ij}$ to be greater than 1 when $\tau_{ij} > 0$, which produces the curvature in $\ln \gamma_i$ vs. $x_i$ needed to satisfy the **spinodal condition** $\dfrac{d \ln \gamma_i}{dx_i} = 0$ — making LLE prediction possible.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Using the Joback group-contribution method, estimate the normal boiling point $T_b$, critical temperature $T_c$, critical pressure $P_c$, and critical volume $V_c$ of **ethanol (C₂H₆O)**. Given group contributions: ($-CH_3$: $T_b = 23.58$, $T_c = 0.0141$, $P_c = -0.0012$, $V_c = 65$; $-OH$ (alcohol): $T_b = 92.88$, $T_c = 0.0741$, $P_c = 0.0112$, $V_c = 28$). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the architectural differences between a purely data-driven Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) for predicting fugacity coefficients $\phi_i$ of a multi-component mixture. Specifically address how the PINN enforces thermodynamic consistency via the Gibbs-Duhem equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify molecular groups: Ethanol = 1 × ($–CH_3$) + 1 × ($–OH$); $N_{atoms} = 9$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $T_b$:

$$T_b = 198 + \sum_k N_k \cdot T_{b,k} = 198 + (1)(23.58) + (1)(92.88) = 314.46 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $T_c$:

$$\sum N_k T_{c,k} = (1)(0.0141) + (1)(0.0741) = 0.0882$$

$$T_c = T_b \left[ 0.584 + 0.965(0.0882) - (0.0882)^2 \right]^{-1}$$

$$T_c = 314.46 \cdot \left[ 0.584 + 0.0851 - 0.00778 \right]^{-1} = 314.46 \cdot \left[ 0.66132 \right]^{-1} = 475.5 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $P_c$:

$$P_c = \left( 0.113 + 0.0032 \cdot 9 - (1)(-0.0012) - (1)(0.0112) \right)^{-2} = (0.1496)^{-2} = 44.68 \, \text{bar}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $V_c$:

$$V_c = 17.5 + (1)(65) + (1)(28) = 110.5 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

*(Compare with literature: $T_c = 514 \, \text{K}$, $P_c = 61.4 \, \text{bar}$, $V_c = 168 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ — Joback has known errors of 5–10% for polar molecules.)*

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the **purely data-driven ANN**:
- Input: $(T, P, \mathbf{x})$ vector.
- Output: $\hat{\phi}_i$ (fugacity coefficient).
- Loss: $L_{data} = \sum_j \left( \hat{\phi}_i^{(j)} - \phi_i^{true,(j)} \right)^2$
- Limitation: No guarantee that predicted $\phi_i$ satisfies the Gibbs-Duhem equation or Maxwell construction.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the **PINN architecture**:
- Same architecture, but augmented loss function:
$$L_{total} = L_{data} + \lambda_1 L_{GD} + \lambda_2 L_{Maxwell}$$
where:
$$L_{GD} = \left[ \sum_i x_i \, d\ln \hat{\phi}_i \right]_{T,P}^2 = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain the **Gibbs-Duhem enforcement**:
- The Gibbs-Duhem equation $\sum_i x_i \, d\mu_i = -S \, dT + V \, dP$ requires that fugacity predictions remain thermodynamically consistent across compositions.
- The PINN adds a residual penalty term $\lambda_1 L_{GD}$ that minimises the Gibbs-Duhem violation, ensuring the predicted $\hat{\phi}_i$ satisfies an exact differential form.
- This is mathematically equivalent to enforcing that $\ln \gamma_i = \partial (n G^E / RT) / \partial n_i$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude that PINNs provide **extrapolation reliability**, **uncertainty quantification**, and **physical plausibility** — critical for chemical engineering deployment where out-of-distribution predictions are common.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (As Per KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ function** in SRK/PR derivations — examiners allocate ~2 marks specifically for the $\omega$-dependent $\alpha$ term. Students who leave $a$ as a constant lose these marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $Z_c = 1/3$ for SRK vs. $Z_c = 0.307$ for PR.** This is a known trap.
> 3. **In Wilson-vs-NRTL questions**, students often forget to state that $\Lambda_{ij} > 0$ is what restricts Wilson from handling LLE — this explicit mathematical observation carries 2 marks.
> 4. **In AI/ML questions**, avoid vague statements like "the model is better." Always quantify: cite accuracy metrics (e.g., MAE on $T_c$ < 2%), uncertainty bounds, or thermodynamic consistency residuals.
> 5. **Unit conversions** — many students write $T_c$ in °C instead of K, causing a 1-mark deduction. Always use **SI or KTU-consistent units** (K, bar, mol).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermodynamic Model Selection & Property Estimation:**

- ⭐ **EOS vs ACM:** EOS is unified (vapour + liquid); ACM applies to liquid only.
- ⭐ **PR vs SRK:** PR gives better liquid density; SRK is simpler and faster for VLE.
- ⭐ **SRK parameters:** $b = RT_c / (8 P_c)$; $a_c = 0.42748 R^2 T_c^2 / P_c$.
- ⭐ **Acentric factor:** Enters via $\alpha$ function; $\omega = 0$ for noble gases, $> 0$ for non-spherical molecules.
- ⭐ **Wilson limitation:** Cannot predict LLE because $\Lambda_{ij} > 0$ always.
- ⭐ **NRTL advantage:** Non-randomness parameter $\alpha_{ij}$ enables LLE prediction.
- ⭐ **UNIQUAC structure:** Combinatorial (entropic) + Residual (enthalpic) contributions.
- ⭐ **UNIFAC:** Predictive ACM using group contributions — use when no experimental data exists.
- ⭐ **Joback method:** Group-contribution for $T_b$, $T_c$, $P_c$, $V_c$; errors of 5–10% for polar species.
- ⭐ **Lee-Kesler:** Substitute for $\omega$ when vapour pressure at $T_r = 0.7$ is unavailable.
- ⭐ **Fugacity coefficient:** $\ln \phi = (Z-1) - \ln(Z-B) - (A/B) \ln(1 + B/Z)$ for SRK.
- ⭐ **AI/ML integration:** Always enforce thermodynamic consistency (Gibbs-Duhem, Maxwell) via physics-informed loss functions in surrogate models.
- ⭐ **Selection rule of thumb:** High-P + non-polar → EOS; Low-P + polar → ACM; No data → UNIFAC.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The course PECHT866 expects students to bridge classical thermodynamics with AI/ML — every property prediction should be justified by both data and physics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Reservoir Image Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oil Reservoir Image Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Segmentation** in the context of oil reservoir characterization is the pixel-level classification of digital rock images (such as thin sections, Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) micrographs, micro-CT scans, and FIB-SEM tomographs) into distinct petrophysical classes — primarily **pore space, rock matrix (grains), fractures, and organic matter (kerogen)**. It is a fundamental step in the digital rock physics (DRP) workflow that enables the quantitative computation of porosity, permeability, pore-size distribution, and rock-typing parameters from 2D or 3D imagery.

Mathematically, given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$, segmentation produces a label map $L \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\}^{H \times W}$ where each pixel is assigned one of $K$ mutually exclusive class labels.

$$L(i,j) = \arg\max_{k \in \{0,\dots,K-1\}} \; P(Y = k \mid X = I(i,j))$$

where $Y$ is the random variable representing the true class at spatial location $(i,j)$ and $X$ is the observed pixel intensity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Image segmentation forms the bridge between raw petrographic imaging and downstream reservoir simulation. Without accurate segmentation, no Digital Rock Physics (DRP) workflow can yield reliable absolute permeability, capillary pressure, or relative permeability curves.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Geologist's Coloring Book"

Imagine a petroleum geologist sitting at a microscope looking at a thin section of sandstone. She carefully traces every pore with a **blue marker**, every quartz grain with a **yellow marker**, and every clay particle with a **green marker**. What she is doing by hand is exactly what an image segmentation algorithm does automatically — but at millions of pixels per second, with sub-micron precision, and without fatigue.

Now imagine scaling that task to a 3D micro-CT volume with **one billion voxels**. A human cannot do this — but a trained U-Net or Mask R-CNN can in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Significance:** Reservoir rock images contain extremely subtle textural variations. A pore of 2 microns versus 5 microns changes predicted permeability by an order of magnitude. Therefore, segmentation accuracy is not a "nice-to-have" — it directly controls the accuracy of reservoir simulation forecasts used in field development planning, enhanced oil recovery (EOR) design, and carbon sequestration storage capacity estimation.

### 1.3 Reservoir Image Modalities (Foundation Knowledge)

| Modality | Imaging Principle | Typical Resolution | Segmentation Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Thin Section** | Transmitted light microscopy | 0.5 – 2 µm | Pore vs. Grain |
| **SEM / BSE** | Electron backscatter | 5 – 50 nm | Pore, Clay, Kerogen |
| **Micro-CT** | X-ray attenuation | 0.5 – 5 µm | Macro-pore network |
| **FIB-SEM** | Focused ion beam milling + SEM | 5 – 10 nm | Nano-pore, Organic matter |
| **Core CT** | Medical/industrial CT scanner | 100 – 500 µm | Fractures, vugs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Segmentation Pipeline — Why Each Step Matters

A standard reservoir image segmentation workflow consists of five sequential stages. Skipping or mis-ordering any stage degrades downstream porosity error significantly.

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & Calibration**
- Acquire raw images at the highest feasible resolution.
- Calibrate intensity histogram using known phantoms (e.g., quartz and air).
- *Why:* Uncalibrated intensities make thresholding ambiguous and bias CNN training.

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing**
- Apply non-local means or anisotropic diffusion filtering.
- Normalize intensity to zero-mean unit-variance per image.
- Augment with rotations, flips, and elastic deformations.
- *Why:* Reduces noise-induced misclassification and improves CNN generalization on small labelled datasets.

**Stage 3 — Segmentation Algorithm**
- Traditional: Otsu thresholding, watershed, region growing, k-means clustering.
- Deep Learning: U-Net, SegNet, DeepLabv3+, Mask R-CNN, Swin-UNet.
- *Why:* Choice determines the boundary precision achievable.

**Stage 4 — Post-processing**
- Morphological operations (opening, closing, hole filling).
- Connected-component analysis to remove spurious small regions.
- *Why:* Cleans artefacts and enforces topological consistency of pore networks.

**Stage 5 — Quantitative Analysis**
- Compute porosity $\phi$, specific surface area, two-point correlation function.
- Feed extracted pore network into a pore-network model for permeability simulation.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

**Porosity from Segmented Image**

$$\phi = \frac{N_{\text{pore}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i,j} \mathbb{1}[L(i,j) = 0]$$

where $N_{\text{pore}}$ is the number of voxels labelled as pore (class 0), $N$ is total voxel count, and $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function.

**Two-Point Correlation Function (geological realism check)**

$$S_2(r) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[L(i) = k] \cdot \mathbb{1}[L(i+r) = k]$$

**Entropy of the predicted probability map** (uncertainty estimation)

$$H(p) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log p_k$$

### 2.3 Loss Functions Used in Reservoir Image Segmentation

**Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) Loss**

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

**Dice Loss** (handles class imbalance between rare pores and dominant matrix)

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2\sum_{i} y_i \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} y_i + \sum_{i} \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a Laplace smoothing constant (typically $10^{-6}$) to avoid division by zero.

**Focal Loss** (focuses learning on hard, misclassified pixels)

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Focal}} = -\alpha_t (1-p_t)^{\gamma} \log(p_t)$$

with focusing parameter $\gamma = 2$ and class weighting $\alpha_t = 0.25$ typical.

**Combined Dice + CE Loss** (KTU standard in literature)

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} + (1-\lambda) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pixel-wise predicted probability | $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $z$ = logit |
| 2 | IoU (Jaccard Index) | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert X \cap Y \vert}{\vert X \cup Y \vert}$ | $X$ predicted, $Y$ ground truth |
| 3 | Dice Coefficient (F1) | $D = \frac{2 \vert X \cap Y \vert}{\vert X \vert + \vert Y \vert}$ | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 4 | Pixel Accuracy | $\text{PA} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Boolean pixel match |
| 5 | Porosity from segmentation | $\phi = \frac{N_{\text{pore}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Surface Area (Koenderink) | $A = \frac{1}{2}\sum \vert \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{dS} \vert$ | $\mu\text{m}^2$ |
| 7 | Mean Curvature (pore wall) | $H = \frac{\kappa_1 + \kappa_2}{2}$ | $\mu\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Convolution operation | $(f * g)[n] = \sum_{m} f[m]\,g[n-m]$ | Feature extraction |
| 9 | Transposed Convolution | $g = W^T f + b$ | Decoder upsampling |
| 10 | Softmax (multi-class) | $p_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probability vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** In markdown tables above, all absolute-value operators use the word "vert" notation indirectly via set cardinality. Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells, as it breaks markdown parsing.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Segmentation-driven digital rock analysis is used in:
1. **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS):** Estimating $\text{CO}_2$ storage capacity in depleted reservoirs.
2. **Shale Gas & Tight Oil:** Quantifying organic-rich nanopores for gas-in-place calculations.
3. **EOR Screening:** Identifying micro-fracture networks favourable for low-salinity or polymer flooding.
4. **Reservoir Simulation Input:** Generating pore-network models used to derive relative permeability curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Dice Coefficient from Set-Theoretic Axioms

We start from the **F1-score** formulation in set theory. For two finite sets $A$ and $B$ representing predicted and ground-truth pore regions:

**Step 1 — Define the Intersection Cardinality**

$$\vert A \cap B \vert = \sum_{i \in \Omega} \mathbb{1}[i \in A] \cdot \mathbb{1}[i \in B]$$

**Step 2 — Recall the Inclusion-Exclusion Principle**

$$\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$$

**Step 3 — Define Precision and Recall**

$$P = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert}, \qquad R = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert B \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Combine via Harmonic Mean (F1 definition)**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $P$ and $R$ into $F_1$ expression**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert^2}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}}{\dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert(\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by cancelling the denominator**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert^2}{\vert A \cap B \vert (\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)}$$

**Step 7 — Final cancellation yields the Dice Coefficient**

$$F_1 = D = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

This derivation shows that **Dice Coefficient is mathematically equivalent to the F1-score**, which is why it is preferred over IoU for imbalanced classes (rare pores).

### 3.2 Derivation of Porosity from a Segmented Volume

Consider a binary segmented 3D micro-CT image of size $N_x \times N_y \times N_z$. Let the label map assign $L(x,y,z) = 0$ for pore and $L(x,y,z) = 1$ for grain.

**Step 1 — Define the indicator function for pore voxels**

$$\mathbb{1}_{\text{pore}}(x,y,z) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } L(x,y,z) = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Count pore voxels by summation**

$$N_{\text{pore}} = \sum_{x=1}^{N_x}\sum_{y=1}^{N_y}\sum_{z=1}^{N_z} \mathbb{1}_{\text{pore}}(x,y,z)$$

**Step 3 — Total voxel count**

$$N_{\text{total}} = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot N_z$$

**Step 4 — Porosity definition**

$$\phi = \frac{N_{\text{pore}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{N_x N_y N_z}\sum_{x,y,z}\mathbb{1}_{\text{pore}}(x,y,z)$$

**Step 5 — Convert voxel count to physical volume**

$$V_{\text{pore}} = N_{\text{pore}} \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z, \qquad V_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{total}} \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

where $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ are voxel edge lengths in micrometres.

**Step 6 — Final porosity in physical units**

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{V_{\text{pore}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{N_{\text{pore}}}{N_{\text{total}}}}$$

This is dimensionless and typically expressed as a percentage ($0 \leq \phi \leq 1$).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional vs Deep Learning Segmentation

| Criterion | Traditional Methods | Deep Learning Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Principle** | Hand-crafted features + heuristics | Learned hierarchical features |
| **Typical Algorithm** | Otsu, Watershed, k-means | U-Net, Mask R-CNN, DeepLab |
| **Training Data Required** | None / minimal | Hundreds to thousands of labelled images |
| **Pore-Boundary Accuracy** | Moderate (sub-pixel errors) | High (sub-voxel possible with attention) |
| **Robustness to Noise** | Low | High (with augmentation) |
| **Class Imbalance Handling** | Manual weighting | Focal loss, Dice loss |
| **Computational Cost (Inference)** | CPU, milliseconds | GPU, tens of milliseconds |
| **Interpretability** | High (transparent math) | Low (black-box features) |
| **Best Use Case** | Quick estimates, low-stakes screening | Production digital rock workflows |
| **3D Native Support** | Yes (ITK, Fiji plugins) | Requires 3D U-Net / V-Net variants |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 End-to-End Reservoir Image Segmentation Pipeline

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** Define image segmentation in the context of digital rock analysis. List any three imaging modalities commonly used for reservoir rock characterization.

**Model Answer:**
Image segmentation in digital rock analysis is the pixel-level classification of petrographic images into distinct rock components such as pore space, grains, and organic matter. It converts raw intensity data into spatially-resolved petrophysical labels, enabling quantitative computation of porosity, permeability, and pore-network topology.

Three imaging modalities:
1. **Optical thin-section microscopy** — transmitted light, 0.5–2 µm resolution.
2. **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** — electron backscatter, 5–50 nm resolution.
3. **Micro-Computed Tomography (Micro-CT)** — X-ray attenuation, 0.5–5 µm resolution.

*(Examiner awards: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for segmentation context, 1 mark for modalities.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the Dice coefficient, and why is it preferred over pixel accuracy for evaluating reservoir pore segmentation?

**Model Answer:**
The Dice coefficient $D$ is defined as

$$D = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are predicted and ground-truth pore sets. It ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

It is preferred over pixel accuracy because pore pixels typically constitute less than 20% of the image (extreme class imbalance). A trivial classifier predicting "all matrix" would score >80% accuracy but Dice = 0, correctly exposing its failure.

*(Examiner awards: 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for class-imbalance justification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B is permitted.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the complete image segmentation pipeline for reservoir rock images. Discuss the role of pre-processing and post-processing stages in determining final segmentation accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The pipeline consists of five stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Sample preparation, mounting, and imaging using appropriate modality (thin section, SEM, micro-CT). Calibration with phantoms ensures intensity reproducibility.

2. **Pre-processing** — 
   - *Filtering:* Non-local means or anisotropic diffusion to suppress acquisition noise while preserving pore edges.
   - *Normalization:* Zero-mean unit-variance per image to harmonize intensity across imaging sessions.
   - *Augmentation:* Random rotations, flips, and elastic deformations to artificially expand the training set and reduce CNN overfitting.

3. **Segmentation Algorithm** — Either classical (Otsu + watershed) or deep learning (U-Net, Mask R-CNN).

4. **Post-processing** — 
   - Morphological closing to fill isolated grain voxels inside pore bodies.
   - Hole-filling and small-component removal to enforce pore-network connectivity.

5. **Quantitative Analysis** — Compute $\phi$, two-point correlation function, and pore-size distribution.

The role of pre/post-processing: Pre-processing enhances signal-to-noise ratio and class separability; post-processing enforces topological consistency. Together they reduce porosity error from typically 8–12% (raw CNN) down to 2–4% (with full pipeline).

*(7 marks: 2 for pipeline diagram, 2 for pre-processing role, 2 for post-processing role, 1 for error reduction context.)*

**(b)** Derive the Dice loss function used for training a CNN-based pore segmentation model. Show why the $\epsilon$ smoothing term is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the Dice coefficient:

$$D = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

For binary segmentation with predictions $\hat{y}_i \in [0,1]$ and ground truth $y_i \in \{0,1\}$, set-cardinality becomes sum:

$$D = \frac{2 \sum_i y_i \hat{y}_i}{\sum_i y_i + \sum_i \hat{y}_i}$$

Dice loss is defined as $1 - D$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i y_i \hat{y}_i}{\sum_i y_i + \sum_i \hat{y}_i}$$

Adding the smoothing term $\epsilon$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i y_i \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i y_i + \sum_i \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}$$

**Why $\epsilon$ is necessary:** During early training iterations, if all ground-truth pore pixels happen to be absent in a batch, $\sum_i y_i = 0$ causing division by zero and gradient explosion. The constant $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$) guarantees numerical stability and avoids NaN losses.

*(7 marks: 2 for Dice coefficient definition, 2 for loss transformation, 2 for $\epsilon$ derivation, 1 for numerical stability reasoning.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast traditional threshold-based segmentation (Otsu's method) with deep learning-based segmentation (U-Net) for reservoir pore identification. Provide mathematical justification for cases where each method fails. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Otsu's Method:**
- Minimizes intra-class intensity variance:

$$\sigma^2_{\text{within}}(t) = w_0(t)\sigma_0^2(t) + w_1(t)\sigma_1^2(t)$$

Optimal threshold $t^*$ maximizes between-class variance: $\sigma_B^2(t^*) = \max_t \sigma_B^2(t)$.

- **Fails** when pore and grain intensity histograms overlap heavily (e.g., micro-CT of carbonate rocks where both phases have similar X-ray attenuation).

**U-Net:**
- Encoder-decoder with skip connections, trained via gradient descent.
- **Fails** when training data is scarce (< 50 labelled images) or when test images contain rock textures not represented in training distribution.

**Mathematical Justification of Otsu Failure:** If the pore mean intensity $\mu_0$ and grain mean $\mu_1$ satisfy $\vert \mu_0 - \mu_1 \vert < 2\sigma$ where $\sigma$ is the within-class standard deviation, the Bayesian optimal error is bounded below by ~25%, which Otsu (being unimodal threshold) cannot beat.

**Mathematical Justification of U-Net Failure:** With $N$ training images, the VC dimension bound gives generalization error:

$$E_{\text{gen}} \leq \sqrt{\frac{h \log(2N/h) + \log(2/\delta)}{N}}$$

For small $N$, this bound is large, meaning the model memorizes training images rather than learning generalizable pore features.

*(7 marks: 2 for Otsu derivation, 2 for U-Net description, 2 for failure conditions, 1 for synthesis.)*

**(b)** A 256 × 256 segmented thin-section of a sandstone shows that 18,500 pixels are labelled as pore and 47,000 pixels are correctly classified as grain (True Positive grain). If the false positive rate for pore is 2,300 pixels and 31,200 pixels are correctly identified as pore, calculate: (i) Pixel Accuracy, (ii) IoU for pore class, (iii) Dice coefficient for pore class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given data:
- TP (pore) = 31,200
- FP (pore) = 2,300
- FN (pore) = 18,500 (pore pixels labelled as grain)
- TN (grain) = 47,000 (Note: this is grain correctly identified; will reassign based on standard confusion matrix)

**Verification of total pixels:**

$$\text{Total} = 256 \times 256 = 65{,}536$$

$$\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN} + \text{TN} = 31{,}200 + 2{,}300 + 18{,}500 + 13{,}536 = 65{,}536 \checkmark$$

(Note: Using TN = 13,536 from arithmetic consistency; the "47,000 grain TP" given in the question represents grain-correct classified, which contributes to TN in the pore-class confusion matrix when pore is the positive class.)

**(i) Pixel Accuracy:**

$$\text{PA} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{31{,}200 + 13{,}536}{65{,}536} = \frac{44{,}736}{65{,}536} = 0.6826$$

$$\text{PA} \approx 68.26\%$$

**(ii) IoU for Pore Class:**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{31{,}200}{31{,}200 + 2{,}300 + 18{,}500} = \frac{31{,}200}{52{,}000} = 0.6000$$

$$\text{IoU} = 60.00\%$$

**(iii) Dice Coefficient for Pore Class:**

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 31{,}200}{2 \times 31{,}200 + 2{,}300 + 18{,}500}$$

$$D = \frac{62{,}400}{84{,}200} = 0.7411$$

$$D \approx 74.11\%$$

**Interpretation:** Despite pixel accuracy of only 68.26%, the Dice score of 74.11% reflects reasonable pore overlap with ground truth. The discrepancy between PA and Dice confirms that pixel accuracy is misleading under class imbalance.

*(7 marks: 2 for confusion matrix setup, 2 for PA and IoU, 2 for Dice, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusion matrix notation slip-up:** Many students mix up TP/FP/FN definitions for multi-class problems. For pore-vs-grain binary segmentation with pore as positive class, "True Positive" means a pore pixel correctly predicted as pore — not the reverse.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert counts to fractions:** Always divide by total pixel count for accuracy; reporting absolute numbers without normalization loses marks.
> 3. **Omitting the smoothing term explanation:** When asked about Dice loss in derivation questions, students often skip the $\epsilon$ rationale, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong axis in IoU vs Dice:** Some students interchange numerator and denominator. Memorize: $\text{IoU} = \text{Intersection} / \text{Union}$, and $\text{Dice} = 2 \times \text{Intersection} / \text{Sum of areas}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Image segmentation** = pixel-wise classification of rock images into pore / grain / clay / kerogen.
- ✅ **Imaging modalities**: Optical, SEM, Micro-CT, FIB-SEM — each with specific resolution and segmentation target.
- ✅ **Pipeline stages**: Acquisition → Pre-processing → Segmentation → Post-processing → Quantitative analysis.
- ✅ **Otsu's method** minimizes intra-class variance; optimal when pore-grain intensity histograms are bimodal.
- ✅ **U-Net** uses encoder-decoder with skip connections; gold standard for biomedical and reservoir segmentation.
- ✅ **Dice coefficient** = $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$; preferred over pixel accuracy for imbalanced pore classes.
- ✅ **IoU (Jaccard)** = $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$; stricter than Dice.
- ✅ **Porosity formula**: $\phi = \dfrac{N_{\text{pore}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ (dimensionless, $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$).
- ✅ **Dice Loss** = $1 - D$; the $\epsilon$ smoothing term prevents division by zero during early training.
- ✅ **Focal Loss** uses $(1-p_t)^{\gamma}$ with $\gamma = 2$ to focus on hard pixels in imbalanced scenes.
- ✅ **Class imbalance** is the central challenge: pores typically <20% of rock image — always use Dice or Focal Loss, never raw cross-entropy alone.
- ✅ **Evaluation metrics must always be reported alongside pixel accuracy** — single-metric reporting is a KTU valuation red flag.
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: porosity computation, pore-network modelling, permeability estimation, CCS storage capacity, EOR screening.
- ✅ **Quantitative metrics range**: IoU and Dice both $\in [0,1]$; Pixel Accuracy $\in [0,1]$; Porosity $\phi \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ **Skip connections in U-Net** preserve high-resolution spatial information lost during downsampling — critical for thin pore throats.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Early Applications of AI Techniques in Chemical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Early Applications of AI Techniques in Chemical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** in the context of Chemical Engineering refers to the development of computational systems capable of performing tasks that traditionally require human intelligence, including reasoning, learning, pattern recognition, and decision-making, applied to chemical process design, operation, control, optimization, and safety analysis.

The **early applications of AI techniques in Chemical Engineering** (spanning the late 1970s to mid-1990s) primarily encompassed:

- **Expert Systems (Knowledge-Based Systems, KBS)**
- **Rule-Based Reasoning Systems**
- **Fuzzy Logic Controllers**
- **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) — early perceptron and back-propagation models**
- **Genetic Algorithms (GAs) for optimization**
- **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) systems**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is to recognize how **symbolic AI** (rule-based, expert systems) transitioned into **data-driven AI** (neural networks, fuzzy systems) within the chemical engineering domain, forming the historical bedrock for modern machine learning and deep learning applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **senior plant operator** with 30 years of experience who can instantly diagnose a faulty heat exchanger just by glancing at a few temperature gauges. He has never formally calculated the Reynolds number at that moment — his decisions come from **accumulated heuristics** ("if the temperature in the upper tray drops suddenly and pressure rises, the condenser is likely fouled").

Now imagine capturing that operator's brain into a computer. That is essentially what the **first generation of AI in chemical engineering** tried to do. In the 1980s, engineers did not have the massive datasets or GPU power we have today. Instead, they relied on:

1. **Expert systems** → Mimicking human expert reasoning through IF–THEN rules
2. **Fuzzy logic** → Handling imprecise sensor readings ("slightly hot", "moderately viscous")
3. **Early ANNs** → Learning input-output mappings from limited process data
4. **Genetic algorithms** → Searching for optimal process conditions using biological evolution metaphors

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Snapshot:** The **Fault Diagnosis Expert System** developed for the **Monsanto Luling plant** in the 1980s used IF–THEN rules to diagnose problems in a chemical reactor, reducing unplanned downtime by approximately **30%** — a landmark achievement in industrial AI.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Accuracy of early expert systems:** Typically **85%–95%** in narrow domains
- **Rule base size (typical):** **200–2,000 IF–THEN rules**
- **Inference speed:** **< 1 second** for forward-chaining systems
- **Knowledge acquisition bottleneck:** Estimated **2–5 person-years** for a medium-scale process

> [!TIP]
> **Cognitive Level Mapping (Bloom's Taxonomy):** This topic primarily addresses KTU CO1 (Understand) and CO2 (Apply) cognitive levels — recognizing the historical AI techniques and their domain-specific deployments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Early AI Techniques in Chemical Engineering

The first wave of AI in chemical engineering can be classified into **four primary paradigms**, each addressing a unique class of chemical engineering problems.

#### A. Expert Systems (Knowledge-Based Systems)

Expert systems were the **flagship AI technology of the 1980s** in chemical engineering. They consisted of:

- **Knowledge Base:** A collection of IF–THEN rules derived from human experts
- **Inference Engine:** The reasoning mechanism (forward or backward chaining)
- **User Interface:** A natural language or menu-driven front end
- **Explanation Facility:** Traces the reasoning chain (e.g., WHY and HOW explanations)

**Canonical Chemical Engineering Examples:**

| System | Year | Domain | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CONPHYDE** | 1982 | Distillation | Physical property estimation |
| **PICON** | 1984 | Process Control | Real-time alarm analysis |
| **FALCON** | 1984 | Fault Diagnosis | On-line process fault detection |
| **DECADE** | 1980s | Catalyst Design | Catalyst selection for reactions |
| **BIOEXPERT** | Late 1980s | Bioprocess | Fermentation monitoring |

#### B. Fuzzy Logic Systems

Introduced by **Lotfi Zadeh (1965)**, fuzzy logic allowed chemical engineers to handle the **inherent imprecision** in sensor data, linguistic operator descriptions, and uncertain thermodynamic measurements.

**Core Concept:** A variable's membership in a set is not binary (0 or 1) but continuous in the interval **[0, 1]**.

For example, the linguistic variable "Reactor Temperature" can be:
- **Cold** → membership $\mu_{cold} = 0.8$ at 320 K
- **Normal** → membership $\mu_{normal} = 0.6$ at 380 K
- **Hot** → membership $\mu_{hot} = 0.9$ at 450 K

#### C. Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) — First Generation

The **Perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958)** and later the **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with Back-Propagation (Rumelhart, 1986)** were the early neural architectures. Chemical engineers used them for:

- Modeling nonlinear **reaction kinetics**
- Predicting **distillation tower compositions**
- Estimating **heat transfer coefficients**
- Soft-sensing of **unmeasurable variables** (e.g., polymer melt index)

#### D. Genetic Algorithms (GAs)

Inspired by **Darwinian evolution**, GAs were used for:

- Optimizing **reactor operating conditions**
- Designing **optimal heat exchanger networks**
- Scheduling **batch processes**
- Estimating **kinetic parameters** from experimental data

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **IF-THEN Rule Format** | IF (antecedent) THEN (consequent) | Backbone of expert systems; encodes heuristic knowledge |
| **Fuzzy Membership Function (Triangular)** | $\mu_A(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, \frac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | Maps crisp input to fuzzy set membership |
| **Fuzzy AND (t-norm, min)** | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Combines multiple fuzzy conditions in a rule |
| **Fuzzy OR (s-norm, max)** | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Aggregates alternative conditions |
| **Centroid Defuzzification** | $y^* = \frac{\int y \cdot \mu(y) \, dy}{\int \mu(y) \, dy}$ | Converts fuzzy output to crisp control action |
| **Perceptron Output** | $y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$ | Early neural computation unit |
| **Sigmoid Activation** | $f(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Nonlinear squashing for ANN output |
| **Back-Propagation Weight Update** | $w_{ij}^{(t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | Iterative learning rule; $\eta$ = learning rate |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $E = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} (y_k - \hat{y}_k)^2$ | Training objective for ANNs |
| **GA Fitness Function (Reactor Yield)** | $F(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{Y_{product}(\mathbf{x})}{Y_{max}}$ | Maximizes yield over operating variables $\mathbf{x}$ |
| **GA Crossover (Single Point)** | $x_{offspring} = \alpha x_{parent1} + (1-\alpha) x_{parent2}$ | Generates new candidate solutions |
| **CSTR Mass Balance (AI-Enhanced)** | $\frac{dC_A}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(C_{A0} - C_A) - k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$ | ANN approximates the nonlinear term $k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In KTU board exam answers, always define variables explicitly. For instance, in the GA fitness function, state that $\mathbf{x}$ is a vector of decision variables (temperature, pressure, residence time), and $Y_{max}$ is the theoretical maximum yield. Marks are awarded for complete notation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Chemical Engineering Domain | Economic / Safety Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Expert Systems | Fault diagnosis, alarm management | **20–40% reduction** in operator response time |
| Fuzzy Logic | pH control, distillation control | Robustness against sensor noise |
| Early ANNs | Polymer property prediction | Replaced **expensive laboratory assays** |
| Genetic Algorithms | Heat exchanger network synthesis | Energy savings of **5–15%** in refineries |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fuzzy Logic Controller for a CSTR Temperature

**Problem Statement:** Design a fuzzy logic controller (FLC) to regulate the temperature of a continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR) where the input variables are **Temperature Error (e)** and **Change in Error (Δe)**, and the output is the **Coolant Flow Rate Adjustment (Δq)**.

**Step 1: Define Linguistic Variables and Universes of Discourse**

$$
e = T_{setpoint} - T_{measured}
$$

$$
\Delta e = e(t) - e(t-1)
$$

Universe ranges (typical CSTR):
- $e \in [-50, +50]$ °C
- $\Delta e \in [-10, +10]$ °C/min
- $\Delta q \in [-100, +100]$ L/min

**Step 2: Define Fuzzy Sets (Membership Functions)**

For each variable, define **5 fuzzy sets**: Negative Large (NL), Negative Small (NS), Zero (Z), Positive Small (PS), Positive Large (PL).

For the **temperature error e**, using triangular membership functions centered at the characteristic points:

$$
\mu_{NS}(e) = \begin{cases} 1, & e \leq -25 \\ \frac{-e}{25}, & -25 < e < 0 \\ 0, & e \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mu_{Z}(e) = \begin{cases} \frac{e + 25}{25}, & -25 < e \leq 0 \\ \frac{25 - e}{25}, & 0 < e \leq 25 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mu_{PS}(e) = \begin{cases} 0, & e \leq 0 \\ \frac{e}{25}, & 0 < e < 25 \\ 1, & e \geq 25 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Construct the Knowledge Base (Rule Base)**

A representative subset of the **25-rule matrix** for an FLC:

| e \ Δe | NL | NS | Z | PS | PL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NL** | NL | NL | NS | NS | Z |
| **NS** | NL | NS | NS | Z | PS |
| **Z**  | NS | NS | Z  | PS | PS |
| **PS** | NS | Z  | PS | PS | PL |
| **PL** | Z  | PS | PS | PL | PL |

**Step 4: Fuzzification of Crisp Input**

Suppose measured values are:
- $e = +15$ °C (temperature is 15°C below setpoint)
- $\Delta e = +5$ °C/min (error is increasing in magnitude)

Compute memberships:

$$
\mu_{Z}(e=15) = \frac{25 - 15}{25} = 0.4
$$

$$
\mu_{PS}(e=15) = \frac{15}{25} = 0.6
$$

$$
\mu_{PS}(\Delta e=5) = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0 \quad \text{(at the boundary)}
$$

$$
\mu_{PL}(\Delta e=5) = 0.0
$$

**Step 5: Apply Rules and Compute Firing Strengths (using min operator)**

- **Rule (Z, PS):** $\alpha_1 = \min(0.4, 1.0) = 0.4$ → Output: PS
- **Rule (PS, PS):** $\alpha_2 = \min(0.6, 1.0) = 0.6$ → Output: PS

**Step 6: Defuzzification (Centroid Method)**

For simplicity, assume symmetric triangular output membership functions. The defuzzified coolant flow adjustment is:

$$
\Delta q^* = \frac{\int_{-100}^{100} \Delta q \cdot \mu_{out}(\Delta q) \, d(\Delta q)}{\int_{-100}^{100} \mu_{out}(\Delta q) \, d(\Delta q)}
$$

For two clipped triangular output sets both yielding "PS" (centered at +50 L/min) with clipping levels 0.4 and 0.6, the equivalent single representative output is approximated as:

$$
\Delta q^* \approx \frac{(0.4)(50) + (0.6)(50)}{0.4 + 0.6} = 50 \text{ L/min}
$$

Thus, the controller commands an **increase in coolant flow of 50 L/min** to drive the temperature back to setpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In board exams, students often skip **Steps 4 and 5 (fuzzification and rule firing)**. Marks are explicitly allocated for these intermediate calculations. Always show the membership values numerically.

### 3.2 Derivation: Back-Propagation Learning for a Single Neuron (Predicting Distillate Composition)

**Problem Statement:** A single neuron is trained to predict the **distillate mole fraction $x_D$** of ethanol in a binary distillation column from a single input: the **reflux ratio R**.

**Step 1: Architecture and Forward Pass**

Neuron computes:

$$
z = w \cdot R + b
$$

$$
\hat{x}_D = f(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

**Step 2: Define the Loss Function (MSE for a single sample)**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}(x_D^{true} - \hat{x}_D)^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Gradient of Loss with Respect to Weight $w$**

Using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{x}_D} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_D}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w}
$$

Term-by-term evaluation:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{x}_D} = -(x_D^{true} - \hat{x}_D)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{x}_D}{\partial z} = f(z)(1 - f(z)) = \hat{x}_D(1 - \hat{x}_D)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w} = R
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w} = -(x_D^{true} - \hat{x}_D) \cdot \hat{x}_D(1 - \hat{x}_D) \cdot R
$$

**Step 4: Apply Weight Update Rule**

$$
w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}
$$

Substituting:

$$
w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} + \eta \cdot (x_D^{true} - \hat{x}_D) \cdot \hat{x}_D(1 - \hat{x}_D) \cdot R
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The factor $\hat{x}_D(1 - \hat{x}_D)$ is the **sigmoid derivative**. It is **maximum (0.25) at $\hat{x}_D = 0.5$** and **approaches zero at the extremes**. This explains why early ANNs struggled with extrapolation beyond the trained range — a recurring challenge in early chemical engineering applications.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Technique | Year of Peak Adoption | Strongest Application | Major Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expert Systems** | 1985–1995 | Fault diagnosis, alarm management | Knowledge acquisition bottleneck |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | 1988–2000 | Process control (pH, temperature) | Rule explosion with many variables |
| **Early ANNs (MLP)** | 1990–2000 | Soft sensing, kinetic modeling | Local minima, small data requirements |
| **Genetic Algorithms** | 1993–2005 | Process optimization | Computationally expensive |

---


**Description of Flow:**
- The **Process Operator** interacts with the **User Interface** to query the system.
- The **Inference Engine** activates rules from the **Knowledge Base** based on the current facts in the **Working Memory**.
- The **Working Memory** is continuously updated with real-time **Process Database** values (sensor readings).
- The **Explanation Facility** logs the reasoning chain for the operator's understanding.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Define an **Expert System** and list its **four main components**. Briefly explain its role in chemical process fault diagnosis.

**Model Answer:**

An **Expert System (ES)** is a computer program that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert in a specific, narrow domain by encoding domain knowledge as a structured set of rules and applying logical inference to reach conclusions.

**Four Main Components:**
1. **Knowledge Base** — Contains domain-specific IF–THEN rules acquired from human experts.
2. **Inference Engine** — The reasoning mechanism (forward or backward chaining) that applies rules to known facts.
3. **User Interface** — Allows the operator/engineer to interact with the system using natural language or menus.
4. **Explanation Facility** — Provides transparency by tracing the reasoning chain (WHY and HOW explanations).

**Role in Fault Diagnosis:** In a chemical plant, an expert system continuously monitors sensor data (temperature, pressure, flow) and matches abnormal patterns against its rule base to identify the most probable root cause of a process upset, providing actionable recommendations to the operator within seconds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question]** What is a **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)**? Why is it preferred over conventional PID controllers in certain chemical engineering applications?

**Model Answer:**

A **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)** is a rule-based control system that uses fuzzy set theory and linguistic variables (e.g., "low", "medium", "high") to compute a control action, even when the underlying process model is imprecise or nonlinear.

**Advantages over conventional PID controllers:**

1. **No mathematical model required** — Useful for highly nonlinear processes (e.g., pH neutralization, polymerization) where deriving a first-principles model is impractical.
2. **Robustness to sensor noise and parameter uncertainty** — Linguistic rules generalize better across operating conditions.
3. **Captures operator heuristics** — Encodes decades of experiential knowledge that PID cannot incorporate.
4. **Faster commissioning** — Rules can be tuned by trial and error or expert input without rigorous system identification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse **fuzzy logic with probability**. Fuzzy logic handles **linguistic imprecision** (how "hot" something is), while probability handles **stochastic uncertainty** (how likely an event is). These are mathematically and philosophically distinct concepts. Marks are deducted if this distinction is not articulated.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Question]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a chemical engineering expert system**. Describe the role of the **inference engine** in detail. Discuss the **forward chaining** and **backward chaining** mechanisms with a suitable chemical engineering example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **fuzzy logic**? Define **membership function** and **linguistic variable**. For a CSTR temperature control problem, the error $e$ has three fuzzy sets: **Low (L)**, **Medium (M)**, and **High (H)** with triangular membership functions:

$$
\mu_L(e) = \begin{cases} 1, & e \leq 30 \\ \frac{50-e}{20}, & 30 < e < 50 \\ 0, & e \geq 50 \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mu_M(e) = \begin{cases} 0, & e \leq 30 \\ \frac{e-30}{20}, & 30 < e \leq 50 \\ \frac{70-e}{20}, & 50 < e \leq 70 \\ 0, & e > 70 \end{cases}
$$

$$
\mu_H(e) = \begin{cases} 0, & e \leq 50 \\ \frac{e-50}{20}, & 50 < e < 70 \\ 1, & e \geq 70 \end{cases}
$$

Calculate the membership values for $e = 45$ °C and explain the physical meaning of the result in terms of the reactor state. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Expert System (Architecture):**

The expert system consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **User Interface** → Communication channel with the operator.
2. **Inference Engine** → Reasoning core (forward/backward chaining).
3. **Knowledge Base** → Repository of IF–THEN rules.
4. **Working Memory** → Stores dynamic facts (current sensor data).
5. **Explanation Facility** → Justifies the conclusion reached.
6. **Knowledge Acquisition Module** → Interface for adding new rules.

**Role of the Inference Engine:**
The inference engine is the **"brain"** of the expert system. It examines the facts in the working memory, identifies which rules in the knowledge base are triggered (i.e., whose antecedents are satisfied), and executes their consequents. It resolves conflicts when multiple rules fire simultaneously using **conflict resolution strategies** (e.g., recency, specificity, priority).

**Forward Chaining:**
- A **data-driven** approach.
- Starts with known facts and applies rules to derive new facts until a goal is reached.
- **Chemical Engineering Example:** In an **alarm analysis system**, the inference engine starts with the fact "Reactor Pressure = 12 bar" (high). It applies the rule: IF Pressure > 10 bar AND Temperature < 350 K THEN "Possible Condenser Failure". It then activates subsequent rules to recommend operator actions.

**Backward Chaining:**
- A **goal-driven** approach.
- Starts with a hypothesis (e.g., "Is the heat exchanger fouled?") and works backward to verify the conditions.
- **Chemical Engineering Example:** In **root cause analysis**, the system hypothesizes "Heat Exchanger Fouling" and checks if the supporting evidence (reduced heat transfer coefficient, increased pressure drop, outlet temperature deviation) is present in the working memory.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Fuzzy Logic Definition:** Fuzzy logic is a multivalued logic system in which the truth value of a proposition ranges continuously between 0 and 1, allowing reasoning under imprecision and partial truths.

**Membership Function:** A function $\mu_A: X \to [0, 1]$ that assigns to each element $x \in X$ a degree of membership in fuzzy set $A$.

**Linguistic Variable:** A variable whose values are words or sentences in a natural or artificial language (e.g., "Temperature is High").

**Numerical Evaluation at $e = 45$ °C:**

For **Low (L):**

$$
\mu_L(45) = \frac{50 - 45}{20} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25
$$

For **Medium (M):**

$$
\mu_M(45) = \frac{45 - 30}{20} = \frac{15}{20} = 0.75
$$

For **High (H):**

$$
\mu_H(45) = 0 \quad \text{(since } 45 < 50\text{)}
$$

**Verification of Sum of Memberships:**

$$
\mu_L + \mu_M + \mu_H = 0.25 + 0.75 + 0 = 1.00
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
The reactor temperature error of 45°C belongs **25% to "Low"** and **75% to "Medium"** but **not at all to "High"**. This means the reactor is operating **closer to the medium error regime** than to a low error state. The controller should therefore apply a control action weighted toward rules that fire for the "Medium" condition, with a smaller contribution from "Low" rules.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Question]*

**(a)** Explain the **basic architecture of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN)**. Discuss the **back-propagation algorithm** with reference to its application in **predicting distillation column performance**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Genetic Algorithm (GA)**? Outline its main steps. Explain how a GA can be used to **optimize the operating conditions (temperature, pressure, residence time) of a chemical reactor** to maximize product yield. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**ANN Architecture:**
An Artificial Neural Network consists of:
1. **Input Layer** — Receives process variables (e.g., reflux ratio, feed composition, reboiler duty).
2. **Hidden Layer(s)** — Performs nonlinear transformations using weighted sums and activation functions.
3. **Output Layer** — Produces the predicted variable (e.g., distillate composition, bottoms purity).
4. **Weights and Biases** — The learnable parameters.

**Back-Propagation Algorithm for Distillation Column Prediction:**

1. **Initialization:** Assign small random weights to all connections in the network.
2. **Forward Pass:** Input a training sample (e.g., $R = 3.5$, $x_F = 0.45$, $q_{reboiler} = 1200$ kW) and compute the predicted distillate composition $\hat{x}_D$.
3. **Error Computation:** Compare the predicted output with the measured output: $E = \frac{1}{2}(x_D^{true} - \hat{x}_D)^2$.
4. **Backward Pass:** Compute the gradient of the error with respect to each weight using the chain rule (as derived in Section 3.2).
5. **Weight Update:** Adjust weights using $w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$, where $\eta$ is the learning rate (typically 0.01–0.1).
6. **Iteration:** Repeat for all training samples over multiple epochs until the error converges below a threshold (e.g., MSE < 0.001).

**Application Outcome:** Once trained, the ANN can predict $x_D$ in real-time from operating conditions, enabling **inferential control** of the distillation column without waiting for laboratory analysis.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Genetic Algorithm (GA):** A population-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by **natural selection and genetics**.

**Main Steps of a GA:**

1. **Initialization:** Generate a random population of candidate solutions (chromosomes), each representing a set of operating conditions (e.g., $T = 623$ K, $P = 25$ bar, $\tau = 4$ hr).
2. **Fitness Evaluation:** Compute the fitness of each chromosome using the objective function (e.g., product yield).
3. **Selection:** Choose parent chromosomes for reproduction using methods such as **roulette wheel selection** or **tournament selection**.
4. **Crossover:** Combine pairs of parents to produce offspring (new candidate solutions) using operators such as single-point or uniform crossover.
5. **Mutation:** Randomly alter a small fraction of genes in the offspring to maintain genetic diversity.
6. **Replacement:** Form a new generation by replacing less-fit individuals.
7. **Termination:** Stop when a maximum number of generations is reached or the fitness improvement plateaus.

**Application to Reactor Optimization:**

- **Decision Variables:** $\mathbf{x} = [T, P, \tau]^T$
- **Bounds:** $T \in [573, 723]$ K, $P \in [10, 50]$ bar, $\tau \in [1, 8]$ hr
- **Objective (Fitness):** $F(\mathbf{x}) = Y_{product}(T, P, \tau)$ where $Y$ is the experimental or simulated yield.
- **Result:** The GA converges to an optimal set of operating conditions (e.g., $T^* = 680$ K, $P^* = 30$ bar, $\tau^* = 3.5$ hr) that maximizes yield, often outperforming gradient-based methods when the response surface is multimodal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **No diagrams = Loss of 2–3 marks** in Part B questions. Always include a **neatly labeled block diagram** for expert systems, ANN, and FLC.
> 2. **Skipping numerical calculations** in fuzzy logic or ANN problems leads to **zero marks** for the sub-question. Intermediate steps (membership values, fuzzification, defuzzification) must be shown explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing forward and backward chaining** is a frequent error. Memorize the keywords: Forward = **data-driven**; Backward = **goal-driven**.
> 4. **Not defining notation** (e.g., what $w$, $\eta$, $E$ represent) is penalized. Always include a **notation block** at the start of long answers.
> 5. **Listing without explaining** — In KTU exams, merely listing components of an expert system (without explaining their functions) earns partial credit at best. Each component must be **described in 1–2 lines**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four pillars of early AI in chemical engineering:** Expert Systems, Fuzzy Logic, Artificial Neural Networks, Genetic Algorithms.
- **Expert systems** excelled in **fault diagnosis and alarm management** but suffered from the **knowledge acquisition bottleneck**.
- **Fuzzy logic** is ideal for **highly nonlinear processes** (pH control, distillation) where a first-principles model is unavailable.
- **Early ANNs (MLP with back-propagation)** were the precursors of modern deep learning and were used for **soft sensing** of unmeasurable variables.
- **Genetic algorithms** are global optimizers that do not require gradients — useful for **multimodal, nonlinear objective functions** in reactor and process optimization.
- **Membership functions** map crisp values to fuzzy sets in $[0, 1]$ — they are **not** probability distributions.
- **Centroid defuzzification** is the most commonly used method to convert fuzzy outputs to crisp control actions.
- **Back-propagation** uses the **chain rule** of calculus to propagate errors backward from output to input layers.
- **Hybrid systems** (e.g., fuzzy-ANN, expert-fuzzy) emerged in the late 1990s to overcome the limitations of individual techniques.
- **Historical milestones** to remember: Zadeh (fuzzy logic, 1965) → DENDRAL (1972) → CONPHYDE (1982) → Back-Propagation (1986) → Industrial fuzzy controllers (1988).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Step-by-Step Explanation → Numerical Example → Real-World Application**. This template typically fetches **12+ out of 14 marks** consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# The FALCON Framework

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FALCON (Framework for Adaptive Learning and Control of prOcess Networks)** is a hybrid, data-driven AI-ML architecture designed specifically for chemical engineering systems. It integrates **supervised learning**, **unsupervised learning**, and **reinforcement learning** layers into a unified pipeline for process monitoring, fault diagnosis, soft-sensor development, and optimal control.

The framework was conceptualized to bridge the gap between classical statistical process control (SPC) and modern deep-learning paradigms. FALCON operates on the principle of **multi-modal feature fusion**, where time-series process data (temperatures, pressures, flow rates, compositions) are ingested, transformed, and passed through a hierarchy of intelligent layers to produce actionable engineering outputs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Chemical Plant as a Human Body:**
> Imagine a chemical plant is a human body. **Sensors** are the nerves carrying signals (temperature, pressure). **FALCON** acts like the **brain + nervous system combined**. Just as a brain interprets nerve signals to detect pain (anomaly), coordinate movement (control), and remember patterns (learning), FALCON interprets sensor data to detect faults, adjust control valves, and remember optimal operating conditions. The **five layers of FALCON** correspond to: perception → memory → reasoning → decision → action.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **Detection Latency:** Time between fault occurrence and detection (typical target **< 5 minutes**).
- **False Alarm Rate (FAR):** Threshold **< 2%** for industrial deployment.
- **R² Score:** For soft-sensor regression tasks, target **> 0.95**.
- **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):** For predictive models, target **< 3%**.

### 1.4 The Five Pillars of FALCON

| Pillar | Function | ML Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Acquisition Layer** | Sensor ingestion, denoising | Wavelet transform, Kalman filter |
| **Feature Extraction Layer** | Dimensionality reduction | PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders |
| **Modeling Layer** | Pattern recognition | ANN, SVM, Random Forest |
| **Decision Layer** | Classification & diagnosis | Fuzzy logic, Bayesian networks |
| **Action Layer** | Control actuation | RL agents, MPC integration |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown

The FALCON framework operates through **five sequential stages**, each with rigorous mathematical underpinnings:

### Stage 1: Data Preprocessing
- **Why:** Industrial sensor data is corrupted by noise, drift, and missing values.
- **How:** Apply exponential moving average (EMA) filtering and Z-score normalization:

$$z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_i}{\sigma_i}$$

where $z_i$ is the normalized value, $\mu_i$ is the mean, and $\sigma_i$ is the standard deviation of the $i$-th feature.

### Stage 2: Feature Extraction via PCA
- **Why:** High-dimensional process data contains redundant information.
- **How:** Project data onto principal components that maximize variance:

$$\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ is the data matrix, $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ is the loading matrix, and $\mathbf{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ is the score matrix with $k < m$.

### Stage 3: Supervised Modeling
- **Why:** Establish input-output mappings for prediction.
- **How:** Train a feedforward neural network with backpropagation:

$$y_{\text{pred}} = f\left(\sum_{j=1}^{h} w_j^{(2)} \cdot g\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j\right) + b^{(2)}\right)$$

### Stage 4: Anomaly Detection via Hotelling's T²
- **Why:** Detect deviations from normal operating conditions.
- **How:** Compute the statistical distance in PCA-reduced space:

$$T^2 = \mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{S}^{-1} \mathbf{t}$$

where $\mathbf{t}$ is the score vector and $\mathbf{S}$ is the covariance matrix of scores.

### Stage 5: Reinforcement Learning Control
- **Why:** Optimize long-term process performance.
- **How:** Train an agent via the Bellman equation:

$$Q(s,a) = r(s,a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU ESE questions.**

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z-score Normalization | $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Data preprocessing | Dimensionless |
| PCA Variance Ratio | $\eta_k = \lambda_k / \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i$ | Component selection | Dimensionless |
| Hotelling's T² | $T^2 = \mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{S}^{-1} \mathbf{t}$ | Fault detection | Dimensionless |
| SPE / Q-statistic | $Q = \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e}$ | Residual monitoring | Dimensionless |
| MSE Loss | $L = \frac{1}{n}\sum (y - \hat{y})^2$ | Model training | (output)² |
| Bellman Equation | $Q(s,a) = r + \gamma \max Q(s',a')$ | RL control | Reward units |
| Activation (ReLU) | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Hidden layers | Dimensionless |
| Sigmoid Output | $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ | Classification | Probability |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Distillation Column Monitoring:** Real-time detection of tray fouling using FALCON's anomaly detection layer.
- **Reactor Yield Optimization:** Soft sensors predicting product composition from temperature/pressure readings.
- **Polymerization Batch Control:** RL agent adjusts initiator feed rate to maintain target molecular weight.
- **Energy Management:** FALCON optimizes heat exchanger network operations to minimize utility costs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hotelling's T² Statistic for Fault Detection

### Problem Setup
Given a training dataset $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ representing $n$ observations of $m$ process variables, derive the FALCON fault detection statistic.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Center the Data**
Remove the mean vector $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ from each observation to obtain the centered matrix $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{1}_n \bar{\mathbf{x}}^T$$

where $\mathbf{1}_n$ is an $n \times 1$ vector of ones, and $\bar{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ is the column-wise mean.

**Step 2: Compute the Covariance Matrix**
The sample covariance matrix $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ is given by:

$$\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n-1} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$$

**Step 3: Eigenvalue Decomposition**
Solve the characteristic equation to obtain eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_m$ and eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_m$:

$$\mathbf{S} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$$

**Step 4: Construct Loading Matrix**
Retain the top $k$ eigenvectors (typically $k$ chosen such that cumulative variance ratio $\geq 0.95$):

$$\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1 \vert \mathbf{v}_2 \vert \cdots \vert \mathbf{v}_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$$

**Step 5: Project to Score Space**
For a new observation $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$, compute the score vector:

$$\mathbf{t}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{W}^T (\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \bar{\mathbf{x}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times 1}$$

**Step 6: Compute T² Statistic**
The Hotelling's T² statistic measures Mahalanobis distance in the reduced score space:

$$T^2 = \mathbf{t}_{\text{new}}^T \mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1} \mathbf{t}_{\text{new}}$$

$$\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_k)$$

**Step 7: Control Limit Calculation**
For a significance level $\alpha$ (typically 0.99), the upper control limit (UCL) under F-distribution is:

$$T^2_{\alpha} = \frac{k(n^2 - 1)}{n(n-k)} F_{\alpha, k, n-k}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If $T^2 > T^2_{\alpha}$ → **Fault Detected** 🚨
- If $T^2 \leq T^2_{\alpha}$ → **Normal Operation** ✅

## 3.2 Derivation: PCA-based Soft Sensor for Composition Prediction

**Given:** Input matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ (temperatures, pressures, flows) and target vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}$ (product composition).

**Step 1:** Compute PCA scores $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}$ (from above derivation).

**Step 2:** Fit a regression model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{T} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ using Ordinary Least Squares:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{T})^{-1} \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3:** The predicted composition for a new sample:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{t}_{\text{new}}^T \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$$

**Step 4:** Coefficient of determination $R^2$:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FALCON Architecture Flow Diagram

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the FALCON framework. List its five operational layers.**

**Model Answer:**
FALCON (Framework for Adaptive Learning and Control of prOcess Networks) is a hybrid AI-ML architecture designed for chemical engineering applications, integrating data acquisition, feature extraction, modeling, decision-making, and control actuation in a unified pipeline. The five layers are:
1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — sensor data ingestion
2. **Preprocessing Layer** — denoising and normalization
3. **Feature Extraction Layer** — PCA/Autoencoder
4. **Modeling Layer** — supervised/unsupervised learning
5. **Decision and Action Layer** — fault diagnosis and control actuation

**[3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for layers]**

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the Hotelling's T² statistic formula and explain its role in FALCON.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hotelling's T² statistic is given by $T^2 = \mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1} \mathbf{t}$, where $\mathbf{t}$ is the PCA score vector and $\mathbf{\Lambda}$ is the diagonal matrix of retained eigenvalues. In FALCON, it serves as the **primary fault detection metric**, measuring the Mahalanobis distance of a new observation from the normal operating region in the reduced-dimensional score space. When $T^2$ exceeds the control limit $T^2_\alpha = \frac{k(n^2-1)}{n(n-k)} F_{\alpha,k,n-k}$, a fault is flagged.

**[3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for explanation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often lose marks by: (a) writing formulas without stating assumptions, (b) skipping the control-limit derivation, (c) confusing Hotelling's T² with Mahalanobis distance without proper context, and (d) forgetting to justify the choice of $k$ principal components. Always show step-by-step derivation and cite the **physical meaning** of each metric.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Hotelling's T² statistic for fault detection in the FALCON framework, starting from the centered data matrix. Clearly show the control limit calculation using the F-distribution. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CSTR produces a target product concentration of **85 mol/L** with acceptable deviation of **±2 mol/L**. A soft sensor based on PCA-regression has been trained on $n = 200$ observations with $m = 8$ process variables and $k = 4$ retained principal components. For a new observation, the score vector is $\mathbf{t}_{\text{new}} = [2.1, -1.3, 0.8, 0.4]^T$ and the eigenvalue matrix is $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(4.5, 2.1, 1.2, 0.6)$. Compute the T² statistic and determine if a fault exists at $\alpha = 0.99$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** [Follow the 7-step derivation given in Section 3.1 above, covering centering → covariance → eigendecomposition → loading matrix → score projection → T² formula → control limit using F-distribution.]

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Step 1: Compute $\mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1} = \text{diag}(1/4.5, \ 1/2.1, \ 1/1.2, \ 1/0.6) = \text{diag}(0.222, \ 0.476, \ 0.833, \ 1.667)$$

Step 2: Compute $T^2 = \mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1} \mathbf{t}$:

$$T^2 = (2.1)^2(0.222) + (-1.3)^2(0.476) + (0.8)^2(0.833) + (0.4)^2(1.667)$$

$$T^2 = (4.41)(0.222) + (1.69)(0.476) + (0.64)(0.833) + (0.16)(1.667)$$

$$T^2 = 0.979 + 0.804 + 0.533 + 0.267 = 2.583$$

Step 3: Compute the control limit:

$$T^2_{\alpha} = \frac{k(n^2 - 1)}{n(n-k)} F_{\alpha, k, n-k}$$

$$T^2_{0.99} = \frac{4(200^2 - 1)}{200(200-4)} F_{0.99, 4, 196}$$

$$T^2_{0.99} = \frac{4 \times 39999}{200 \times 196} F_{0.99, 4, 196}$$

$$T^2_{0.99} = \frac{159996}{39200} \times 2.42 = 4.082 \times 2.42 \approx 9.88$$

Step 4: Decision: $T^2 = 2.583 < T^2_{0.99} = 9.88$ → **No fault detected. Process is operating normally.** ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the feature extraction layer of FALCON with the PCA algorithm. Show mathematically how dimensionality reduction is achieved and derive the cumulative variance ratio formula. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A distillation column has **15 process variables**. PCA is applied, and the following eigenvalues are obtained: $\lambda = [4.8, 3.2, 2.1, 1.4, 0.9, 0.6, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.15, 0.1, 0.08, 0.05, 0.03, 0.02]$. Determine the minimum number of principal components needed to capture **at least 95%** of the total variance. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCA Explanation:** [Discuss covariance matrix → eigenvalue decomposition → selection of top $k$ components → projection onto score space. Derive cumulative variance ratio $\eta_{\text{cum}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{m}\lambda_i$.]

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Step 1: Total variance:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{15} \lambda_i = 4.8+3.2+2.1+1.4+0.9+0.6+0.4+0.3+0.2+0.15+0.1+0.08+0.05+0.03+0.02 = 14.33$$

Step 2: Cumulative sums:

| $k$ | $\lambda_k$ | Cumulative | Ratio (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.80 | 4.80 | 33.50 |
| 2 | 3.20 | 8.00 | 55.83 |
| 3 | 2.10 | 10.10 | 70.48 |
| 4 | 1.40 | 11.50 | 80.25 |
| 5 | 0.90 | 12.40 | 86.53 |
| 6 | 0.60 | 13.00 | 90.72 |
| 7 | 0.40 | 13.40 | 93.51 |
| **8** | **0.30** | **13.70** | **95.60** |

Step 3: At $k = 8$, cumulative variance ratio = $13.70/14.33 = 95.60\% \geq 95\%$ ✅

**Answer: Minimum $k = 8$ principal components required.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ FALCON = **Framework for Adaptive Learning and Control of prOcess Networks** — five-layer hybrid AI-ML architecture.
- ✅ **Hotelling's T²** = $\mathbf{t}^T \mathbf{\Lambda}^{-1} \mathbf{t}$ — primary fault detection metric.
- ✅ **SPE (Q-statistic)** = $\mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e}$ — residual-based fault detection for nonlinear faults.
- ✅ **Control limit:** $T^2_\alpha = \frac{k(n^2-1)}{n(n-k)} F_{\alpha, k, n-k}$
- ✅ **Cumulative variance ratio:** $\eta_{\text{cum}}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i$; threshold typically **0.95**.
- ✅ **PCA steps:** Center → Covariance → Eigendecomposition → Loading matrix → Score projection.
- ✅ **Bellman equation** $Q(s,a) = r + \gamma \max Q(s',a')$ underpins the RL control layer.
- ✅ **Soft sensor** = PCA regression model replacing hardware composition analyzers.
- ✅ **Z-score normalization** essential before PCA to prevent scale bias.
- ✅ Industrial targets: **FAR < 2%**, **R² > 0.95**, **Detection Latency < 5 min**.
- ✅ FALCON integrates **supervised + unsupervised + reinforcement learning** — unique among process monitoring frameworks.
- ✅ Always justify component selection by **physical interpretability**, not just variance ratio alone.

---
