topics.title: CATDEX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CATDEX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of CATDEX

In the context of **PECHT866 (AI-ML Application in Chemical Engineering)**, **CATDEX** is the abbreviated form used to denote **CATEGORICAL DATA EXPLORATION** — a foundational, pre-modelling analytical framework that systematically characterizes, summarizes, visualises, and statistically tests **non-numeric (qualitative) variables** found in chemical engineering datasets before they are fed into any supervised or unsupervised Machine Learning pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **CATDEX (Categorical Data Exploration)** is the *first-pass qualitative audit* of every categorical column in a chemical process dataset (e.g., catalyst type, reactor class, feedstock grade, solvent family). It precedes any encoding, scaling, or model training step.

Mathematically, CATDEX can be viewed as a tuple:

$$
\text{CATDEX} \;=\; \bigl\{\, \mathcal{C},\; f_{\text{freq}},\; \chi^{2},\; V,\; H(\cdot),\; I(\cdot;\cdot) \,\bigr\}
$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of categorical variables, $f_{\text{freq}}$ is the frequency map, $\chi^2$ is the chi-square statistic, $V$ is **Cramér's V**, $H(\cdot)$ is the Shannon entropy, and $I(\cdot;\cdot)$ is the **Mutual Information** between two categorical attributes.

---

## 1.2 Why CATDEX Matters in Chemical Engineering

Chemical engineering datasets are rich in **categorical descriptors** that pure numerical ML pipelines (e.g., linear regression, SVR) cannot consume directly. Examples encountered in real plants and laboratories:

| Domain | Categorical Variable | Typical Distinct Levels |
|---|---|---|
| Catalysis | Catalyst type (Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu-Zn, Zeolite-Y) | 5–15 |
| Reaction Engineering | Reactor class (CSTR, PFR, Batch, Semibatch, Microreactor) | 4–6 |
| Separation | Distillation tray type (Sieve, Valve, Bubble-cap, Packing) | 4–8 |
| Feedstock | Crude source (Brent, WTI, Maya, Urals, Athabasca) | 5–10 |
| Process Control | Alarm severity (Low, Medium, High, Critical) | 4 |
| Materials | Polymer family (PE, PP, PVC, PET, Nylon-6) | 6–12 |

If these are **ignored, mis-encoded, or poorly explored**, the resulting ML model exhibits *systematic bias* and *poor generalisation* — a phenomenon called the **categorical-blindness error** in industrial AI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – CO1 Mapping:** CATDEX directly satisfies Course Outcome CO1: *"Preprocess and explore chemical engineering datasets for AI/ML readiness."* Expect direct questions on encoding, frequency tables, and chi-square testing.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Plant Engineer's Notebook

Imagine a senior plant engineer who has just received **six months of daily reactor log sheets** for a **fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit**. Before she writes a single equation, she opens the notebook and does the following *manually*:

1. She **tallies** how many days each *catalyst brand* was used (frequency table).
2. She **checks** whether the *feedstock origin* and *reactor pressure class* ever appear together (contingency table).
3. She **rates** the *association* between feedstock and product yield grade using intuition — strong, weak, or none (Cramér's V intuition).
4. She **notes** which categorical levels are extremely rare and may need to be *merged* (rare-level collapsing).
5. She **flags** highly correlated categorical pairs to avoid feeding redundant features to a neural net (multicollinearity audit).

**CATDEX is precisely this notebook — but automated, statistically rigorous, and reproducible.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think of CATDEX as the **"engineer's pre-flight checklist before take-off."** No pilot flies without it; no AI model in chemical engineering should fly without it either.

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology Used in CATDEX

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Variable** | Categories with no inherent order (e.g., catalyst type) |
| **Ordinal Variable** | Categories with a defined rank (e.g., alarm severity: Low < Medium < High) |
| **Binary Variable** | Exactly two levels (e.g., pass / fail, on-spec / off-spec) |
| **High-Cardinality Feature** | Categorical column with > 50 distinct levels (e.g., batch ID) |
| **Rare Level** | A category whose frequency falls below a threshold (commonly **5 %**) |
| **Cardinality** | The number of unique categories in a feature, denoted $\vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert$ |
| **Mode** | The most frequent category |

---

## 1.5 KTU-Relevant Notation Block

For the rest of the notes, we adopt the following consistent notation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\;\rightarrow\; \text{random categorical variable} \\
\mathcal{L}_X &\;=\; \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_K\} \;\rightarrow\; \text{set of } K \text{ distinct levels of } X \\
n &\;\rightarrow\; \text{total number of observations (sample size)} \\
n_{ij} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{observed count in cell } (i,j) \text{ of a contingency table} \\
E_{ij} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{expected count under the null hypothesis of independence} \\
f_k &\;\rightarrow\; \text{observed frequency of level } x_k
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CATDEX Pipeline — Six Logical Stages

A complete CATDEX audit consists of **six rigorously sequenced stages**:

1. **Level Enumeration** — Identify every distinct category $x_k$ and record cardinality $K = \vert \mathcal{L}_X \vert$.
2. **Frequency Profiling** — Compute absolute frequency $f_k$ and relative frequency $\hat{p}_k = f_k / n$.
3. **Rare-Level Detection** — Flag categories with $\hat{p}_k < 0.05$ (the **5 %** rule) for possible merging or re-grouping.
4. **Bivariate Association Testing** — For pairs of categorical variables, compute chi-square, Cramér's V, and Mutual Information.
5. **Encoding Recommendation** — Based on cardinality and association strength, recommend an encoding scheme (see §2.4).
6. **Visualisation Output** — Generate bar plots, mosaic plots, stacked bar charts, and heat-mapped contingency tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why six stages?** Skipping any one introduces a documented failure mode: e.g., skipping Stage 3 silently inflates dimensionality after one-hot encoding; skipping Stage 4 leaks redundant features into the model.

---

## 2.2 Frequency Distribution and Mode

For a categorical variable $X$ with $K$ levels observed over $n$ records:

$$
\hat{p}_k \;=\; \frac{f_k}{n}, \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, K
$$

The **mode** is the level with the highest frequency:

$$
\text{Mode}(X) \;=\; \arg\max_{x_k \in \mathcal{L}_X} f_k
$$

The **class-imbalance ratio (CIR)** is a chemical-engineering-specific diagnostic:

$$
\text{CIR} \;=\; \frac{\max_k \, f_k}{\min_k \, f_k}
$$

A CIR > **20** indicates severe imbalance and triggers stratified sampling.

---

## 2.3 Chi-Square Test of Independence

Given two categorical variables $X$ (with $r$ levels) and $Y$ (with $c$ levels), form the $r \times c$ contingency table. The chi-square statistic is:

$$
\chi^{2} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{\bigl(n_{ij} - E_{ij}\bigr)^{2}}{E_{ij}}
$$

where the **expected count** under the null hypothesis of independence is:

$$
E_{ij} \;=\; \frac{\bigl(\sum_{j} n_{ij}\bigr)\bigl(\sum_{i} n_{ij}\bigr)}{n} \;=\; \frac{n_{i\cdot}\; n_{\cdot j}}{n}
$$

Degrees of freedom:

$$
\text{df} \;=\; (r-1)(c-1)
$$

Decision rule: reject $H_0$ (independence) at significance level $\alpha$ if

$$
\chi^{2} \;>\; \chi^{2}_{\alpha,\,(r-1)(c-1)}
$$

---

## 2.4 Cramér's V — Normalised Association Strength

Chi-square grows with sample size, so we normalise using **Cramér's V**:

$$
V \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\chi^{2}}{n \,\cdot\, \min(r-1,\,c-1)}}
$$

$$
0 \;\le\; V \;\le\; 1
$$

| $V$ Range | Interpretation (Cohen's convention) |
|---|---|
| $[0.00, 0.10)$ | Negligible |
| $[0.10, 0.30)$ | Weak |
| $[0.30, 0.50)$ | Moderate |
| $[0.50, 1.00]$ | Strong |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use in CATDEX:** A $V > 0.7$ between two categorical features signals **redundancy** — drop one before training to combat multicollinearity.

---

## 2.5 Shannon Entropy of a Categorical Variable

For a discrete variable $X$ with estimated distribution $\hat{p}_k$:

$$
H(X) \;=\; -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{p}_k \,\log_{2} \hat{p}_k \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

The **maximum entropy** occurs at uniform distribution:

$$
H_{\max}(X) \;=\; \log_{2} K
$$

The **normalised entropy** (a purity indicator) is:

$$
\tilde{H}(X) \;=\; \frac{H(X)}{H_{\max}(X)} \;\in\; [0, 1]
$$

---

## 2.6 Mutual Information Between Two Categorical Variables

$$
I(X;Y) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \hat{p}(x_i,y_j) \,\log_{2}\!\left(\frac{\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)}{\hat{p}(x_i)\,\hat{p}(y_j)}\right) \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

Useful identities:

$$
I(X;Y) \;=\; H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) \;=\; H(X) - H(X \mid Y)
$$

---

## 2.7 Encoding Strategies — The CATDEX Output Layer

The final CATDEX recommendation depends on cardinality and the variable type:

| Encoding | Best Suited When | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **One-Hot** | Low cardinality ($K \le 10$), nominal | $K$ binary dummy columns |
| **Ordinal** | Ordinal $X$, order preserved | Integer map $1, 2, \ldots, K$ |
| **Label** | Tree-based models only | $0, 1, \ldots, K-1$ |
| **Target (Mean)** | High cardinality + supervised target | $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x_k]$ |
| **Frequency** | Anonymous, no target leakage | $f_k / n$ |
| **Binary / Hashing** | Very high cardinality ($K > 100$) | Bit-vector of length $b \ll K$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Linear / distance-based models (Linear Regression, KNN, SVM-RBF):** One-hot is mandatory — label encoding introduces a *false ordinal* bias.

---

## 2.8 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula | Use in CATDEX | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{p}_k = f_k / n$ | Relative frequency | $\sum_k \hat{p}_k = 1$ |
| 2 | $\text{CIR} = \max f_k / \min f_k$ | Imbalance diagnostic | CIR > 20 ⇒ stratify |
| 3 | $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot} n_{\cdot j} / n$ | Expected count | All $E_{ij} \ge 5$ |
| 4 | $\chi^{2} = \sum (n_{ij}-E_{ij})^{2}/E_{ij}$ | Independence test | df = $(r-1)(c-1)$ |
| 5 | $V = \sqrt{\chi^{2} / (n \min(r-1,c-1))}$ | Normalised association | $0 \le V \le 1$ |
| 6 | $H(X) = -\sum \hat{p}_k \log_2 \hat{p}_k$ | Information content | Units: bits |
| 7 | $\tilde{H}(X) = H(X) / \log_2 K$ | Purity indicator | $0 \le \tilde{H} \le 1$ |
| 8 | $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$ | Mutual information | $I \ge 0$; $I = 0$ iff independent |
| 9 | $\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1)$ | Critical-value lookup | Used with $\chi^2$ table |
| 10 | $\text{Mode}(X) = \arg\max f_k$ | Most common level | Single value (modal class) |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of CATDEX in Chemical Engineering

| Use Case | CATDEX Output Used | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst screening | $V(\text{Catalyst}, \text{Yield grade})$ | Drop redundant catalyst descriptors |
| Anomaly detection | $H(\text{Alarm code})$ | Low-entropy alarms → deterministic rules |
| Process digital twin | $\text{CIR}(\text{Operating mode})$ | Rebalance training data |
| Soft-sensor design | $I(\text{Feed type}, Y)$ | Feature selection for inferential model |
| Quality control | Rare-level flagging | Merge low-volume SKUs |

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Expected Count $E_{ij}$ Under Independence

**Statement.** Under the null hypothesis $H_0$: "$X$ and $Y$ are independent," the expected count in cell $(i,j)$ of an $r \times c$ contingency table equals the product of the marginal totals divided by the grand total.

**Derivation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Under } H_0, \;\; P(X = x_i,\; Y = y_j) \;=\; P(X=x_i)\,P(Y=y_j) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Estimate the marginal probabilities from the data:} \\
& \hat{P}(X = x_i) \;=\; \frac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}, \qquad \hat{P}(Y = y_j) \;=\; \frac{n_{\cdot j}}{n} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Combine via independence:} \\
& \hat{P}(X = x_i,\; Y = y_j) \;=\; \frac{n_{i\cdot}}{n}\cdot\frac{n_{\cdot j}}{n} \;=\; \frac{n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j}}{n^{2}} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Multiply by total sample size } n \text{ to obtain the expected count:} \\
& E_{ij} \;=\; n \cdot \hat{P}(X = x_i,\; Y = y_j) \;=\; \frac{n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j}}{n} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Conclude:} \quad \boxed{\,E_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j}}{n}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity requirement:** All $E_{ij} \ge 5$. If any expected count is below 5, combine adjacent levels (Yates / Fisher correction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Cramér's V from Pearson's Chi-Square

**Statement.** Show that $V$ as defined in §2.4 lies in $[0, 1]$.

**Derivation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Start with Pearson's statistic:} \\
& \chi^{2} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(n_{ij} - E_{ij})^{2}}{E_{ij}} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{The maximum possible value of } \chi^{2} \text{ for fixed } n,\, r,\, c \text{ is:} \\
& \chi^{2}_{\max} \;=\; n \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Define } V \text{ as the square root of } \chi^{2} \text{ normalised by its maximum:} \\
& V \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\chi^{2}}{\chi^{2}_{\max}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\chi^{2}}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1)}} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Bounds: } \chi^{2} \ge 0 \Rightarrow V \ge 0; \quad \chi^{2} \le \chi^{2}_{\max} \Rightarrow V \le 1 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \boxed{\,0 \;\le\; V \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^{2}}{n\,\min(r-1,\,c-1)}} \;\le\; 1\,} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & V = 0 \Leftrightarrow \text{perfect independence (all } n_{ij} = E_{ij}) \\
& V = 1 \Leftrightarrow \text{perfect association}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Mutual Information $I(X;Y)$ from Entropy

**Statement.** Prove the symmetric identity $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$.

**Derivation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & H(X,Y) \;=\; -\sum_{i,j} \hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2 \hat{p}(x_i,y_j) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & H(X) + H(Y) \;=\; -\sum_{i}\hat{p}(x_i)\log_2\hat{p}(x_i) \;-\; \sum_{j}\hat{p}(y_j)\log_2\hat{p}(y_j) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Using } \hat{p}(x_i) = \sum_j \hat{p}(x_i,y_j) \text{ and } \hat{p}(y_j) = \sum_i \hat{p}(x_i,y_j): \\
& H(X) + H(Y) \;=\; -\sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2\hat{p}(x_i) \;-\; \sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2\hat{p}(y_j) \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Subtract: } \\
& H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) \\
& = -\sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\bigl[\log_2\hat{p}(x_i)+\log_2\hat{p}(y_j)-\log_2\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\bigr] \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & = -\sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2\!\left(\frac{\hat{p}(x_i)\hat{p}(y_j)}{\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)}\right) \\
& = \sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2\!\left(\frac{\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)}{\hat{p}(x_i)\hat{p}(y_j)}\right) \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \boxed{\,I(X;Y) \;=\; \sum_{i,j}\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2\!\left(\frac{\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)}{\hat{p}(x_i)\hat{p}(y_j)}\right)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Chemical Engineering Dataset

A pilot plant collected 200 hours of FCC operation across **two feedstocks (Light, Heavy)** and **three catalyst generations (G1, G2, G3)**. The contingency table is:

|  | **G1** | **G2** | **G3** | **Row Total $n_{i\cdot}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light** | 30 | 25 | 15 | 70 |
| **Heavy** | 20 | 45 | 65 | 130 |
| **Column Total $n_{\cdot j}$** | 50 | 70 | 80 | **n = 200** |

### 3.4.1 Compute Expected Counts

Using $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j} / n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= (70)(50)/200 = 17.50 \\
E_{12} &= (70)(70)/200 = 24.50 \\
E_{13} &= (70)(80)/200 = 28.00 \\
E_{21} &= (130)(50)/200 = 32.50 \\
E_{22} &= (130)(70)/200 = 45.50 \\
E_{23} &= (130)(80)/200 = 52.00
\end{aligned}
$$

All $E_{ij} \ge 5$ ✔ — chi-square approximation valid.

### 3.4.2 Compute $\chi^{2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^{2} &=\sum_{i,j}\frac{(n_{ij}-E_{ij})^{2}}{E_{ij}} \\
&=\frac{(30-17.5)^{2}}{17.5}+\frac{(25-24.5)^{2}}{24.5}+\frac{(15-28)^{2}}{28} \\
&\quad+\frac{(20-32.5)^{2}}{32.5}+\frac{(45-45.5)^{2}}{45.5}+\frac{(65-52)^{2}}{52} \\
&=\frac{156.25}{17.5}+\frac{0.25}{24.5}+\frac{169}{28}+\frac{156.25}{32.5}+\frac{0.25}{45.5}+\frac{169}{52} \\
&= 8.929 + 0.010 + 6.036 + 4.808 + 0.005 + 3.250 \\
\chi^{2} &\approx 23.04
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 Compute Cramér's V

Degrees of freedom: $(r-1)(c-1) = (2-1)(3-1) = 2$, $\min(r-1,c-1) = 1$.

$$
V \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\chi^{2}}{n\,\min(r-1,c-1)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{23.04}{200 \cdot 1}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.1152} \;\approx\; 0.339
$$

**Interpretation:** Moderate association (per Cohen's table in §2.4) — feedstock type and catalyst generation are **not independent** in this dataset.

### 3.4.4 Hypothesis Test at $\alpha = 0.05$

Critical value: $\chi^{2}_{0.05,2} = 5.991$.

Since $23.04 > 5.991$, reject $H_0$. Feedstock and catalyst generation are **statistically associated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering consequence:** A ML model predicting FCC yield from these two features should **not** encode them independently — interaction terms or target encoding must be considered.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — CATDEX Decision Logic

| Diagnostic Output | Numerical Threshold | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| $K = \vert \mathcal{L}_X \vert > 50$ | High cardinality | Hashing / target encoding |
| $\hat{p}_k < 0.05$ | Rare level | Merge with semantically similar level |
| CIR > 20 | Severe imbalance | Stratified sampling / SMOTE on target |
| $\chi^{2} > \chi^{2}_{\alpha,\text{df}}$ | Independence rejected | Keep both features, consider interaction |
| $V > 0.7$ | Strong redundancy | Drop one feature |
| $I(X;Y) > 0.5 \cdot H(X)$ | High mutual info | Combine into a single index |
| $\tilde{H}(X) < 0.3$ | Low diversity | Re-classify or remove |

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CATDEX Workflow — End-to-End Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the full CATDEX pipeline from raw categorical data to ML-ready encoded output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Chemical Engineering Dataset] --> B[Stage 1: Level Enumeration]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Frequency Profiling]
    C --> D{Rare Level Present?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Stage 3a: Merge or Re-bin Levels]
    D -- No  --> F[Stage 4: Bivariate Association]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Chi-Square]
    G --> H[Compute Cramer V]
    H --> I[Compute Mutual Information]
    I --> J{V > 0.7?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Drop Redundant Feature]
    J -- No  --> L[Stage 5: Encoding Selection]
    K --> L
    L --> M{K <= 10?}
    M -- Yes --> N[One-Hot Encoding]
    M -- No  --> O{Tree-Based Model?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Label Encoding]
    O -- No  --> Q[Target or Hashing Encoding]
    N --> R[Stage 6: Visualisation Output]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[ML-Ready Encoded Dataset]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Each rectangular node is an operational stage.
- Diamond nodes are conditional decision gates.
- The flow terminates at the **ML-Ready Encoded Dataset** node, which is the direct input to any scikit-learn / TensorFlow regressor or classifier.

---

## 4.2 CATDEX Data-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INGEST[Data Ingestion Layer]
        A1[CSV from DCS Historian]
        A2[LIMS Records]
        A3[Manual Lab Logs]
    end

    subgraph AUDIT[CATDEX Audit Layer]
        B1[Frequency Table Builder]
        B2[Contingency Table Builder]
        B3[Statistical Test Engine]
        B4[Encoding Recommender]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Encoded Feature Matrix X]
        C2[Diagnostic Report PDF]
        C3[Encoding Map JSON]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
```

**Functional Notes:**
- The **Audit Layer** is the heart of CATDEX — it is stateless and re-runnable.
- The **Output Layer** produces both machine-readable artefacts (`C1`, `C3`) and a human-readable audit report (`C2`), essential for KTU practical-record submissions.

---

## 4.3 Bivariate CATDEX Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Two Categorical Variables X and Y] --> Q1{Both Nominal?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> TestChi[Apply Chi-Square Test]
    Q1 -- No  --> TestSpearman[Apply Spearman Rank]
    TestChi --> Q2{All Eij >= 5?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ComputeV[Compute Cramers V]
    Q2 -- No  --> Fisher[Use Fishers Exact Test]
    ComputeV --> Classify{V Value}
    Classify -- Less than 0.10 --> Neg[Negligible: Drop one]
    Classify -- 0.10 to 0.30 --> Weak[Weak: Keep both]
    Classify -- 0.30 to 0.50 --> Mod[Moderate: Consider interaction]
    Classify -- Above 0.50 --> Strong[Strong: Combine or drop]
    TestSpearman --> Classify
    Fisher --> Classify
```

---

## 4.4 Encoding Selection Sub-Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Categorical Column X] --> Card{Cardinality K}
    Card -- K le 10 --> Nom{Nominal?}
    Card -- 10 K le 50 --> Mid[Frequency or Target Encoding]
    Card -- K gt 50 --> High[Hashing or Binary Encoding]
    Nom -- Yes --> OneHot[One-Hot]
    Nom -- No --> Ord[Ordinal Map]
```

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
**Define CATDEX. List any four (4) categorical variables typically found in a chemical engineering dataset.**

**Model Answer:**
CATDEX stands for **CATEGORICAL DATA EXPLORATION**. It is the systematic pre-modelling analysis of qualitative (non-numeric) variables in a dataset — encompassing frequency profiling, rare-level detection, bivariate association testing, and encoding recommendation.

Four categorical variables in a chemical engineering dataset:
1. **Catalyst type** (e.g., Pt, Pd, Ni, Zeolite-Y)
2. **Reactor class** (CSTR, PFR, Batch, Microreactor)
3. **Feedstock grade** (Light, Medium, Heavy sour)
4. **Alarm severity** (Low, Medium, High, Critical)

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for any four valid variables)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**State the formula for Cramér's V. What is its range, and what does a value of $V = 0.85$ between *catalyst brand* and *reactor type* imply for ML feature selection?**

**Model Answer:**

$$
V \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\chi^{2}}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1)}}
$$

Range: $0 \le V \le 1$.

A value of $V = 0.85$ indicates **strong association** (above the 0.50 threshold). For ML feature selection, one of the two variables should be **dropped** to combat multicollinearity, or they should be combined into a single engineered feature (e.g., catalyst-reactor interaction term).

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark range + 1 mark interpretation)*
