## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**, exactly mirroring the KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **complete CATDEX workflow** for a chemical engineering dataset containing categorical descriptors. Mention the six operational stages and the diagnostic produced at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A methanol synthesis reactor dataset contains 300 observations cross-tabulated by **catalyst type** (Cu-Zn, Cu-Zn-Cr, Cu-Zn-Al) and **reactor class** (Lurgi, ICI, Mitsubishi). The contingency table is:

| | Lurgi | ICI | Mitsubishi | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cu-Zn | 60 | 40 | 20 | 120 |
| Cu-Zn-Cr | 30 | 50 | 30 | 110 |
| Cu-Zn-Al | 10 | 30 | 30 | 70 |
| **Total** | 100 | 120 | 80 | **300** |

Compute the **chi-square statistic**, the **degrees of freedom**, and **Cramér's V**. Test the null hypothesis of independence at $\alpha = 0.05$ ($\chi^{2}_{0.05,4} = 9.488$). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

Refer to the **CATDEX Workflow diagram in §4.1**. The six stages are:

| Stage | Diagnostic Produced |
|---|---|
| 1. Level Enumeration | Cardinality $K$, level list $\mathcal{L}_X$ |
| 2. Frequency Profiling | Frequency table $\{f_k\}$, relative frequencies $\{\hat{p}_k\}$ |
| 3. Rare-Level Detection | List of rare levels ($\hat{p}_k < 0.05$) |
| 4. Bivariate Association | $\chi^{2}$, Cramér's $V$, Mutual Information $I$ |
| 5. Encoding Recommendation | Encoding map (one-hot / label / target / hashing) |
| 6. Visualisation | Bar plots, mosaic plots, heat-maps |

*(3 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for the six stages, 2 marks for diagnostic outputs)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Expected counts** using $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j} / n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= (120)(100)/300 = 40.00 \\
E_{12} &= (120)(120)/300 = 48.00 \\
E_{13} &= (120)(80)/300 = 32.00 \\
E_{21} &= (110)(100)/300 = 36.67 \\
E_{22} &= (110)(120)/300 = 44.00 \\
E_{23} &= (110)(80)/300 = 29.33 \\
E_{31} &= (70)(100)/300 = 23.33 \\
E_{32} &= (70)(120)/300 = 28.00 \\
E_{33} &= (70)(80)/300 = 18.67
\end{aligned}
$$

All $E_{ij} \ge 5$ ✔.

**Step 2 — Chi-square statistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^{2} &=\frac{(60-40)^{2}}{40}+\frac{(40-48)^{2}}{48}+\frac{(20-32)^{2}}{32} \\
&\quad+\frac{(30-36.67)^{2}}{36.67}+\frac{(50-44)^{2}}{44}+\frac{(30-29.33)^{2}}{29.33} \\
&\quad+\frac{(10-23.33)^{2}}{23.33}+\frac{(30-28)^{2}}{28}+\frac{(30-18.67)^{2}}{18.67} \\
&= \frac{400}{40}+\frac{64}{48}+\frac{144}{32}+\frac{44.49}{36.67}+\frac{36}{44}+\frac{0.45}{29.33} \\
&\quad+\frac{177.69}{23.33}+\frac{4}{28}+\frac{128.37}{18.67} \\
&= 10.000 + 1.333 + 4.500 + 1.213 + 0.818 + 0.015 \\
&\quad + 7.617 + 0.143 + 6.876 \\
\chi^{2} &\approx 32.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Degrees of freedom:**

$$
\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(3-1) = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Cramér's V:**

$$
V = \sqrt{\frac{32.52}{300 \cdot 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{32.52}{600}} = \sqrt{0.0542} \approx 0.233
$$

**Step 5 — Hypothesis test:**

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$, df = 4 is $\chi^2_{0.05,4} = 9.488$. Since $32.52 > 9.488$, **reject $H_0$**. Catalyst type and reactor class are **not independent** in this dataset. Association strength: **Weak-to-Moderate** ($V \approx 0.233$).

*(2 marks expected counts, 3 marks chi-square, 1 mark df, 1 mark V)*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Shannon entropy** and **Mutual Information** measures used in CATDEX. Derive the identity $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$ from first principles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymerisation reactor dataset contains 500 batches tagged by **initiator type** (Peroxide, Azo, Redox) and **polymer quality grade** (A, B, C). Compute the **entropy of initiator type** $H(X)$, the **entropy of quality grade** $H(Y)$, and their **Mutual Information** $I(X;Y)$ given the joint frequency table:

| | Grade A | Grade B | Grade C | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peroxide | 100 | 60 | 40 | 200 |
| Azo | 80 | 90 | 30 | 200 |
| Redox | 20 | 50 | 30 | 100 |
| **Total** | 200 | 200 | 100 | **500** |

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Shannon Entropy** of a categorical variable $X$ with distribution $\{\hat{p}_k\}$:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{p}_k \log_2 \hat{p}_k
$$

It measures the **average information content** (in bits) per observation. $H(X) = 0$ if $X$ is deterministic; $H(X) = \log_2 K$ if $X$ is uniformly distributed.

**Mutual Information** between $X$ and $Y$:

$$
I(X;Y) = \sum_{i,j} \hat{p}(x_i,y_j) \log_2 \frac{\hat{p}(x_i,y_j)}{\hat{p}(x_i)\hat{p}(y_j)}
$$

It measures the **reduction in uncertainty** about $X$ given knowledge of $Y$ (and vice versa). $I(X;Y) = 0$ iff $X$ and $Y$ are independent.

**Derivation** of $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$:

Refer to the full derivation in **§3.3** above.

*(3 marks definitions, 4 marks derivation)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Marginal probabilities** (each entry divided by $n = 500$):

$$
\hat{p}(X=\text{Peroxide}) = 0.40,\quad \hat{p}(X=\text{Azo}) = 0.40,\quad \hat{p}(X=\text{Redox}) = 0.20
$$

$$
\hat{p}(Y=\text{A}) = 0.40,\quad \hat{p}(Y=\text{B}) = 0.40,\quad \hat{p}(Y=\text{C}) = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Entropy of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[0.40\log_2 0.40 + 0.40\log_2 0.40 + 0.20\log_2 0.20] \\
&= -[(-0.5288) + (-0.5288) + (-0.4644)] \\
&= 1.5220 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Entropy of $Y$:**

Identical marginal structure → $H(Y) = 1.5220$ bits.

**Step 4 — Joint probabilities** $\hat{p}(x_i, y_j) = n_{ij} / 500$:

| | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peroxide | 0.20 | 0.12 | 0.08 |
| Azo | 0.16 | 0.18 | 0.06 |
| Redox | 0.04 | 0.10 | 0.06 |

**Step 5 — Joint entropy $H(X,Y)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X,Y) &= -\sum_{i,j} \hat{p}(x_i,y_j)\log_2 \hat{p}(x_i,y_j) \\
&= -[0.20\log_2 0.20 + 0.12\log_2 0.12 + 0.08\log_2 0.08 \\
&\quad + 0.16\log_2 0.16 + 0.18\log_2 0.18 + 0.06\log_2 0.06 \\
&\quad + 0.04\log_2 0.04 + 0.10\log_2 0.10 + 0.06\log_2 0.06] \\
&= -[(-0.4644) + (-0.3685) + (-0.2915) + (-0.4230) + (-0.4450) + (-0.2435) \\
&\quad + (-0.1858) + (-0.3322) + (-0.2435)] \\
&= 2.9974 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Mutual Information:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X;Y) &= H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) \\
&= 1.5220 + 1.5220 - 2.9974 \\
&= 0.0466 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $I(X;Y) \approx 0.047$ bits is very small relative to $H(X) \approx 1.52$ bits → initiator type provides **negligible information** about polymer quality grade in this dataset (only $\approx 3.1\%$ of $H(X)$ is shared).

*(2 marks marginals, 2 marks entropies, 2 marks joint entropy, 1 mark MI)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — CATDEX Numerical Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting to check $E_{ij} \ge 5$** before applying chi-square: Lose **1–2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Using $\min(r-1, c-1)$ instead of $\min(r, c) - 1$** in Cramér's V: very common error. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing $\log_2$ and $\ln$** in entropy: KTU expects base-2 unless stated otherwise.
> 4. **Reporting $\chi^2$ without df**: always quote both.
> 5. **Ignoring ordinal vs nominal distinction** in encoding recommendation: full-marks question can be reduced to 50%.

---

## 5.3 ⚡ Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **CATDEX = CATEGORICAL DATA EXPLORATION** — qualitative pre-modelling audit.
- ✅ Six stages: **Level Enumeration → Frequency Profiling → Rare-Level Detection → Bivariate Association → Encoding Recommendation → Visualisation**.
- ✅ Cardinality $K = \vert \mathcal{L}_X \vert$ governs encoding choice.
- ✅ Rare-level threshold: $\hat{p}_k < 0.05$ → merge or re-bin.
- ✅ Chi-square statistic: $\chi^{2} = \sum (n_{ij}-E_{ij})^{2}/E_{ij}$ with $E_{ij} = n_{i\cdot}\, n_{\cdot j} / n$.
- ✅ Degrees of freedom: $\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1)$.
- ✅ Cramér's V: $V = \sqrt{\chi^{2}/[n\,\min(r-1,c-1)]}$, range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ $V > 0.7$ → drop redundant feature; $V > 0.5$ → strong association.
- ✅ Shannon entropy: $H(X) = -\sum \hat{p}_k \log_2 \hat{p}_k$, range $[0, \log_2 K]$.
- ✅ Mutual Information: $I(X;Y) = H(X)+H(Y)-H(X,Y)$, zero iff independent.
- ✅ Class-Imbalance Ratio: CIR > 20 → stratified sampling.
- ✅ Encoding rules:
  - $K \le 10$ nominal → **one-hot**
  - Ordinal → **integer map** preserving order
  - $K > 50$ → **hashing** or **target encoding**
  - Tree models tolerate **label** encoding; linear models do not.
- ✅ Always validate $E_{ij} \ge 5$ before applying chi-square approximation.
- ✅ CATDEX maps directly to **CO1** of KTU 2024 scheme PECHT866.
- ✅ Common engineering categoricals: catalyst type, reactor class, feedstock grade, alarm severity, polymer family.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BIOEXPERT and the ExSep Algorithms
Exploring Sub-discipline of AI: Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Exploring Sub-discipline of AI: Machine Learning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Machine Learning ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Machine Learning (ML)** is formally defined as a *sub-discipline of Artificial Intelligence (AI)* that enables computational systems to **learn patterns, relationships, and decision boundaries directly from empirical data** without being explicitly programmed with rigid, rule-based instructions. According to the classical formulation by **Arthur Samuel (1959)**, ML is the "field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed." A more mathematically rigorous definition by **Tom Mitchell (1997)** states: *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."* In the context of **PECHT866 (AI-ML Applications in Chemical Engineering)**, ML is positioned as the *data-driven counterpart* to first-principles modeling. Where traditional Chemical Engineering relies on **mass, energy, and momentum balances** (governed by ODEs/PDEs), ML augments these with empirical inference from historical plant data, sensor streams, and experimental observations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Machine Learning sits at the intersection of AI, Statistics, and Optimization. It is **NOT** a replacement for chemical engineering fundamentals — it is a *complement* that handles non-linearity, noise, and high-dimensionality inherent in real plant data. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Apprentice Chemical Engineer"** > Imagine hiring a junior engineer with no prior plant experience. You don't hand them a 1000-page operations manual and say "memorize this." Instead, you give them **two years of historical logbook data** (temperatures, pressures, flow rates, catalyst activities, and product purities). The apprentice, by observing patterns, eventually learns that *"when the reactor temperature exceeds 280 °C and the feed ratio falls below 1.05, the yield drops sharply within 4 hours."* This rule was *never taught* explicitly — it was **learned from data**. > > Machine Learning is precisely this *apprentice*. Given a dataset of input-output pairs, an ML algorithm tunes its internal parameters until it can reliably map inputs (operating conditions) to outputs (yields, conversions, purities). ## 1.3 Why Machine Learning Matters in Chemical Engineering Chemical processes exhibit: - **Strong non-linearity** (Arrhenius kinetics, vapor-liquid equilibria) - **High dimensionality** (hundreds of sensors in a modern DCS) - **Process noise and drift** (sensor fouling, catalyst deactivation) - **Difficult-to-measure quality variables** (polymer molecular weight, biomass concentration) ML addresses these by **learning the input-output mapping** directly, enabling applications such as **soft sensors, inferential estimators, predictive maintenance, process optimization, and fault detection.** ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of ML — High-Level Taxonomy | ML Paradigm | Data Type | Chemical Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | **Supervised Learning** | Labeled $(X, y)$ pairs | Predicting reactor conversion from feed temperature | | **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabeled $X$ only | Clustering similar operating regimes from DCS data | | **Reinforcement Learning** | Agent–Environment interaction | Real-time optimization of a distillation column reflux ratio | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for Exams:** "**S**upervised = **S**how the answer (labeled), **U**nsupervised = **U**ncover the answer (no labels), **R**einforcement = **R**eward-based trial and error." ## 1.5 Historical Positioning of ML within AI AI is the broadest umbrella. ML is one of its **three principal sub-disciplines**, the other two being *Symbolic Reasoning* (expert systems, knowledge graphs) and *Robotics* (embodied intelligence). ML itself further decomposes into **Deep Learning** (neural networks with many layers) and **Classical ML** (regression, decision trees, SVMs, clustering). For KTU-level work in Chemical Engineering, the focus remains on **Classical ML** foundations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline — Operational Logic Supervised learning is the most exam-relevant paradigm for PECHT866. The operational pipeline is decomposed below: 1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect historical records of process variables (temperatures, pressures, flow rates, compositions) and corresponding quality/output variables (conversion, yield, purity). 2. **Feature Engineering:** Select, transform, and scale input variables. *In ChemE: dimensionless groups (Re, Sc, Pr) are often more informative than raw variables.* 3. **Model Hypothesis Selection:** Choose a functional form $h_\theta(x)$ (linear, polynomial, neural). 4. **Loss Function Definition:** Quantify the discrepancy between predictions and observations. 5. **Optimization:** Tune parameters $\theta$ to minimize the loss (typically via Gradient Descent). 6. **Validation:** Evaluate on unseen test data using metrics like $R^2$, RMSE, MAE. ## 2.2 Linear Regression — The Foundational Supervised Model For a single feature, the hypothesis is: $$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$ For $n$ features (multi-variable regression, common in ChemE where $x_1 = T$, $x_2 = P$, $x_3 = F$, etc.): $$h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$$ **The "Why":** Linear regression is the *least-squares best-fit hyperplane* in $n$-dimensional feature space. In ChemE, it forms the basis of correlations used since the 1940s (e.g., Dittus-Boelter, Wilson equation approximations). ### 2.2.1 Cost Function (Mean Squared Error) The most common loss is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$ where: - $m$ = number of training samples - $h_\theta(x^{(i)})$ = predicted output for the $i$-th sample - $y^{(i)}$ = actual (observed) output for the $i$-th sample - The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a *mathematical convenience* that cancels the exponent during differentiation. ### 2.2.2 Gradient Descent Update Rule $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$ For linear regression with MSE, this expands to: $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter). The intuition: take small steps proportional to the negative slope of the cost surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Gradient descent is the *workhorse optimizer* behind nearly every ML model — from linear regression to deep neural networks. In ChemE process optimization, this is mathematically analogous to **steepest-descent methods** used in NLP solvers and process simulators. ### 2.2.3 Closed-Form Normal Equation (Alternative to Gradient Descent) $$\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$ This gives the optimal $\theta$ in one shot, but is computationally expensive for $n > 10{,}000$ features (inverse is $O(n^3)$). ## 2.3 Classification — Logistic Regression For binary classification (e.g., "Product passes/fails spec"), the **sigmoid function** squashes linear output into $[0,1]$: $$h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}}}$$ The cost function switches to **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)**: $$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$ ## 2.4 Unsupervised Learning — K-Means Clustering Used in ChemE to identify **operating regimes** (steady-state, transient, fault) from unlabeled DCS data. The algorithm minimizes **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**: $$J_{\text{K-means}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$ where $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** most frequently asked in KTU university examinations for this module. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case in ChemE | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linear Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Modeling yield vs. temperature | | 2 | Multi-Variable Hypothesis | $h_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Correlating $T, P, F \to X$ (conversion) | | 3 | MSE Cost Function | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_i (h_\theta(x^{(i)})-y^{(i)})^2$ | Loss for regression models | | 4 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_i (h_\theta - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Parameter optimization | | 5 | Normal Equation | $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form linear regression | | 6 | Sigmoid Activation | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Binary classification output | | 7 | Logistic Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x})$ | Predict pass/fail product spec | | 8 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_i [y\log h + (1-y)\log(1-h)]$ | Cost for classifiers | | 9 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Cluster operating regimes | | 10 | R² Score | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Goodness-of-fit metric | | 11 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum_i (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}$ | Regression error magnitude | > [!WARNING] > **Units Alert:** RMSE has the same units as the output variable. If predicting conversion (dimensionless, 0–1), RMSE is also dimensionless. If predicting temperature in °C, RMSE is in °C. ## 2.6 Bias–Variance Tradeoff (Conceptual) A KTU-favorite theoretical concept. The expected prediction error decomposes as: $$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$$ - **High Bias (Underfitting):** Model too simple (e.g., linear fit to Arrhenius data). - **High Variance (Overfitting):** Model too complex, memorizes training noise. - **Irreducible Error $\sigma^2$:** Inherent data noise — *cannot be eliminated by any model*. > [!TIP] > **ChemE Intuition:** Underfitting = using a straight line for exponential kinetics. Overfitting = fitting a 50th-order polynomial through 30 noisy data points. Both fail in extrapolation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Regression Normal Equation We seek the parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimizes the MSE cost $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. ### Step 1: Express the Cost Function in Matrix Form The MSE for $m$ training samples, with design matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ and target vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, is: $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$ **Explanation:** Each row of $\mathbf{X}$ is one training sample $[1, x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \dots, x_n^{(i)}]$. The product $\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ produces a vector of all predictions. Subtracting $\mathbf{y}$ gives the error vector. The quadratic form yields the sum of squared errors. ### Step 2: Expand the Quadratic Form $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} \right)$$ **Explanation:** Using the identity $(AB)^T = B^TA^T$, the scalar $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ equals its transpose $\mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta}$, so both middle terms collapse to $2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$. $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} \right)$$ ### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ Using the matrix calculus identities: - $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} (\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ symmetric) - $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} (\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{b}$ - $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} (\mathbf{c}) = 0$ (constant) $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{1}{2m} \left( 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right) = \frac{1}{m} \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right)$$ ### Step 4: Set the Gradient to Zero (Stationary Point) $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = 0$$ ### Step 5: Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$ Multiplying both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$: $$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}}$$ This is the **Normal Equation** — the unique closed-form solution, provided $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is invertible (i.e., features are not perfectly collinear). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Linear Regression for Reactor Conversion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** A CSTR has the following historical data relating reactor temperature $T$ (°C) to conversion $X$ (fraction): > > | Sample $i$ | Temperature $T$ (°C) | Conversion $X$ | > |---|---|---| > | 1 | 60 | 0.42 | > | 2 | 70 | 0.55 | > | 3 | 80 | 0.66 | > | 4 | 90 | 0.74 | > | 5 | 100 | 0.81 | > > Fit a linear model $X = \theta_0 + \theta_1 T$ using the Normal Equation. ### Step 1: Construct the Design Matrix $\mathbf{X}$ and Target Vector $\mathbf{y}$ The design matrix (with the bias column of 1s prepended) and target: $$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 60 \\ 1 & 70 \\ 1 & 80 \\ 1 & 90 \\ 1 & 100 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.42 \\ 0.55 \\ 0.66 \\ 0.74 \\ 0.81 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 2: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 60 & 70 & 80 & 90 & 100 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 60 \\ 1 & 70 \\ 1 & 80 \\ 1 & 90 \\ 1 & 100 \end{bmatrix}$$ Row-by-row multiplication: $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum 1 & \sum T \\ \sum T & \sum T^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 400 \\ 400 & 33000 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Explanation:** - $\sum 1 = 5$ (five samples) - $\sum T = 60+70+80+90+100 = 400$ - $\sum T^2 = 3600+4900+6400+8100+10000 = 33000$ ### Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum y \\ \sum T \cdot y \end{bmatrix}$$ Computing the sums: $$\sum y = 0.42 + 0.55 + 0.66 + 0.74 + 0.81 = 3.18$$ $$\sum T \cdot y = (60)(0.42) + (70)(0.55) + (80)(0.66) + (90)(0.74) + (100)(0.81)$$ Evaluating each term: $$\sum T \cdot y = 25.2 + 38.5 + 52.8 + 66.6 + 81.0 = 264.1$$ So: $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.18 \\ 264.1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Determinant of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ $$\det(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) = (5)(33000) - (400)(400) = 165000 - 160000 = 5000$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Inverse $$(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{5000} \begin{bmatrix} 33000 & -400 \\ -400 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.6 & -0.08 \\ -0.08 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 6: Compute $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ $$\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.6 & -0.08 \\ -0.08 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3.18 \\ 264.1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Computing each element: $$\theta_0 = (6.6)(3.18) + (-0.08)(264.1) = 20.988 - 21.128 = -0.14$$ $$\theta_1 = (-0.08)(3.18) + (0.001)(264.1) = -0.2544 + 0.2641 = 0.0097$$ ### Step 7: Final Fitted Model $$\boxed{X = -0.14 + 0.0097 \, T}$$ **Interpretation:** For every 1 °C increase in reactor temperature, conversion rises by **0.97 percentage points** (since $\theta_1 = 0.0097$). The negative intercept is unphysical (extrapolation artifact) but acceptable within the observed range $60 \le T \le 100$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Gradient Descent Convergence Criterion For gradient descent to converge on linear regression, the **condition number** of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ must be finite and $\alpha$ must satisfy: $$0 < \alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}) / m)}$$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$. If $\alpha$ is too large, gradient descent **diverges (oscillates)**; if too small, convergence is impractically slow. > [!NOTE] > **Chemical Engineering Analogy:** This is identical to the **stability constraint** on the time-step $\Delta t$ in explicit numerical integration of stiff ODEs (e.g., reactor dynamics): $\Delta t < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{J})}$ where $\mathbf{J}$ is the Jacobian. --- ## 3.4 Logistic Regression — Gradient Derivation (Summary) For logistic regression, the cost gradient is: $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$ **Notice the structural similarity to linear regression!** The only difference is the definition of $h_\theta$ (sigmoid vs. linear). This elegant parallel is a key KTU exam point. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Position of Machine Learning within the AI Landscape ```mermaid graph TD A[Artificial Intelligence AI]:::root A --> B[Symbolic Reasoning] A --> C[Robotics] A --> D[Machine Learning ML]:::mlnode D --> E[Classical ML] D --> F[Deep Learning] E --> G[Supervised Learning] E --> H[Unsupervised Learning] E --> I[Reinforcement Learning] G --> J[Linear Regression] G --> K[Logistic Regression] G --> L[Decision Trees] G --> M[SVM] H --> N[K Means Clustering] H --> O[PCA] H --> P[DBSCAN] I --> Q[Q Learning] I --> R[Policy Gradient] F --> S[CNN] F --> T[RNN LSTM] F --> U[Transformers] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mlnode fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` **Reading Guide:** The red-highlighted node "Machine Learning ML" is the focal sub-discipline. Below it, the hierarchy decomposes into Classical ML and Deep Learning. ChemE applications predominantly live in the Classical ML branch. --- ## 4.2 Supervised Learning Workflow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR rawData["Raw Plant Data DCS Logs"]:::input --> preprocess["Preprocessing
Scaling and Filtering"]:::process preprocess --> split{"Train Test Split
80 20"}:::decision split --> trainSet["Training Set Xtrain ytrain"]:::data split --> testSet["Test Set Xtest ytest"]:::data trainSet --> modelTrain["Model Training
theta optimization via Gradient Descent"]:::core modelTrain --> trainedModel["Trained Model h theta of x"]:::output trainedModel --> predict["Prediction on Test Set"]:::process testSet --> predict predict --> metrics["Performance Metrics
R squared, RMSE, MAE"]:::eval metrics --> decision2{"Model Acceptable"}:::decision decision2 -->|Yes| deploy["Deploy to Plant
Inferential Sensor"]:::deploy decision2 -->|No| tune["Hyperparameter Tuning
Change alpha, features, model"]:::process tune --> modelTrain classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef process fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 classDef data fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 classDef core fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef eval fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef deploy fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.3 Linear Regression Geometric Intuition ```mermaid graph LR A["Feature Space
T axis horizontal"]:::axis --> B["Target Space
X axis vertical"]:::axis B --> C["Data Points
5 samples from CSTR"]:::points C --> D["Best Fit Line
X equals theta0 plus theta1 T"]:::line D --> E["Residual Errors
Vertical distance from points to line"]:::err classDef axis fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#000 classDef points fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef line fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:3px classDef err fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Conceptual Takeaway:** Linear regression finds the line that **minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances** from data points — this is the geometric meaning of minimizing MSE. --- ## 4.4 Gradient Descent Convergence Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Initialize theta randomly"]:::start --> computeGrad["Compute Gradient of J theta"]:::core computeGrad --> check{"Gradient Magnitude
less than epsilon"}:::decision check -->|Yes| converged["Converged
Optimal theta found"]:::end check -->|No| updateTheta["Update theta equals theta minus alpha times gradient"]:::update updateTheta --> computeGrad classDef start fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef core fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 classDef update fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef end fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chemical Engineering ML Soft Sensor ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Plant["Real World Chemical Plant"] sensor1["Temperature Sensor TC 101"]:::sensor sensor2["Pressure Sensor PT 102"]:::sensor sensor3["Flow Sensor FT 103"]:::sensor sensor4["Composition Sensor AT 104"]:::sensor end subgraph DCS["Distributed Control System"] historian["Process Historian
Time Series Database"]:::dcs end subgraph ML["Machine Learning Pipeline"] ingest["Data Ingestion Layer"]:::ml clean["Data Cleaning
Outlier Removal and Imputation"]:::ml feat["Feature Engineering
Dimensionless Numbers"]:::ml train["Model Training Module"]:::ml infer["Inference Engine
Real Time Prediction"]:::ml end subgraph Output["Decision Support"] opConsole["Operator Dashboard
Estimated Conversion Displayed"]:::output controller["Advanced Controller MPC Setpoint"]:::output alarm["Alarm System
Drift Detection Trigger"]:::output end sensor1 --> historian sensor2 --> historian sensor3 --> historian sensor4 --> historian historian --> ingest ingest --> clean clean --> feat feat --> train train --> infer infer --> opConsole infer --> controller infer --> alarm classDef sensor fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef dcs fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef ml fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef output fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Reading Guide:** This is the canonical **Inferential Sensor (Soft Sensor)** architecture used in refineries and polymer plants worldwide. ML replaces hardware analyzers (which are expensive and lag-prone) with software-based predictions. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define Machine Learning. Explain any two types of ML with a chemical engineering example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Machine Learning is a sub-discipline of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn patterns and make predictions from data without being explicitly programmed, improving performance with experience (Mitchell, 1997). **Type 1 — Supervised Learning (1 mark):** Uses labeled data (input-output pairs). - *ChemE Example:* Predicting reactor conversion from historical pairs of (Temperature, Pressure, Flow → Conversion). **Type 2 — Unsupervised Learning (1 mark):** Uses unlabeled data to discover hidden structure. - *ChemE Example:* Clustering 10,000 hourly DCS records into 3 distinct operating regimes (steady-state, transient, faulty) without prior labels. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. State one limitation of each in process modeling.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | |---|---|---| | Data | Labeled $(X, y)$ | Unlabeled $X$ only | | Goal | Predict $y$ from $X$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ | | ChemE Use | Conversion prediction | Operating regime clustering | **Limitation of Supervised (1 mark):** Requires large volumes of high-quality labeled data; in chemE, quality labels (e.g., polymer MW) are expensive and slow to obtain. **Limitation of Unsupervised (1 mark):** Results are not directly interpretable for control; clusters must be manually validated against process knowledge. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the Normal Equation for linear regression. Starting from the MSE cost function, show all matrix algebra steps clearly.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Express MSE in matrix form for $m$ samples and $n$ features: $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Expand the quadratic form using $(AB)^T = B^TA^T$: $$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} \right]$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using standard matrix calculus identities ($\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^T A \theta) = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$, and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^T b) = b$): $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{1}{m} \left[ \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \right]$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Set the gradient to zero for the stationary point (minimum): $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = 0$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ by multiplying by the inverse: $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$ $$\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** State the existence condition: this solution is valid only when $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})$ is non-singular, i.e., features are linearly independent. If singular, regularization (Ridge/Lasso) or feature removal is needed. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **For the CSTR data given below, fit a linear regression model $X = \theta_0 + \theta_1 T$ using the Normal Equation. Predict conversion at $T = 85$ °C.** | Sample | $T$ (°C) | $X$ (fraction) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 50 | 0.30 | | 2 | 65 | 0.45 | | 3 | 75 | 0.58 | | 4 | 85 | 0.70 | | 5 | 95 | 0.78 | **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Construct $\mathbf{X}$ (with bias column) and $\mathbf{y}$: $$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 50 \\ 1 & 65 \\ 1 & 75 \\ 1 & 85 \\ 1 & 95 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 \\ 0.45 \\ 0.58 \\ 0.70 \\ 0.78 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute key sums: - $\sum 1 = 5$ - $\sum T = 50+65+75+85+95 = 370$ - $\sum T^2 = 2500+4225+5625+7225+9025 = 28600$ - $\sum y = 0.30+0.45+0.58+0.70+0.78 = 2.81$ - $\sum T \cdot y = (50)(0.30)+(65)(0.45)+(75)(0.58)+(85)(0.70)+(95)(0.78)$ - $= 15.0 + 29.25 + 43.50 + 59.50 + 74.10 = 221.35$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Form $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$: $$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 370 \\ 370 & 28600 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.81 \\ 221.35 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute determinant: $$\det = (5)(28600) - (370)(370) = 143000 - 136900 = 6100$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute inverse: $$(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{6100} \begin{bmatrix} 28600 & -370 \\ -370 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.689 & -0.0607 \\ -0.0607 & 0.00082 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute $\boldsymbol{\theta}$: - $\theta_0 = (4.689)(2.81) + (-0.0607)(221.35) = 13.176 - 13.436 = -0.260$ - $\theta_1 = (-0.0607)(2.81) + (0.00082)(221.35) = -0.1706 + 0.1815 = 0.0109$ So the fitted model is: $X = -0.260 + 0.0109 \, T$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Predict at $T = 85$ °C: $$X_{\text{pred}} = -0.260 + (0.0109)(85) = -0.260 + 0.9265 = \mathbf{0.667}$$ **Comparison with observed:** Observed $X = 0.70$, predicted $X = 0.667$. Absolute error $= 0.033$ (3.3% error) — acceptable for an approximate model. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the three main paradigms of Machine Learning with one chemical engineering application for each. Draw a block diagram of the supervised learning workflow.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Paradigm 1 — Supervised Learning (1.5 marks):** - *Definition:* Learning a mapping from labeled input-output pairs $(X, y)$. - *ChemE Application:* Predicting the **octane number of gasoline** from near-infrared (NIR) spectra. Historical (spectrum, octane) pairs train a regression model that replaces slow laboratory assays with real-time NIR-based soft sensors. **Paradigm 2 — Unsupervised Learning (1.5 marks):** - *Definition:* Discovering hidden structure in unlabeled data $X$. - *ChemE Application:* **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** on a 50-sensor dataset to reduce dimensionality and identify the 3–4 dominant modes of variation in a distillation column (e.g., feed composition, reboiler duty, ambient temperature). **Paradigm 3 — Reinforcement Learning (1.5 marks):** - *Definition:* An agent learns an optimal policy by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards/penalties. - *ChemE Application:* Real-time **dynamic optimization of a batch reactor** — the agent adjusts temperature setpoints to maximize final product yield, receiving a reward proportional to yield and a penalty for constraint violations. **Block Diagram (2.5 marks):** (Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.2 — Supervised Learning Workflow Block Diagram, reproduced in student exam answer form). --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the bias-variance tradeoff in ML model selection. How does it apply to fitting a kinetic model $r = kC^n$ using experimental data?** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Bias-Variance Decomposition (3 marks):** The expected prediction error at any input $x$ decomposes as: $$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$ **Bias (1 mark):** Error from wrong model assumptions. A linear model fitting exponential kinetics has high bias. **Variance (1 mark):** Error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations. A 10th-order polynomial through 20 noisy points has high variance. **Irreducible noise (1 mark):** Inherent measurement error — cannot be reduced by any model. **Application to Kinetic Model Fitting (3 marks):** For $r = kC^n$, the **linearized form** is $\ln r = \ln k + n \ln C$. Consider three candidate models: - *Model A:* Constant ($n=0$, zero-order) — **High bias**, low variance. - *Model B:* First-order ($n=1$) — balanced bias/variance if true order is ~1. - *Model C:* Third-order polynomial in $\ln C$ — **High variance** if data is limited; fits noise. **Optimal Choice (1 mark):** Use **cross-validation** (e.g., 5-fold CV) to compute validation MSE for each model and select the one with minimum validation error. This balances bias and variance to minimize generalization error. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ML Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the bias column:** When forming $\mathbf{X}$ for the Normal Equation, students often omit the leading column of 1s. This causes $\theta_0$ (intercept) to be unidentifiable. **Always include the bias term explicitly.** > > 2. **Confusing MSE with RMSE:** MSE has units of $(\text{output})^2$; RMSE has the same units as the output. Examiners may explicitly ask for one — read carefully. > > 3. **Linear vs. Logistic Confusion:** Linear regression outputs continuous values (conversion, yield). Logistic regression outputs probabilities in $[0,1]$ for classification (pass/fail). Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks easily. > > 4. **Missing the gradient descent update derivation:** Many students write the formula but cannot derive it. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes derivations — practice the matrix calculus steps from $(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$ to the final gradient. > > 5. **Units in MSE/RMSE:** Always quote the units explicitly (e.g., "RMSE = 0.012 in conversion fraction"). Examiners reward unit awareness. > > 6. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor explanation:** Many students include the $\frac{1}{2}$ but cannot explain *why*. Always state: "It is a mathematical convenience that cancels during differentiation." --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Quick Recall for KTU Module 2:** - ✅ **ML Definition:** Learning from experience E to improve performance P on task T (Mitchell, 1997). - ✅ **Three Paradigms:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Reinforcement (reward-based). - ✅ **Linear Regression Hypothesis:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$; vectorized: $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$. - ✅ **MSE Cost:** $J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta - y)^2$ — penalizes squared errors. - ✅ **Gradient Descent Rule:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$; $\alpha$ = learning rate. - ✅ **Normal Equation:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$ — closed-form, no iteration needed. - ✅ **Sigmoid Function:** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ — squashes output to $[0,1]$ for classification. - ✅ **Logistic Cost (Binary Cross-Entropy):** $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum [y\log h + (1-y)\log(1-h)]$. - ✅ **K-Means Objective:** Minimize WCSS = $\sum_k \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$. - ✅ **Bias-Variance:** Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$; underfit = high bias; overfit = high variance. - ✅ **ChemE Applications:** Soft sensors, inferential estimators, process optimization, fault detection, PCA-based monitoring. - ✅ **Stability of Gradient Descent:** $\alpha < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ — analog of explicit ODE time-step stability. - ✅ **R² Metric:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$; $R^2 \to 1$ indicates excellent fit. - ✅ **RMSE Metric:** $\sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum (y - \hat{y})^2}$ — same units as output. - ✅ **Train/Test Split:** Typically 80/20 or 70/30 to assess generalization. - ✅ **Feature Scaling:** Critical for gradient descent convergence; use standardization (z-score) or min-max scaling. - ✅ **Multicollinearity:** When $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ is singular, use Ridge Regression (L2 regularization) or drop correlated features.

topics.title: Supervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an algorithmic model is trained on a **labelled dataset** $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where each input vector $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is paired with a corresponding known target output $y_i$. The objective is to learn a mapping function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ parameterized by $\theta$ such that the predicted output $\hat{y} = f_\theta(x)$ generalizes well to **unseen** data drawn from the same underlying distribution.

In the context of **Chemical Engineering**, supervised learning translates historical plant/process data (temperatures, pressures, flow rates, concentrations, catalyst activities) into predictive models that can estimate **outputs** like conversion, yield, selectivity, purity, or classify products as **on-spec / off-spec**.

The two principal sub-classes are:

| Sub-class | Target $y$ Type | Typical ChE Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Regression** | Continuous (real-valued) | Predicting reactor conversion, heat-transfer coefficient, distillation column efficiency |
| **Classification** | Categorical (discrete labels) | Product grade classification (A/B/C), fault detection (Normal / Faulty), polymer quality (Accept / Reject) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lab Technician"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a senior lab technician who has spent 30 years analysing samples. Every day, a junior engineer hands over the operating conditions (temperature, pressure, residence time), and the technician instantly announces the expected yield. The technician did not "derive" anything from first principles — he learned the relationship by **observing thousands of correctly labelled (input, output) pairs**. Supervised learning builds exactly such an "experienced expert" digitally.

**Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary):**

- **Features / Predictors** ($x$): Independent process variables (e.g., $T$, $P$, $F_{in}$, $C_A$).
- **Label / Target** ($y$): The dependent variable to be predicted (yield, purity, class).
- **Hypothesis / Model** ($f_\theta$): The mathematical function approximator being learned.
- **Training Set**: The portion of labelled data used to fit the parameters $\theta$.
- **Test Set**: Held-out data used to estimate **generalization performance**.
- **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$: Quantitative measure of prediction error.
- **Generalization**: Ability of the trained model to perform accurately on **unseen** data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify whether the ChE problem is regression (e.g., predicting $X_A$, conversion) or classification (e.g., polymer grade).
2. **Data Collection & Labelling** — Gather historical plant/SCADA/DAQ records and ensure each sample carries a correct $y$ label.
3. **Data Pre-processing** — Handle missing values, remove outliers (e.g., sensor glitches), scale features (Standardization / Min-Max), encode categorical variables.
4. **Train–Validation–Test Split** — Typically $70\%:15\%:15\%$ or $80\%:10\%:10\%$.
5. **Model Selection** — Choose hypothesis family: Linear Regression, Decision Tree, SVM, k-NN, Random Forest, Neural Network, etc.
6. **Loss Function Specification** — MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
7. **Optimization** — Adjust parameters $\theta$ by minimizing $\mathcal{L}$, usually via **Gradient Descent**.
8. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — Use **Cross-Validation** on the training fold.
9. **Final Evaluation** — Compute held-out test metrics (RMSE, R², Accuracy, F1).
10. **Deployment** — Integrate into plant DCS / soft-sensor for real-time inference.

## 2.2 The Bias–Variance Tradeoff (Core KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias** = error from *wrong assumptions* (underfitting).
> **Variance** = error from *sensitivity to training fluctuations* (overfitting).
> The **irreducible error** is noise inherent in the data (sensor noise, stochastic molecular events).

The expected prediction error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^{2}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big)}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big)}_{\text{Model Sensitivity}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ChE Intuition:** A linear model predicting a non-linear reaction rate curve exhibits **high bias** (it cannot capture the curvature). A 20-degree polynomial fitted to 50 noisy composition points exhibits **high variance** (it oscillates wildly between data points and fails on new batches).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Model (1-D) | $\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x$ | $w_0$ = intercept, $w_1$ = slope |
| 2 | Linear Model (Multi-D) | $\hat{y} = w^{T}x + b$ | $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | Same units as $y^{2}$ |
| 4 | Root Mean Squared Error | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 5 | Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 6 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | $1$ = perfect, $0$ = baseline mean |
| 7 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum \!\left[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$ | Classification |
| 8 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 9 | L2 Regularization (Ridge) | $\mathcal{L}_{R} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum w_j^{2}$ | Penalizes large weights |
| 10 | L1 Regularization (Lasso) | $\mathcal{L}_{L} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ | Induces sparsity |
| 11 | Confusion Matrix Counts | TP, FP, FN, TN | Classification |
| 12 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | Fraction correct |
| 13 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 14 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| 15 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec}\cdot\text{Rec}}{\text{Prec}+\text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 16 | k-Fold CV Error | $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k}\mathcal{L}_{j}$ | Robust estimate |
| 17 | Softmax (Multi-class) | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilistic output |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ChE plants, supervised learning powers **soft-sensors** (inferring hard-to-measure variables like composition from easy-to-measure temperatures/flows), **process digital twins**, **predictive maintenance** of pumps/columns, and **closed-loop MPC** boosters. It enables real-time release of pharmaceutical batches (PAT — Process Analytical Technology) and predictive control of polymerization reactors where on-line viscosity sensors are unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Solution

For a linear regression model $\hat{y}_i = w^{T}x_i + b$, we wish to minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors**:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\!\left(y_i - w^{T}x_i - b\right)^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared term** (for clarity, absorb $b$ into $w$ by augmenting $x$ with a constant $1$; equivalently we work with $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ and $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$):

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - w^{T}x_i)^{2} = (y - Xw)^{T}(y - Xw)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the matrix product**:

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = y^{T}y - y^{T}Xw - w^{T}X^{T}y + w^{T}X^{T}Xw$$

Since $y^{T}Xw$ is a scalar, it equals its transpose $w^{T}X^{T}y$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = y^{T}y - 2\,w^{T}X^{T}y + w^{T}X^{T}Xw$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient w.r.t. $w$ and set to zero (first-order optimality condition)**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -2X^{T}y + 2X^{T}Xw = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $w$**:

$$X^{T}Xw = X^{T}y$$

$$w^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$$

where $X^{+}$ = $(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** when $X^{T}X$ is invertible (i.e., features are not perfectly collinear).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Students are expected to know this derivation, recognize the **Normal Equation**, and articulate the conditions under which it fails (singular $X^{T}X$ → use Ridge regularization).

## 3.2 Gradient Descent Derivation for Single-Variable MSE

Consider $\mathcal{L}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(y_i - w x_i)^{2}$ (ignoring bias for brevity).

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{i}x_i(y_i - w x_i) = \frac{2}{N}\sum_{i}x_i(w x_i - y_i)$$

**Step 2 — Apply iterative update**:

$$w^{(t+1)} = w^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{2}{N}\sum_{i}x_i(w^{(t)} x_i - y_i)$$

**Step 3 — Convergence criterion**: Stop when $\vert \mathcal{L}^{(t+1)} - \mathcal{L}^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ or after a fixed number of epochs $T$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (ChE Context)

> **Problem:** A CSTR is operated at various temperatures $T$ (°C), and the steady-state conversion $X_A$ is recorded. Use OLS to fit $X_A = w_0 + w_1 T$.

**Data (5 samples):**

| Sample | $T_i$ (°C) | $X_{A,i}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 0.32 |
| 2 | 60 | 0.45 |
| 3 | 70 | 0.55 |
| 4 | 80 | 0.62 |
| 5 | 90 | 0.70 |

**Step 1 — Build $X$ (augmented with 1's for bias)**:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 50 \\ 1 & 60 \\ 1 & 70 \\ 1 & 80 \\ 1 & 90 \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.32 \\ 0.45 \\ 0.55 \\ 0.62 \\ 0.70 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $X^{T}X$**:

$$X^{T}X = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 350 \\ 350 & 25500 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $X^{T}y$**:

$$X^{T}y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.32+0.45+0.55+0.62+0.70 \\ 50(0.32)+60(0.45)+70(0.55)+80(0.62)+90(0.70) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.64 \\ 190.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Determinant** of $X^{T}X$:

$$\det(X^{T}X) = (5)(25500) - (350)^{2} = 127500 - 122500 = 5000$$

**Step 5 — Inverse**:

$$(X^{T}X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{5000}\begin{bmatrix} 25500 & -350 \\ -350 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.10 & -0.07 \\ -0.07 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve**:

$$w^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.10 & -0.07 \\ -0.07 & 0.001 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.64 \\ 190.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13.464 - 13.342 \\ -0.1848 + 0.1906 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.122 \\ 0.0058 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Model**:

$$\boxed{\hat{X}_A = 0.122 + 0.0058\,T}$$

**Verification for $T=75°C$**: $\hat{X}_A = 0.122 + 0.0058(75) = 0.122 + 0.435 = 0.557$ (a reasonable interpolation between samples 3 and 4).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supervised Learning Block Architecture (ChE Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph dataLayer["DATA ACQUISITION"]
        direction TB
        nodeA["Plant SCADA / DCS"]
        nodeB["Lab QC Database"]
        nodeC["Historical Logs CSV"]
    end

    subgraph preprocessLayer["PRE-PROCESSING"]
        direction TB
        nodeD["Cleaning & Imputation"]
        nodeE["Outlier Removal"]
        nodeF["Feature Scaling (StandardScaler)"]
    end

    subgraph splitLayer["DATA PARTITIONING"]
        direction TB
        nodeG["Training Set 70 percent"]
        nodeH["Validation Set 15 percent"]
        nodeI["Test Set 15 percent"]
    end

    subgraph modelLayer["MODEL TRAINING"]
        direction TB
        nodeJ["Hypothesis f_theta"]
        nodeK["Loss Function MSE / Cross Entropy"]
        nodeL["Optimizer Gradient Descent"]
    end

    subgraph evalLayer["EVALUATION"]
        direction TB
        nodeM["Metrics R2 RMSE F1"]
        nodeN["Cross Validation k Fold"]
        nodeO["Hyperparameter Tuning"]
    end

    subgraph deployLayer["DEPLOYMENT"]
        direction TB
        nodeP["Soft Sensor Inference"]
        nodeQ["Plant Dashboard Alerts"]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ
```

## 4.2 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visualization (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Underfitting\nHigh Bias\nLow Variance\nModel too simple\nEx Linear for cubic data"]
    nodeB["Good Fit\nLow Bias\nLow Variance\nOptimal complexity"]
    nodeC["Overfitting\nLow Bias\nHigh Variance\nModel too complex\nEx 20 deg polynomial"]
    nodeA -->|Increase complexity| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Increase complexity| nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Moving left → right increases model complexity. The *sweet spot* is the minimum of the combined **Bias² + Variance** curve, above which the irreducible noise floor $\sigma^{2}_\varepsilon$ dominates.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
Differentiate between **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning. Give **one chemical engineering example** of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Supervised learning** uses *labelled* data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ to learn the mapping $x \rightarrow y$. It includes regression and classification tasks.
  - *ChE Example:* Predicting reactor conversion $X_A$ from feed temperature, pressure and residence time (Regression).
- **Unsupervised learning** uses *unlabelled* data $\{x_i\}$ to discover hidden structure (clusters, manifolds, anomalies).
  - *ChE Example:* Clustering historical operating data of a distillation column to identify distinct operating regimes without predefined labels.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
Define the **bias-variance tradeoff**. State its mathematical decomposition.

**Model Answer:**
The bias-variance tradeoff describes the decomposition of the expected prediction error of a learning algorithm into three components:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}\right] = \text{Bias}^{2}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) + \text{Var}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) + \sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}$$

where:
- $\text{Bias}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) = \mathbb{E}\!\big[\hat{f}(x)\big] - f(x)$ — error from wrong/oversimplified assumptions.
- $\text{Var}\!\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^{2}\right]$ — sensitivity to training set fluctuations.
- $\sigma^{2}_\varepsilon$ — irreducible noise (sensor noise, stochastic molecular events).

A *good* model achieves low bias **and** low variance simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** closed-form solution for a multiple linear regression model $\hat{y} = Xw$. State and justify one condition under which the OLS solution fails, and explain how **Ridge regression** addresses it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Define the loss as the squared-error norm:
$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \vert \vert y - Xw \vert \vert^{2}_{2} = (y - Xw)^{T}(y - Xw)$$

2. Expand:
$$\mathcal{L}(w) = y^{T}y - 2w^{T}X^{T}y + w^{T}X^{T}Xw$$

3. Differentiate w.r.t. $w$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -2X^{T}y + 2X^{T}Xw = 0$$

4. Rearrange (Normal Equation):
$$X^{T}Xw = X^{T}y$$

5. Closed-form solution:
$$w^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$$

**Condition of Failure:** When $X^{T}X$ is **singular** (non-invertible), i.e., when features are linearly dependent (e.g., $T_K = T_{^\circ C} + 273.15$ gives perfect collinearity). This also arises when $N < d+1$ (fewer samples than features).

**Ridge Fix:** Adds an $\ell_2$ penalty $\lambda I$ to make the matrix invertible and well-conditioned:
$$w^{*}_{\text{Ridge}} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}y$$

where $\lambda > 0$ ensures $X^{T}X + \lambda I$ is positive definite.

---

**(b)** A batch reactor dataset contains $N = 100$ observations of conversion $X_A$ versus temperature $T$, pressure $P$, and catalyst loading $C_{cat}$. You fit a linear model and obtain $\text{RMSE} = 0.08$ on training and $\text{RMSE} = 0.21$ on test data. Diagnose the issue and propose **two** remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Diagnosis:** Train error ($0.08$) is *much* smaller than test error ($0.21$) → classic signature of **overfitting** (high variance). The model has memorized the training noise rather than learning the underlying trend.
2. **Evidence:** A large gap between training and test error is the textbook indicator of overfitting, NOT underfitting (which would show both errors large and similar).
3. **Remedy 1 — Regularization:** Apply Ridge ($\ell_2$) or Lasso ($\ell_1$) regression to shrink the coefficients:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Ridge}} = \text{MSE} + \lambda \sum_j w_j^{2}$$
Tune $\lambda$ via cross-validation.
4. **Remedy 2 — Reduce Model Complexity / Feature Selection:** Drop irrelevant features (e.g., use Lasso to set some coefficients to exactly $0$), or reduce polynomial degree.
5. **Remedy 3 (alternative):** Collect more training samples (variance decreases $\propto 1/N$ asymptotically).
6. **Remedy 4 (alternative):** Apply early stopping during gradient descent or use ensemble bagging to reduce variance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)** algorithm for classification. A polymer plant classifies product pellets as "Pass" or "Fail" using $k = 5$ neighbors based on tensile strength and elongation. Given a new pellet with $(x_1, x_2) = (45, 30)$ and the 5 nearest labelled neighbours:

| Neighbour | $(x_1, x_2)$ | Label |
|---|---|---|
| $N_1$ | (42, 28) | Pass |
| $N_2$ | (50, 35) | Fail |
| $N_3$ | (44, 31) | Pass |
| $N_4$ | (47, 29) | Fail |
| $N_5$ | (46, 32) | Pass |

Predict the class using majority voting. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:**
1. Compute Euclidean distance from query $x$ to every training point:
$$d(x, x_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - x_{i,j})^{2}}$$

2. Select the $k$ smallest distances (the $k$ nearest neighbours).
3. For classification, take the **majority vote** of their labels.

**Computation for the query $(45, 30)$:**

$$d(x, N_1) = \sqrt{(45-42)^{2} + (30-28)^{2}} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$

$$d(x, N_2) = \sqrt{(45-50)^{2} + (30-35)^{2}} = \sqrt{25+25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$$

$$d(x, N_3) = \sqrt{(45-44)^{2} + (30-31)^{2}} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

$$d(x, N_4) = \sqrt{(45-47)^{2} + (30-29)^{2}} = \sqrt{4+1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$

$$d(x, N_5) = \sqrt{(45-46)^{2} + (30-32)^{2}} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$

**Vote Tally:** Pass = 3 ($N_1, N_3, N_5$), Fail = 2 ($N_2, N_4$).

**Prediction:** **"Pass"** (majority vote).

---

**(b)** Explain the **confusion matrix** and derive the formulas for **Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Accuracy**. Why is **accuracy alone insufficient** for imbalanced chemical-quality datasets? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Confusion Matrix** for binary classification:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN}$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$\text{Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$\text{F1} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Why Accuracy Fails on Imbalanced Data:**
Consider a pharma batch-release scenario where only $1\%$ of batches are contaminated. A trivial classifier predicting "Clean" for **every** batch achieves $99\%$ accuracy yet **misses all contaminated batches** — a catastrophic public-health failure.

In such cases:
- **Precision** tells us: *"Of the batches we flagged as contaminated, how many really were?"*
- **Recall** tells us: *"Of the truly contaminated batches, how many did we catch?"*
- **F1-score** balances both, making it the preferred metric when class distributions are skewed — common in chemical fault detection and safety-critical QC.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing precision and recall:** Precision is about *predicted* positives, recall is about *actual* positives. Mixing them up loses $1\text{–}2$ marks per sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to scale/normalize** when applying k-NN to features of different units (e.g., temperature in K vs. pressure in bar). Always StandardScaler before distance-based methods.
> 3. **Reporting only $R^{2}$ or accuracy** without discussing domain suitability — KTU examiners expect you to *justify* the metric choice.
> 4. **Omitting units** in RMSE/MAE answers (marking scheme deducts for ambiguous answers).
> 5. **Skipping the assumption list** in OLS (linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals — LINE assumptions).
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the train/test split rationale** in any model-building answer — this is a guaranteed $1$-mark question in 14-mark problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Supervised learning = learning from **labelled** $(x, y)$ pairs to predict $y$ for new $x$.
- ✅ Two branches: **Regression** (continuous $y$) and **Classification** (categorical $y$).
- ✅ Pipeline: Data → Pre-process → Split → Train → Validate → Test → Deploy.
- ✅ Train/Validation/Test split is mandatory to detect **overfitting**.
- ✅ **MSE** for regression; **Cross-Entropy** for classification.
- ✅ **Bias² + Variance + σ²** = total expected error (irreducible noise floor).
- ✅ Overfitting → high train accuracy, low test accuracy → use **regularization, more data, simpler model, cross-validation**.
- ✅ Underfitting → both errors high → use **more complex model, more features, less regularization**.
- ✅ **OLS closed-form:** $w^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y$. Fails when $X^{T}X$ is singular → use **Ridge** with $\lambda I$ term.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent:** iterative, $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$. Learning rate $\eta$ critically controls convergence.
- ✅ **k-NN:** Lazy, non-parametric; sensitive to feature scaling; majority vote for classification, mean for regression.
- ✅ **Imbalanced data** ⇒ accuracy is misleading ⇒ use Precision, Recall, F1, or ROC-AUC.
- ✅ **ChE applications:** soft-sensors, predictive maintenance, product quality classification, digital twins, PAT, MPC boosters.
- ✅ Always **justify metric choice** and **report units** in numerical answers for full marks.
- ✅ **Cross-validation** (especially $k$-fold) is the gold standard for hyperparameter tuning when data is limited — typical ChE pilot-plant datasets are small ($N < 200$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
