> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a goal-directed computational framework of machine learning in which an autonomous **agent** acquires an optimal behavioral policy by sequentially interacting with a dynamic **environment**, executing **actions**, observing resultant **states**, and receiving scalar **reward** signals — with the explicit objective of maximizing the expected cumulative long-term return.

In the context of **Chemical Engineering**, Reinforcement Learning provides a model-free, data-driven control and decision-making paradigm where the controller (agent) learns optimal operating set-points for processes such as **CSTR temperature regulation, distillation reflux ratio tuning, catalytic reactor feed modulation, and batch scheduling**, without requiring an explicit first-principles dynamic model.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Agent | $\mathcal{A}$ | The decision-maker (e.g., a controller in a plant loop) |
| Environment | $\mathcal{E}$ | The external dynamic system (e.g., a chemical reactor) |
| State | $s \in \mathcal{S}$ | A complete snapshot of the environment (T, P, C$_A$) |
| Action | $a \in \mathcal{A}$ | The control move the agent chooses (e.g., valve position) |
| Reward | $r \in \mathbb{R}$ | Scalar feedback signal (e.g., $-$cost, $+$purity) |
| Policy | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Strategy mapping states to action probabilities |
| Return (Cumulative) | $G_t$ | Discounted sum of future rewards from time $t$ |
| Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | Weighting constant prioritizing near vs. far rewards |
| Value Function | $V^\pi(s)$ | Expected return starting from state $s$ under policy $\pi$ |
| Action-Value (Q) Function | $Q^\pi(s,a)$ | Expected return for action $a$ in state $s$ under $\pi$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine training a puppy to fetch a ball.** Every time the puppy moves toward the ball, you give it a treat (**positive reward**). If it wanders off, you say "No!" (**negative reward**). The puppy doesn't understand the physics of throwing or catching — it simply learns a *behavior* by trial-and-error to maximize the number of treats it receives.

**Reinforcement Learning works identically for a chemical plant:**

- The **puppy** = the RL controller / algorithm.
- The **ball and room** = the chemical process (temperature, concentration, pressure).
- The **treats and scoldings** = the reward function (e.g., $+$1 for staying inside set-point, $-$10 for violating safety limits).
- The **learned behavior** = the optimal control policy.

The agent initially knows **nothing** about the plant dynamics. Through thousands of simulated or real interactions, it discovers which **action (e.g., opening a coolant valve by $\Delta u$)** yields the highest long-term yield, purity, or energy efficiency.

## 1.4 Why RL Matters for Chemical Engineers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Traditional **PID controllers** require a known transfer function. **MPC (Model Predictive Control)** requires an accurate dynamic model. But many chemical processes — polymerization kinetics, bioreactors, multiphase flows — are **highly nonlinear, partially observed, and time-varying**. RL bypasses the need for an explicit model by learning directly from interaction, making it ideal for **adaptive process control, real-time optimization (RTO), and digital twin tuning**.

## 1.5 Types of Reinforcement Learning

1. **Model-Free RL** — Learns directly from experience (Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN).
2. **Model-Based RL** — Builds an internal model of the environment (Dyna, PILCO).
3. **Value-Based RL** — Learns value/Q-functions (Q-Learning, DQN).
4. **Policy-Based RL** — Directly parameterizes the policy (REINFORCE, PPO).
5. **Actor-Critic RL** — Hybrid of value + policy methods (A2C, DDPG).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) — Foundation of RL

An RL problem is formally modeled as a **Markov Decision Process**, denoted by the 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.

**The Markov Property:**
$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots, s_0, a_0) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

This states that **the future depends only on the present, not the past**. For a chemical process, this implies the future state (next temperature, next concentration) depends only on the current state and current action — a property many lumped-parameter CSTR models already satisfy.

## 2.2 The Return (Cumulative Reward)

The objective of the agent is to maximize the **discounted return** $G_t$:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

- If $\gamma = 0$: Agent is **myopic** — only immediate reward matters.
- If $\gamma \to 1$: Agent is **far-sighted** — future rewards are weighted almost equally.

## 2.3 State-Value Function $V^\pi(s)$

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

This represents the **expected long-term reward** the agent will accumulate starting from state $s$ and thereafter following policy $\pi$.

## 2.4 Action-Value Function $Q^\pi(s, a)$

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

This represents the **expected return** if the agent takes action $a$ in state $s$ and then follows policy $\pi$.

## 2.5 The Bellman Expectation Equation (Core Identity)

The value function satisfies a **recursive decomposition**:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

Similarly for the action-value:

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s', a') \right]$$

## 2.6 Bellman Optimality Equation

For the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$, we obtain:

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

The optimal policy is then:

$$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q^*(s, a)$$

## 2.7 Q-Learning Update Rule (Off-Policy TD Control)

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

Where:
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ = **learning rate**
- The term in brackets is the **TD-error** $\delta_t$.

## 2.8 Exploration vs. Exploitation ($\varepsilon$-Greedy)

To balance learning new strategies and using known good strategies:

$$a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \quad (\text{exploit}) \\ \text{random } a \in \mathcal{A} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \quad (\text{explore}) \end{cases}$$

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they constitute ~70% of KTU RL numerical/derivations.**

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Total discounted return | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | Bellman expectation for V | $V$ in reward units |
| 3 | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')Q^\pi(s', a')]$ | Bellman expectation for Q | $Q$ in reward units |
| 4 | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Bellman optimality for V | Optimal value |
| 5 | $Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')]$ | Bellman optimality for Q | Optimal action-value |
| 6 | $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha [r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)]$ | Q-Learning update | Incremental |
| 7 | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$ | Optimal policy | Deterministic |
| 8 | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | TD-error | Reward units |
| 9 | $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ | Markov property | Probability |

## 2.10 Real-World Chemical Engineering Utility

| RL Concept | Chemical Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|
| State $s$ | Sensor readings: T, P, C$_A$, pH, flowrate |
| Action $a$ | Valve opening %, pump speed, feed flowrate |
| Reward $r$ | $+$product purity, $-$energy cost, $-$safety violation |
| Policy $\pi$ | Optimal control law replacing PID / MPC |
| Q-function | Long-term value of each control decision |
| Exploration | Trying new operating points (in simulation) |
| Exploitation | Locking onto known best set-points |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi(s)$

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of $V^\pi(s)$:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand $G_t$ as immediate reward + discounted future return:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 — Condition on action $a$ and next state $s'$:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V^\pi(s')$:**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

**This is the Bellman Expectation Equation — a system of linear equations in $V^\pi$ that can be solved iteratively (Policy Evaluation) or directly (Linear System Solve for small state spaces).**

## 3.2 Derivation: Bellman Optimality Equation

**Step 1 — For the optimal policy, we maximize over actions:**

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V^*(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2 — Make the expectation explicit:**

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

**Step 3 — For $Q^*$, substitute the max into the inner expression:**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Q-Learning Update Rule from TD(0)

**Step 1 — Temporal Difference Target (1-step bootstrap):**

The estimated return using current estimate $Q$ is:

$$\hat{G}_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a)$$

**Step 2 — Define the TD-error as the difference between target and current estimate:**

$$\delta_t = \hat{G}_t - Q(S_t, A_t) = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$

**Step 3 — Stochastic Gradient Descent step on the squared TD-error loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \delta_t^2$:**

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \delta_t$$

**Step 4 — Expand $\delta_t$:**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

**Convergence Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992):** Under the conditions that all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often, learning rate $\alpha$ decays appropriately (e.g., $\sum \alpha_t = \infty, \sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$), and reward is bounded, $Q(s,a) \to Q^*(s,a)$ with probability 1.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Q-Learning Update

**Problem:** Consider a 3-state ($s_1, s_2, s_3$) chemical batch process. The agent takes actions $\{a_1, a_2\}$. Given the current Q-table, perform one Q-learning update.

**Initial Q-Table (all zeros):**
$Q(s_1, a_1) = 0$, $Q(s_1, a_2) = 0$, $Q(s_2, a_1) = 0$, $Q(s_2, a_2) = 0$, $Q(s_3, a_1) = 0$, $Q(s_3, a_2) = 0$

**Transition observed:** Agent was in state $s_1$, took action $a_1$, received reward $r = +5$, transitioned to state $s_2$.

**Hyperparameters:** $\alpha = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — Identify TD-target:**

$$\text{Target} = r + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_2, a) = 5 + 0.9 \cdot \max(0, 0) = 5$$

**Step 2 — Compute TD-error:**

$$\delta = \text{Target} - Q(s_1, a_1) = 5 - 0 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Apply update:**

$$Q(s_1, a_1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \cdot 5 = 0.5$$

**Updated Q-Table:** Only $Q(s_1, a_1)$ becomes **0.5**; all others remain **0**.

This demonstrates how Q-learning **bootstraps** — even with no prior knowledge, a single informative experience nudges the Q-value toward the observed reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Agent-Environment Interaction Loop (Core RL Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["AGENT\nPolicy pi"] -- "Action a_t" --> nodeB["ENVIRONMENT\nChemical Process"]
    nodeB -- "Reward r_{t+1}" --> nodeA
    nodeB -- "Next State s_{t+1}" --> nodeA

**Interpretation:** The agent (controller) observes state $s_t$ (e.g., CSTR temperature), selects action $a_t$ (e.g., coolant flowrate), the environment (reactor) transitions to $s_{t+1}$ and emits reward $r_{t+1}$ (e.g., closer to set-point).

## 4.2 MDP Block-Level Functional Architecture

flowchart TB

        stepA["State Space S\nT, P, C_A, F"]

```

    nodeLoop{"For each episode"}

    nodeObserve["Observe r and s_next"]

## 4.4 Chemical Engineering RL Control Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePlant["CHEMICAL PLANT\nReactor or Column"]
    nodeSensor["Sensors\nT, P, C, F"]
    nodeRLAgent["RL CONTROLLER\nQ Network or Table"]
    nodeActuator["Actuators\nValves and Pumps"]
    nodeReward["Reward Calculator\nPurity and Energy Penalty"]
    nodePlant -- "process variables" --> nodeSensor
    nodeSensor -- "state s_t" --> nodeRLAgent
    nodeRLAgent -- "action a_t" --> nodeActuator
    nodeActuator -- "control signals" --> nodePlant

```

**Engineering Insight:** In a real **CSTR temperature control** application, the state $s_t$ contains the reactor temperature, jacket temperature, and reactant concentration. The agent's action modulates the coolant flow set-point. The reward function typically encodes:
$$r_t = -w_1 (T_{sp} - T_{measured})^2 - w_2 \cdot \text{EnergyConsumed}_t - w_3 \cdot \text{SafetyViolation}_t$$

where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are weighting constants tuned by the engineer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define Reinforcement Learning. List its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a machine learning paradigm in which an **agent** learns an optimal **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an **environment** through **actions**, observing **states**, and receiving **rewards**, with the goal of maximizing the **expected cumulative discounted return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

**Key Components (1 mark each bullet):**
1. Agent — decision-maker (controller).
2. Environment — external dynamic system (plant).
3. State $s$ — environment observation.
4. Action $a$ — control decision.
5. Reward $r$ — scalar feedback signal.
6. Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ — behavior strategy.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in reinforcement learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **exploration-exploitation tradeoff** balances two competing strategies:

- **Exploitation**: Choose the best-known action based on current knowledge to maximize immediate reward (greedy with respect to $Q(s,a)$).
- **Exploration**: Choose random or less-known actions to gather new information about the environment that may yield higher long-term rewards.

**Resolution Strategy (1 mark):** The **$\varepsilon$-greedy policy** selects a random action with probability $\varepsilon$ and the greedy action with probability $1 - \varepsilon$. Over time, $\varepsilon$ is typically **annealed** (decayed) from a high value (e.g., 0.9) to a low value (e.g., 0.01), allowing early exploration and late exploitation.

**Chemical Engineering Example:** A controller must decide whether to stay at the proven optimum (exploit) or try a new feed-flow set-point that might improve yield but could damage catalyst (explore).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^\pi(s)$ in a Markov Decision Process. Clearly state the meaning of each term and explain how it can be solved iteratively.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Q-Learning algorithm with its update rule. Mention its convergence properties.**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the state-value function (1 mark):**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand $G_t$ into immediate + future reward (2 marks):**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+2} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the action $a$ and next state $s'$ (2 marks):**

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize inner expectation as $V^\pi(s')$ and write final equation (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]}$$

**Meaning of each term (1 mark):**
- $\pi(a \mid s)$: probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ under policy $\pi$.
- $p(s', r \mid s, a)$: environment's transition probability.
- $r$: immediate reward.
- $\gamma$: discount factor for future rewards.
- $V^\pi(s')$: long-term value of the next state.

**Iterative Solution (Policy Evaluation) (1 mark):** Start with arbitrary $V_0(s)$, then iterate:
$$V_{k+1}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V_k(s')]$$
until $\|V_{k+1} - V_k\|_\infty < \delta$ for a small tolerance $\delta$.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Define Q-function (1 mark):**
$$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**Step 2 — Write Bellman Optimality for $Q^*$ (2 marks):**
$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Q-Learning Update Rule (2 marks):**
The update is a stochastic gradient step minimizing $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} [r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)]^2$:

$$\boxed{Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]}$$

**Step 4 — Algorithm Steps (1 mark):**
1. Initialize $Q(s,a)$ arbitrarily.
2. Observe $s_t$.
3. Choose $a_t$ via $\varepsilon$-greedy.
4. Execute $a_t$, observe $r_{t+1}, s_{t+1}$.
5. Update $Q(s_t, a_t)$.
6. Set $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$, repeat.

**Step 5 — Convergence Properties (1 mark):** Per Watkins & Dayan (1992), Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 if:
- All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often.
- Learning rate $\alpha$ satisfies: $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
- Rewards are bounded and $\gamma < 1$.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A CSTR temperature control system has 3 discrete states (Low, Normal, High) and 2 actions (Increase coolant, Decrease coolant). The current Q-table and observed transition are given below. Apply one Q-Learning update step using $\alpha = 0.2$ and $\gamma = 0.8$.**

| State \ Action | Increase Coolant | Decrease Coolant |
|---|---|---|
| Low | 0.4 | 0.6 |
| Normal | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| High | 0.3 | 0.7 |

**Observed Transition:** Agent was in state **Normal**, took action **Increase Coolant**, received reward $r = +2$, transitioned to state **Low**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework. How does the Markov property simplify RL problem formulation in a chemical process?**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify relevant values from the Q-table (1 mark):**
- Current $Q(s_t, a_t) = Q(\text{Normal}, \text{IncreaseCoolant}) = 0.8$
- Next state $s_{t+1} = \text{Low}$
- Reward $r_{t+1} = +2$

**Step 2 — Compute the max over next-state actions (2 marks):**
$$\max_{a} Q(s_{t+1}, a) = \max(Q(\text{Low}, \text{IncreaseCoolant}), Q(\text{Low}, \text{DecreaseCoolant})) = \max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute TD-target (1 mark):**
$$\text{TD-Target} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) = 2 + 0.8 \times 0.6 = 2 + 0.48 = 2.48$$

**Step 4 — Compute TD-error (1 mark):**
$$\delta_t = 2.48 - 0.8 = 1.68$$

**Step 5 — Apply Q-Learning update (2 marks):**
$$Q(\text{Normal}, \text{IncreaseCoolant}) \leftarrow 0.8 + 0.2 \times 1.68 = 0.8 + 0.336 = 1.136$$

**Final Updated Q-value:** $Q(\text{Normal}, \text{IncreaseCoolant}) = \mathbf{1.136}$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The Q-value increased from 0.8 to 1.136, reflecting the positive reward of $+2$ for moving from Normal toward Low temperature, indicating that this action is currently estimated to be more valuable for future iterations.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Define MDP (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ providing the formal mathematical framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty.

- $\mathcal{S}$: Set of states (e.g., {Low, Normal, High} temperature).
- $\mathcal{A}$: Set of actions (e.g., {Increase, Decrease} coolant).
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: Transition probability — probability of moving to $s'$ given action $a$ in state $s$.
- $R(s, a, s')$: Reward function.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1]$: Discount factor.

**Step 2 — State the Markov Property (2 marks):**

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

The future depends only on the **present state and action**, not on the history of past states.

**Step 3 — Why this simplifies RL in chemical processes (2 marks):**
1. **Sufficient statistic:** The current state $s_t$ (containing T, P, C$_A$, etc.) is a sufficient summary of all past history — no need to store or process extensive historical sensor data.
2. **Recursive decomposition:** Enables the Bellman equations, making value functions tractable.
3. **Real-world satisfaction:** Many lumped-parameter chemical reactor models (e.g., CSTR with perfect mixing assumption) naturally satisfy the Markov property because their state variables fully describe system dynamics.
4. **Reduced dimensionality:** Avoids the curse of history — instead of conditioning on entire trajectories, we condition only on current state.

**Step 4 — Concluding remark (1 mark):**
The MDP framework provides the **mathematical scaffolding** upon which all RL algorithms — from tabular Q-Learning to Deep Q-Networks (DQN) — are built, making it the cornerstone of RL for chemical process control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $V$ and $Q$:** Many students mix up state-value $V^\pi(s)$ with action-value $Q^\pi(s,a)$. Remember: $V$ depends only on state; $Q$ depends on state-action pair.
> 2. **Forgetting the max operator:** In Q-Learning's update rule, the term is $\max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a)$, NOT $\sum_a Q(s_{t+1}, a)$. Mistaking this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Incorrect reward sign convention:** If reward is defined as a *cost*, ensure consistency with the sign convention throughout the derivation.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU examiners award step-marks. Always show the conditioning on $a$ and $s'$ explicitly when deriving Bellman equations.
> 5. **Ignoring the Markov Property:** Any question about RL formulation **must** mention the Markov Property — its omission is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Q-Table indexing errors:** When solving numerical updates, double-check that you are reading the **correct row and column** from the Q-table.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Reinforcement Learning (Module 2)**

- [x] **Definition**: RL = agent learns optimal policy via interaction to maximize cumulative reward.
- [x] **MDP**: 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ is the formal framework.
- [x] **Markov Property**: $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, \text{history}) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.
- [x] **Return**: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ — discounted cumulative reward.
- [x] **State-Value**: $V^\pi(s)$ = expected return from state $s$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Action-Value**: $Q^\pi(s, a)$ = expected return from $s$ taking action $a$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Bellman Expectation**: $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$.
- [x] **Bellman Optimality**: $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$.
- [x] **Q-Learning Update**: $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha[r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)]$.
- [x] **TD-Error**: $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t)$.
- [x] **$\varepsilon$-Greedy**: With prob. $\varepsilon$ explore (random), with prob. $1-\varepsilon$ exploit (greedy).
- [x] **Convergence**: Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ if all $(s,a)$ pairs are visited infinitely often and $\alpha$ decays appropriately.
- [x] **Optimal Policy**: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$.
- [x] **Chemical Engineering Use Cases**: CSTR temperature control, distillation reflux tuning, batch scheduling, catalyst selection, RTO.
- [x] **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$**: 0 = myopic, $\to 1$ = far-sighted.
- [x] **Exploration vs. Exploitation**: Fundamental tradeoff — explore to discover, exploit to earn.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: CLASSIFICATION PROBLEMS — FOUNDATIONAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** is a **supervised machine learning** paradigm in which the algorithm learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where the target variable $y_i$ belongs to a **discrete, unordered set of class labels** $\mathcal{Y} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$.

In the context of **Chemical Engineering**, classification problems are central to:
- **Process Fault Diagnosis** (normal vs. faulty operation)
- **Product Quality Grading** (Grade A, B, C of polymer batches)
- **Phase Identification** (vapor, liquid, two-phase)
- **Catalyst Deactivation State** (fresh, partially deactivated, fully deactivated)
- **Corrosion Severity Classification** (low, medium, high)

The model output is a **categorical label** (not a continuous number), distinguishing classification fundamentally from regression.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Quality Control Inspector at a Distillation Column**
>
> Imagine a chemical plant quality inspector standing at the end of a distillation column. Every minute, a sample of distillate comes out. The inspector looks at measurable properties: **refractive index, boiling point, density, color** — and based on these inputs, must decide: *"Is this batch Pharmaceutical Grade, Industrial Grade, or Reject?"*
>
> The inspector's brain is essentially a **classifier**. The features $\mathbf{x}$ are the measured properties; the output $y$ is the grade. A machine learning classifier learns the same decision rule, but from thousands of historical samples where the grade is already known (supervised learning).

## 1.3 Types of Classification Problems

| Type | Number of Classes | Example (Chemical Engg.) |
|------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Binary Classification** | $K = 2$ | Pass / Fail of pressure test |
| **Multiclass Classification** | $K \geq 3$ | Type of corrosion: uniform, pitting, crevice, stress |
| **Multilabel Classification** | Multiple labels per sample | Polymer exhibiting both crystallinity and crosslinking |
| **Imbalanced Classification** | Skewed class priors | Rare fault events (1% abnormal, 99% normal) |

## 1.4 Key Terminology — The KTU Vocabulary You MUST Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Class Label ($y$)**: The discrete output category.
> - **Feature Vector ($\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$)**: The $d$-dimensional input vector of process variables.
> - **Decision Boundary**: The hypersurface in feature space that separates different classes.
> - **Discriminative Model**: Learns $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ directly (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM).
> - **Generative Model**: Learns $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y)$ and $P(y)$, then uses Bayes' rule (e.g., Naive Bayes).
> - **Hard Classification**: Output is a single label $\hat{y} \in \mathcal{Y}$.
> - **Soft Classification**: Output is a probability vector $\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_K)$.

## 1.5 The Supervised Learning Pipeline (Chemical Engineering Context)

The standard KTU-recommended workflow is:

1. **Data Acquisition** — Collect historical process data (DCS, lab measurements).
2. **Preprocessing** — Handle missing values, normalize, encode categorical variables.
3. **Train/Test Split** — Typically 70/30 or 80/20, **stratified** for imbalanced data.
4. **Model Training** — Fit classifier on training set.
5. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — Cross-validation (e.g., 5-fold CV).
6. **Evaluation** — Confusion matrix, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC.
7. **Deployment** — Real-time inference on plant DCS/SCADA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mathematical Anatomy of Classification

Classification algorithms differ in **how they model the decision boundary**. We will examine the major families in increasing complexity.

---

### 2.1.1 Logistic Regression (The Workhorse of Linear Classification)

Despite its name, logistic regression is a **classification** algorithm. It models the **posterior probability** $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$ using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**.

**Core Model:**

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

where the **linear score** is:

$$z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j x_j + b$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Loss Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss):**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

where $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not MSE?** Using Mean Squared Error for classification creates a **non-convex** loss surface riddled with local minima. Cross-entropy is convex for logistic regression, guaranteeing convergence to the **global optimum**.

---

### 2.1.2 k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Classifier

A **non-parametric, instance-based** learner. For a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$, find the $k$ training samples closest to it (using Euclidean or Manhattan distance) and assign the **majority class**.

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \text{mode}\{y_i : \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)\}$$

Common distance metric:

$$d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{m=1}^{d}(x_{i,m} - x_{j,m})^2} \quad \text{(Euclidean)}$$

---

### 2.1.3 Naive Bayes Classifier

Based on **Bayes' Theorem** with the "naive" assumption of **conditional feature independence**:

$$P(y = C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(y = C_k) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

The predicted class is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(y = C_k) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = C_k)$$

For continuous features (e.g., temperature, pressure), use **Gaussian Naive Bayes**:

$$P(x_j \mid y = C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{j,k}^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_{j,k})^2}{2\sigma_{j,k}^2}\right)$$

---

### 2.1.4 Support Vector Machine (SVM) — Linear Case

Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

For non-linearly separable data, the **soft-margin** formulation introduces slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$$

The constant $C > 0$ controls the **bias-variance trade-off**.

---

### 2.1.5 Decision Tree Classifier

Recursively partitions the feature space using **axis-aligned splits**. At each node, the algorithm selects the feature $x_j$ and threshold $t$ that maximizes **Information Gain**:

$$\text{IG}(S, x_j) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(x_j)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S)$ is the **Shannon entropy** of set $S$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

Alternatively, splits can be chosen by minimizing **Gini impurity**:

$$G(S) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$$

---

## 2.2 The Confusion Matrix — The Heart of Classification Evaluation

For **binary classification**, predictions and ground truths form a $2 \times 2$ matrix:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| Metric | Formula | Chemical Engg. Interpretation |
|--------|---------|-------------------------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correct classification rate. **Misleading on imbalanced data** (e.g., 99% normal = 99% accuracy by predicting "normal" always). |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alarms raised, how many were real? Critical for false-alarm cost. |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all actual faults, how many did we catch? Critical for safety — missing a fault is costly. |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate. |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall. |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Used in ROC curve. |
| **ROC-AUC** | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})$ | Area under Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. **0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect**. |
| **Log Loss** | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i [y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)]$ | Penalizes confident wrong predictions. |
| **Cohen's Kappa** | $\frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Agreement corrected for chance. |
| **Matthews Corr. Coeff. (MCC)** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric even for imbalanced classes. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use Case — Tennessee Eastman Process (TEP) Fault Classification**
>
> The Tennessee Eastman Process benchmark provides 52 measured variables and 20 programmed faults. Classification algorithms are used to:
> - **Detect** which of the 20 faults is occurring (multiclass, 21 classes including normal).
> - Classify the **severity** of catalyst poisoning.
> - Distinguish sensor **drift vs. actual process fault** (a notoriously hard binary problem).
>
> SVMs with RBF kernels and Random Forests historically achieve **>95% accuracy** on TEP fault classification. This is a **direct, board-relevant application** the examiner loves to test.

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Sigmoid Function for Logistic Regression?

### Step 1 — Start with the Odds Ratio

For binary classification, define the **odds** of class 1:

$$\text{odds} = \frac{P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(y=0 \mid \mathbf{x})} = \frac{P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{1 - P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})}$$

### Step 2 — Take the Logarithm (Logit)

$$\text{logit}(p) = \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

This transformation maps $p \in (0, 1)$ to $\mathbb{R}$, allowing a linear model on the log-odds.

### Step 3 — Invert to Recover the Probability

Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p = (1-p) e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p + p \cdot e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b} = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p(1 + e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}) = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}}{1 + e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)}}$$

This is the **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: By assuming the **log-odds are linear** in the features, we are forcing a smooth, monotonic, bounded probability model. The sigmoid is the natural "inverse" of this assumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Estimate for Logistic Regression

### Step 1 — Likelihood Function

Given $N$ independent samples, the likelihood of observing the data under parameters $\mathbf{w}, b$ is:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{p}_i)^{(1-y_i)}$$

where $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$. This works because when $y_i = 1$ we get $\hat{p}_i$, and when $y_i = 0$ we get $(1 - \hat{p}_i)$.

### Step 2 — Log-Likelihood

Taking the natural log (monotonic, easier to optimize):

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \log L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Optimization Objective

We **maximize** the log-likelihood, equivalently **minimize** the negative log-likelihood:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Gradient (for gradient descent)

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{p}_i - y_i \right) x_{i,j}$$

This has a **beautiful form** identical in structure to the linear regression gradient — the prediction error $(\hat{p}_i - y_i)$ multiplied by the feature $x_{i,j}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The gradient descent update rule** is therefore:
> $$w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{p}_i - y_i) x_{i,j}$$
> where $\alpha$ is the learning rate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Confusion Matrix & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A classifier is deployed in a refinery to detect **compressor surge** from vibration spectra. Out of **200 test samples**, the results are:
> - Actual surge cases: 50 (Positive class)
> - Actual normal cases: 150 (Negative class)
> - Correctly identified surge: 42
> - Correctly identified normal: 138
> - False alarms: 12
> - Missed surges: 8

### Step 1 — Construct the Confusion Matrix

|  | Predicted Surge | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Surge** | TP = 42 | FN = 8 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 12 | TN = 138 |

**Verification:** $TP + FN + FP + TN = 42 + 8 + 12 + 138 = 200$ ✓

### Step 2 — Compute Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{42 + 138}{200} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

### Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{42}{42 + 12} = \frac{42}{54} = 0.778 = 77.8\%$$

### Step 4 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity)

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{42}{42 + 8} = \frac{42}{50} = 0.84 = 84\%$$

### Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.778 \cdot 0.84}{0.778 + 0.84} = \frac{1.307}{1.618} = 0.808 = 80.8\%$$

### Step 6 — Compute Specificity & FPR

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{138}{138 + 12} = \frac{138}{150} = 0.92 = 92\%$$

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 0.08 = 8\%$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Recall of 84%**: We **missed 16%** of surge events. In a real refinery, this is **unacceptable** for safety-critical faults.
> - **Precision of 77.8%**: About 1 in 4 alarms is a false alarm. Operations team will lose trust in the system (the "cry wolf" problem).
> - **Engineering Recommendation**: **Lower the decision threshold** from 0.5 to ~0.3 to boost recall, accepting a small drop in precision. This is the **precision-recall trade-off**, a favorite KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Decision Boundary — Derivation for Linear Classifier

For a linear classifier $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$, the **decision boundary** is the locus where the classifier is indifferent between classes:

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

In 2D ($d = 2$), this is a line:

$$w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0 \implies x_2 = -\frac{w_1}{w_2} x_1 - \frac{b}{w_2}$$

The **perpendicular distance** from a point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to this hyperplane is:

$$\text{dist}(\mathbf{x}_i, \text{boundary}) = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\|\mathbf{w}\|_2}$$

This is exactly what **SVMs maximize** — the margin is the distance from the boundary to the nearest data point.

---

## 3.5 The Bias-Variance Trade-off in Classification

> [!NOTE]
> | Regime | Symptom | Cause | Fix |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Underfitting (High Bias)** | Low training AND low test accuracy | Model too simple (e.g., linear boundary on spiral data) | Increase model complexity, add features |
> | **Overfitting (High Variance)** | High training, low test accuracy | Model memorizes noise | Regularization, more data, simpler model, dropout |
> | **Good Fit** | Training ≈ Test accuracy, both high | Optimal complexity | — |

For **logistic regression**, regularization adds a penalty term:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_i [y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)] + \lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|^2$$

where $\lambda$ is the **regularization strength** (L2 / Ridge) and $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 = \sum_j w_j^2$.
