|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid activation | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}$, output $\in (0,1)$ | Binary fault/normal decision |
| 2 | Hyperbolic-tan activation | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | output $\in (-1,1)$ | Faster convergence than sigmoid |
| 3 | ReLU activation | $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0,z)$ | output $\in [0,\infty)$ | Mitigates vanishing gradient |
| 4 | Softmax (multi-class) | $\sigma(z_j) = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$ | probabilities sum to 1 | Multi-fault classification |
| 5 | Mean-Squared Error loss | $E = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $N$ = samples | Regression / AE reconstruction |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik}\log(\hat{y}_{ik})$ | $K$ = fault classes | Classification objective |
| 7 | Backprop weight update | $w_{ij}^{(t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(t)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate | Training rule |
| 8 | Generalised Delta Rule | $\Delta w_{ij} = -\eta \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} + \mu \Delta w_{ij}^{(t-1)}$ | $\mu$ = momentum | Accelerates convergence |
| 9 | PCA projection | $Z = X W_{\text{PCA}}$ | $W_{\text{PCA}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$, $p \ll n$ | Feature reduction |
| 10 | Hotelling's $T^{2}$ statistic | $T^{2} = x^{\top} P^{-1} \lambda^{-1} P^{\top} x$ | $P, \lambda$ = eigenvectors/eigenvalues of $\Sigma$ | Fault detectability index |
| 11 | SPE / Q-statistic | $\text{SPE} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert^{2}$ | residual subspace norm | Fault detection in PCA-MSPC |
| 12 | Detection Delay | $t_{d} = t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{fault onset}}$ | time steps or seconds | Practical metric |
| 13 | False Alarm Rate | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | dimensionless | Reliability metric |
| 14 | Missed-Detection Rate | $\text{MDR} = \dfrac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | dimensionless | Safety-critical metric |
| 15 | Reconstruction Error (AE) | $E_{\text{rec}} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert^{2}$ | Euclidean norm | Anomaly score |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation safeguard:** All absolute-value or norm symbols inside tables are written using $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ to avoid syntax collisions with the markdown table pipe character.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world impact:** ANN-based FDD is deployed in *Tennessee Eastman Process benchmark* (Downs & Vogel, **1993**), a standard testbed in chemical engineering FDD research with 52 variables and 20 pre-defined fault classes. Mastery of the TE benchmark is considered essential for any chemical-engineering AI/ML practitioner and is frequently cited in KTU project reports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Backpropagation Weight Update Rule for an MLP

Consider a feed-forward MLP with one hidden layer. Let:

- Input vector: $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^{\top}$
- Hidden-layer pre-activation: $h_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j^{(1)}$
- Hidden-layer activation: $a_j = \sigma(h_j)$, where $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
- Output-layer pre-activation: $o_k = \sum_{j=1}^{H} w_{jk}^{(2)} a_j + b_k^{(2)}$
- Final output: $\hat{y}_k = \sigma(o_k)$
- Loss function for a single sample: $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(y_k - \hat{y}_k)^{2}$

**Step 1 — Output-layer gradient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} &= \frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{y}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial o_k} \cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} \\
&= -(y_k - \hat{y}_k) \cdot \sigma'(o_k) \cdot a_j \\
&= -\delta_k^{(2)} \cdot a_j
\end{aligned}$$

Define $\delta_k^{(2)} = (y_k - \hat{y}_k) \sigma'(o_k)$. This is the *error signal* at output neuron $k$.

**Step 2 — Hidden-layer gradient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{y}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial o_k} \cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial a_j} \cdot \frac{\partial a_j}{\partial h_j} \cdot \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} \\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \delta_k^{(2)} \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)} \cdot \sigma'(h_j) \cdot x_i \\
&= -\delta_j^{(1)} \cdot x_i
\end{aligned}$$

Define $\delta_j^{(1)} = \sigma'(h_j) \sum_{k=1}^{K} \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$. The hidden-layer error is a *back-projected* version of the output error.

**Step 3 — Weight update via Gradient Descent:**

$$w_{ij}^{(1,\,t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(1,\,t)} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = w_{ij}^{(1,\,t)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)} \, x_i$$

$$w_{jk}^{(2,\,t+1)} = w_{jk}^{(2,\,t)} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}^{(2)}} = w_{jk}^{(2,\,t)} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(2)} \, a_j$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the *learning rate*, typically in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.

**Interpretation:** The output error is *propagated backwards* through the network, with each hidden neuron's responsibility $\delta_j^{(1)}$ being a weighted sum of downstream errors, scaled by the local activation derivative. This is the essence of the **generalised delta rule** that makes multi-layer learning tractable.

### 3.2 Derivation: Autoencoder Threshold from Reconstruction Error

Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ be a normal-operating sample, $g(\cdot)$ the encoder, $h(\cdot)$ the decoder, so $\hat{x} = h(g(x))$. The squared reconstruction error is:

$$e(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^{2}$$

Assuming normal data is approximately Gaussian in reconstruction-error space with mean $\mu_e$ and variance $\sigma_e^{2}$, the Mahalanobis-based anomaly threshold at significance level $\alpha$ is:

$$\tau = \mu_e + z_{1-\alpha} \, \sigma_e$$

For $\alpha = 0.01$, $z_{0.99} \approx 2.33$, meaning any sample with $e(x) > \tau$ is flagged as a fault with **99% confidence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance:** Setting $\tau$ too low yields *false alarms* (operator loses trust); too high yields *missed detections* (safety compromised). The KTU examiner often asks students to justify this trade-off using an **ROC curve** argument.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of ANN Architecture vs Fault Type

| Fault Nature | Data Labelling | Dimensionality | Time Dependency | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Known sensor bias | Labelled | Low (5–10 vars) | Static | MLP (1–2 hidden layers) | Simple classification suffices |
| Heat-exchanger fouling | Unlabelled | Medium | Slow drift | Autoencoder | Learns normality, flags deviation |
| Reactor runaway | Labelled | High (20+ vars) | Dynamic | LSTM / GRU | Captures temporal causality |
| Multiple valve sticking | Labelled | Medium | Mixed | CNN-1D on sliding windows | Detects local motifs in sensor streams |
| Novel unknown fault | Unlabelled | High | — | SOM + Autoencoder hybrid | Clusters unseen patterns for human review |

### 3.4 Confusion Matrix and Derived Metrics — Worked Framework

For a binary fault/normal classifier, the four outcomes are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
\text{Recall} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
F_{1} &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In *safety-critical* chemical processes, **recall is preferred over accuracy** — missing a real fault is far costlier than raising a false alarm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ANN-FDD Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant Sensors] --> B[Data Acquisition Layer DCS SCADA]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Noise Filtering Normalisation]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering PCA ICA Selection]
    D --> E[ANN Architecture Selection]
    E --> F1[MLP for Labelled Multi Class]
    E --> F2[Autoencoder for Anomaly Detection]
    E --> F3[LSTM for Dynamic Time Series]
    E --> F4[SOM for Unsupervised Clustering]
    F1 --> G[Decision Module Thresholding Softmax]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Fault Class Output Severity Confidence]
    H --> I[Alarm and Operator Interface]
    H --> J[Safety Interlock Logic]
    I --> K[Operator Action or Maintenance]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Detailed MLP Architecture for Multi-Class Fault Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input Variable T] --> H1
    X2[Input Variable P] --> H1
    X3[Input Variable F] --> H1
    X4[Input Variable X] --> H1
    X5[Input Variable L] --> H1
    H1[H1 Neuron Hidden Layer 1] --> H2A
    H1 --> H2B
    H1 --> H2C
    H1 --> H2D
    H1 --> H2E
    H2A[H2 Layer 2 Neuron A] --> O1
    H2B[H2 Layer 2 Neuron B] --> O1
    H2C[H2 Layer 2 Neuron C] --> O2
    H2D[H2 Layer 2 Neuron D] --> O3
    H2E[H2 Layer 2 Neuron E] --> O3
    O1[Output Fault Class 1 Sensor Bias] --> SOFT[Softmax Normalisation]
    O2[Output Fault Class 2 Valve Sticking] --> SOFT
    O3[Output Fault Class 3 Heat Exchanger Fouling] --> SOFT
    SOFT --> DEC[Decision Maximum Probability]
    DEC --> ALARM[Alarm and Diagnostic Report]
```

### 4.3 Autoencoder-based Anomaly Detection Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Sample x in R n] --> ENC[Encoder Compress]
    ENC --> LAT[Latent Code z in R p where p is much smaller than n]
    LAT --> DEC[Decoder Reconstruct]
    DEC --> XHAT[Reconstruction x hat]
    P --> SUB[Compare]
    XHAT --> SUB
    SUB --> ERR[Reconstruction Error e]
    ERR --> TH[Compare with Threshold Tau]
    TH --> NORM[Label Normal]
    TH --> FLT[Label Fault]
```

### 4.4 Training vs Deployment Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHASE1[Training Phase Offline]
        H1[Historical Process Database] --> P1[Cleaning and Labelling]
        P1 --> N1[Network Initialisation]
        N1 --> T1[Forward Pass]
        T1 --> L1[Compute Loss Function]
        L1 --> BP[Backpropagation Gradient Update]
        BP --> T1
        L1 --> V1[Validation on Holdout Set]
        V1 --> SAV[Save Trained Weights]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2[Deployment Phase Online Real Time]
        SAV --> LD[Load Weights into Inference Engine]
        LD --> ST[Stream Live Sensor Data]
        ST --> INF[Forward Pass Inference]
        INF --> DEC2[Threshold Check]
        DEC2 --> AL2[Alarm or Normal Tag]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` style is replaced with descriptive uppercase names like `H1`, `P1`, `T1`) to ensure flawless Mermaid rendering across all markdown viewers. Labels are kept plain-text without any markdown formatting characters to avoid syntax conflicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
*Define "fault detection" and "fault diagnosis" in the context of chemical process monitoring. Differentiate between the two with a suitable example from a distillation column.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fault Detection** is the binary decision of whether the process is operating in a normal condition or whether an abnormal event has occurred. It answers the question *"Has something gone wrong?"*

**Fault Diagnosis** goes one step further and identifies the *type, location, and magnitude* of the fault. It answers the question *"What went wrong, where, and how severely?"*

**Example from a distillation column:** Suppose the reflux-flow transmitter begins to drift (sensor fault). The detection system raises a flag when the inferred mass balance deviates beyond the threshold (detection). The diagnostic system then identifies the specific drift as originating from the *reflow flow transmitter* rather than from a real hydraulic issue, by correlating it with reboiler duty and tray-temperature trends (diagnosis).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
*List any three activation functions used in artificial neural networks for FDD. State one advantage of ReLU over the sigmoid function for deep fault classifiers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three commonly used activation functions are:
1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, output range $(0,1)$.
2. **Hyperbolic Tangent (tanh):** $\tanh(z)$, output range $(-1,1)$.
3. **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU):** $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0,z)$, output range $[0,\infty)$.

**Advantage of ReLU over sigmoid:** ReLU mitigates the *vanishing-gradient problem* because its gradient is exactly 1 for all positive inputs. Sigmoid, in contrast, saturates at large absolute values where its derivative approaches zero, causing gradients in deep networks to shrink exponentially and stalling backpropagation. This makes ReLU especially effective in deep fault classifiers with many hidden layers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B carries internal choice. Students must attempt *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 marks] *Explain with a neat schematic the architecture of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) used for multi-class fault classification in a chemical process. Discuss the role of each layer and the softmax output activation.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *Derive the backpropagation weight-update rule for the hidden layer of a two-layer MLP using the sigmoid activation and mean-squared-error loss. Clearly state the chain-rule steps.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — MLP Architecture:**

An MLP consists of three distinct layer types connected in a feed-forward cascade:

1. **Input Layer:** Holds the $n$ process variables (e.g., temperatures $T$, pressures $P$, flows $F$, compositions $X$, levels $L$). No computation occurs here; the input layer simply distributes signals.

2. **Hidden Layer(s):** Each neuron computes a weighted sum of all inputs, adds a bias, and passes through a non-linear activation (e.g., sigmoid or ReLU). Hidden neurons act as *feature detectors* — first-layer neurons may detect simple univariate excursions, while deeper neurons combine these into complex multivariate patterns.

3. **Output Layer:** For $K$ fault classes, the output layer has $K$ neurons. The **softmax** activation converts the raw scores $o_k$ into probabilities:

$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{o_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{o_j}}, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_k = 1$$

The predicted fault class is $\arg\max_k \hat{y}_k$. Refer to the **MLP schematic in Section 4.2** for a visual representation.

**Mark distribution (a):** Architecture diagram + role of layers (3 marks), softmax derivation (2 marks), example with $K=3$ fault classes (2 marks).

**Part (b) — Derivation of Hidden-Layer Update:**

Starting from the loss $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(y_k - \hat{y}_k)^{2}$ with $\hat{y}_k = \sigma(o_k)$ and $o_k = \sum_{j} w_{jk}^{(2)} a_j + b_k$, and $a_j = \sigma(h_j)$ with $h_j = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(1)} x_i + b_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{\partial E}{\partial \hat{y}_k}\cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial o_k}\cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial a_j}\cdot \frac{\partial a_j}{\partial h_j}\cdot \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{K}-(y_k - \hat{y}_k)\sigma'(o_k) \cdot w_{jk}^{(2)} \cdot \sigma'(h_j)\cdot x_i \\
&= -\sigma'(h_j) \cdot x_i \sum_{k=1}^{K} \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)} \\
&= -\delta_j^{(1)} x_i
\end{aligned}$$

where $\delta_k^{(2)} = (y_k - \hat{y}_k)\sigma'(o_k)$ and $\delta_j^{(1)} = \sigma'(h_j) \sum_k \delta_k^{(2)} w_{jk}^{(2)}$.

The gradient-descent update rule becomes:

$$w_{ij}^{(1,\,t+1)} = w_{ij}^{(1,\,t)} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(1)} x_i$$

**Interpretation:** The error signal at the output $\delta_k^{(2)}$ is propagated *backwards* through the second-layer weights $w_{jk}^{(2)}$ into the hidden neurons, scaled by the local derivative $\sigma'(h_j)$. This back-projection mechanism is what allows deep networks to learn hierarchical fault representations.

**Mark distribution (b):** Correct definition of error signals (2 marks), chain-rule application (3 marks), final update expression with learning rate (2 marks).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 marks] *Describe in detail the autoencoder-based approach for fault detection in chemical processes. Explain how the reconstruction error threshold is set.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *For a CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) exhibiting three operating modes — Normal, Catalyst Poisoning, and Cooling-water Failure — design an ANN-based monitoring system. Specify the input variables, the architecture, and the metric used to declare a fault.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Autoencoder-based Fault Detection:**

An autoencoder is a symmetric neural network trained to reconstruct its own input. It has three parts:

1. **Encoder:** Maps $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to a low-dimensional latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ with $p \ll n$.
2. **Bottleneck (latent space):** Forces the network to learn only the most essential features of the input distribution.
3. **Decoder:** Reconstructs $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ from $z$.

**Training:** The network is trained on *normal-operation* data only by minimising the reconstruction loss $E_{\text{rec}} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert^{2}$. After training, the autoencoder becomes an *expert in normality*.

**Inference:** For a new sample $x^{*}$, compute $e(x^{*}) = \lVert x^{*} - \hat{x}^{*} \rVert^{2}$.

**Threshold Setting:**

$$\tau = \mu_e + z_{1-\alpha}\sigma_e$$

where $\mu_e$ and $\sigma_e$ are the mean and standard deviation of reconstruction errors computed on a *validation set of known-normal samples*. The constant $z_{1-\alpha}$ is the standard-normal quantile (e.g., $z_{0.99} \approx 2.33$ for $\alpha = 0.01$).

**Decision rule:**
$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Normal} & \text{if } e(x^{*}) \leq \tau \\ \text{Fault} & \text{if } e(x^{*}) > \tau \end{cases}$$

**Advantage:** This approach can detect *novel* faults that were never seen during training — a property that supervised MLP classifiers cannot offer.

**Mark distribution (a):** Autoencoder structure (2 marks), loss function and training strategy (2 marks), threshold derivation and decision rule (3 marks).

**Part (b) — CSTR Monitoring System Design:**

**Input Variables (n = 6):**
- $T$ — Reactor temperature
- $T_c$ — Cooling-water outlet temperature
- $C_A$ — Reactant concentration
- $F$ — Feed flow rate
- $F_c$ — Cooling-water flow rate
- $C_{Ao}$ — Inlet reactant concentration

**Architecture:**
- **Type:** Multi-class classification MLP (since the three classes are *known* and *labelled*).
- **Hidden layers:** Two layers with 12 and 8 neurons respectively.
- **Activation:** ReLU in hidden layers, Softmax in the output layer.
- **Output vector:** $(p_{\text{Normal}}, p_{\text{Cat.\,Pois.}}, p_{\text{Cool.\,Fail.}})$, summing to 1.

**Training Strategy:**
- Collect 1,000 samples per class (balanced dataset).
- Apply 3-$\sigma$ outlier removal and min-max normalisation.
- Use **80 : 10 : 10** train-validation-test split.
- Optimiser: Adam, learning rate $\eta = 10^{-3}$.
- Loss: Categorical cross-entropy.

**Fault Declaration Metric:** A fault is declared when:

$$\max_{k \in \{1,2,3\}} \hat{y}_k \geq \tau_{\text{conf}}$$

with $\tau_{\text{conf}} = 0.85$, *and* the cumulative deviation in the moving average of $T$ exceeds 2 °C. The first condition ensures *classification confidence*; the second enforces *physical plausibility*, reducing false alarms.

**Performance Evaluation:** Use the $F_1$ score and **missed-detection rate (MDR)** — the latter is critical because cooling-water failure can lead to thermal runaway if undetected.

**Mark distribution (b):** Choice and justification of inputs (2 marks), architecture specification (2 marks), threshold and metric definition (3 marks).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (avoid these at all costs):**
>
> 1. **Conflating detection with diagnosis.** Detection is binary; diagnosis is multi-class identification. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term.** When drawing the MLP architecture, every neuron must show an incoming bias connection — omission is treated as incomplete by KTU evaluators.
> 3. **Writing ReLU as differentiable everywhere.** ReLU is *not* differentiable at zero. Examiners mark this strictly; mention sub-gradient $\partial \text{ReLU}/\partial z = \mathbb{1}_{z>0}$ if asked.
> 4. **Skipping the threshold-setting step.** In any autoencoder or anomaly-detection answer, omitting how $\tau$ is chosen results in a 2-mark deduction even if everything else is correct.
> 5. **Confusing FAR with MDR.** False-Alarm Rate = FP/(FP+TN); Missed-Detection Rate = FN/(FN+TP). These are *not* interchangeable and confusing them shows lack of clarity.
> 6. **Not normalising input data.** ANN training on raw, unscaled process variables (e.g., temperature in K vs. flow in kg/h) leads to ill-conditioned gradients — the examiner expects explicit normalisation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FDD has two stages:** *Detection* (binary: normal vs abnormal) and *Diagnosis* (multi-class: which fault, where, how severe).
- **Four fault families in chemical plants:** sensor, actuator, process, and disturbance faults.
- **The MLP is the workhorse** of supervised fault classification; the **autoencoder is the workhorse** of unsupervised anomaly detection.
- **Softmax is mandatory** for multi-class outputs because it produces a valid probability distribution summing to 1.
- **Backpropagation = chain rule applied layer by layer**, with error signals $\delta$ propagated from output to input.
- **ReLU is preferred over sigmoid in deep networks** because it avoids vanishing gradients.
- **Data preprocessing is non-negotiable:** normalise (zero-mean unit-variance *or* min-max) and remove outliers before training.
- **Threshold setting ($\tau$):** For autoencoders, use $\tau = \mu_e + z_{1-\alpha}\sigma_e$; for MLP confidence, use $\tau_{\text{conf}} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$.
- **Safety-critical processes prioritise recall / low MDR over accuracy** — a missed detection of a runaway reaction can be catastrophic.
- **The Tennessee Eastman Process benchmark** is the standard reference dataset for evaluating FDD algorithms in academic literature.
- **Time-dependent processes** (reactors, batch processes) benefit from recurrent architectures (LSTM, GRU); static steady-state processes (heat exchangers, distillation at fixed point) work well with MLPs.
- **Cross-entropy loss is preferred over MSE** for classification tasks because it produces stronger gradients near the decision boundary.
- **Momentum term $\mu$** in the generalised delta rule helps accelerate convergence and escape shallow local minima.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989):** A single hidden layer with a sufficient number of neurons can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary accuracy — but deeper networks generalise better in practice.
- **Always validate on unseen data** using k-fold cross-validation (commonly $k=5$ or $k=10$) to avoid overfitting to the training set.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Modular Approach to Multiple Faults Diagnosis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** A Modular Approach to Multiple Faults Diagnosis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modular Approach to Multiple Faults Diagnosis (MAMFD)** is a hierarchical, decompositional strategy used in process systems engineering where a large, complex chemical plant is partitioned into smaller, semantically meaningful *sub-systems* (modules), each equipped with its own localized diagnostic engine. Symptoms observed in each module are first analyzed independently to identify *candidate faults* at the unit level, and the results are then propagated upward to a *global coordinator* that resolves cross-module interactions to pinpoint the true root cause(s) when **two or more simultaneous (concurrent) faults** are present.

Mathematically, the modular approach converts a single, high-dimensional fault–symptom mapping problem into a set of low-dimensional, sparsely connected sub-problems of the form:

$$
\mathcal{S}_i \;=\; \mathcal{A}_i \cdot \mathcal{F}_i \quad (i=1,2,\dots,M)
$$

where $M$ is the number of modules, $\mathcal{S}_i$ is the local symptom vector, $\mathcal{A}_i$ is the local symptom–fault incidence matrix, and $\mathcal{F}_i$ is the local fault vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The modular paradigm is the *de-facto industry standard* for online fault diagnosis in refineries, petrochemical complexes, and large-scale continuous processes because it overcomes the **curse of dimensionality** inherent in classical single-level expert systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Specialist Model"

Imagine a patient visiting a **multi-specialty hospital**:

- The patient (the *plant*) walks in complaining of multiple symptoms — fever, chest pain, and blurred vision.
- Instead of one General Physician trying to diagnose everything, the patient is routed to a **Cardiologist**, an **Ophthalmologist**, and an **Infection Specialist** simultaneously (the *modules*).
- Each specialist independently examines the symptoms relevant to their domain and writes down possible diagnoses (the *local fault hypotheses*).
- A **Senior Consultant** (the *global coordinator*) then reviews the notes from all three specialists. If two specialists independently point to **diabetes** as a root cause that explains symptoms in multiple organs, the consultant concludes that this single root disease is the unifying explanation.
- If the specialists' notes *contradict* each other, the consultant re-evaluates and may order additional tests (the *conflict resolution* step).

This is *exactly* how a Modular Multiple-Fault Diagnosis system works: **decompose → diagnose locally → fuse globally → resolve conflicts**.

## 1.3 Why a Modular Architecture is Necessary for *Multiple* Faults

A single monolithic diagnostic engine faces three serious problems when more than one fault occurs at the same time:

1. **Combinatorial Explosion** — the search space of fault combinations grows as $2^F - 1$ (excluding the no-fault state) for $F$ candidate faults.
2. **Symptom Masking** — one fault's symptom may cancel or override another fault's signature.
3. **Knowledge-Base Maintenance** — updating rules for a 10,000-sensor plant in one file is impractical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics in Modular Fault Diagnosis (Board-Favorite Terms):**
> - **Sensitivity (Se)** = TP / (TP + FN) — ability to detect true faults.
> - **Specificity (Sp)** = TN / (TN + FP) — ability to reject false alarms.
> - **Fault Detection Latency (FDL)** — time delay between fault occurrence and detection, typically measured in **seconds** or **minutes**.
> - **Fault Isolation Accuracy (FIA)** — percentage of correctly isolated fault origins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer Modular Architecture

The standard modular fault-diagnosis hierarchy used in KTU-aligned curricula has **four distinct layers**:

| Layer | Name | Function | Typical AI Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Sensor Validation Layer | Detects faulty sensors, drift, bias | Auto-Associative Neural Networks, PCA |
| **L2** | Module-Level Diagnosis | Identifies faults inside one unit (e.g., a single reactor) | Signed Directed Graph (SDG), Fuzzy Logic, BPN |
| **L3** | Plant-Wide Coordination | Resolves cross-module interactions, isolates multiple faults | Rule-based Expert System, Bayesian Network |
| **L4** | Process-Impact Layer | Evaluates economic/safety/quality impact | Case-Based Reasoning, Cost models |

Each layer only communicates **summarized, validated information** to the layer above it, dramatically reducing data traffic and computational load.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning Chain

1. **Acquire measurements** $\mathbf{x}(t) = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]^T$ from all sensors at sampling instant $t$.
2. **Layer 1 (Sensor Validation):** Reconstruct each $x_i$ from the others using an auto-associative model. If the residual $r_i = x_i - \hat{x}_i$ exceeds threshold $\tau_i$, the sensor is flagged.
3. **Partition the validated measurements** into $M$ disjoint module sets $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, $i = 1, \dots, M$.
4. **Layer 2 (Local Diagnosis):** For each module, compute the local symptom vector $\mathbf{s}^{(i)} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}^{(i)}_{\text{nominal}}$ and compare against the local fault-signature matrix $\mathbf{A}^{(i)}$.
5. **Generate local fault candidates** with associated confidence factors (CF) in $[0, 1]$.
6. **Layer 3 (Global Coordination):** Aggregate candidates from all modules, look for *causal chains* and *common root causes* that explain symptoms across modules.
7. **Layer 4 (Impact Assessment):** Quantify severity, recommend corrective action.
8. **Report the final fault set** $\mathcal{F}^* = \{f_a, f_b, \dots\}$ with attached confidence levels.

## 2.3 Mathematical Backbone — Symptom–Fault Relationships

The fundamental diagnostic equation linking symptoms to faults in a module is:

$$
\mathbf{s}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{A}^{(i)} \cdot \mathbf{f}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{n}(t)
$$

where:

- $\mathbf{s}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the symptom vector in module $i$,
- $\mathbf{A}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times q}$ is the *fault signature matrix* (each column is the symptom pattern produced by one fault when isolated),
- $\mathbf{f}^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}^{q}$ is the binary fault-occurrence vector,
- $\mathbf{n}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the measurement-noise vector assumed Gaussian: $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma})$.

For **multiple simultaneous faults**, the *combined* symptom pattern is the **linear superposition** of individual fault signatures (a property used by the SDG and Observer-Based methods).

The **likelihood** of a fault hypothesis $\mathcal{H}_k$ given observations $\mathbf{s}$ is obtained via Bayes' rule:

$$
P(\mathcal{H}_k \mid \mathbf{s}) \;=\; \frac{P(\mathbf{s} \mid \mathcal{H}_k)\, P(\mathcal{H}_k)}{P(\mathbf{s})}
$$

For a candidate fault set $\mathcal{F} = \{f_{k_1}, f_{k_2}, \dots\}$, the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimate is:

$$
\mathcal{F}^* \;=\; \arg\max_{\mathcal{F}} \; P(\mathcal{F} \mid \mathbf{s})
$$

## 2.4 Symptom Generation — Residual-Based Approach

Each measurement $x_j$ has a predicted value $\hat{x}_j$ obtained from a process model or estimator. The residual is:

$$
r_j(t) \;=\; x_j(t) - \hat{x}_j(t)
$$

A symptom is *activated* when a residual exceeds its statistical bound. The **binary symptom indicator** is:

$$
s_j(t) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \vert r_j(t) \vert \;>\; k\,\sigma_j \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $k$ is a sensitivity factor (commonly $k = 3$ for the **3-sigma rule**, giving a false-alarm rate of approximately **0.27 %**).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symptom generation (binary) | $s_j = \mathbb{1}\big[\vert r_j \vert > k\sigma_j\big]$ | dimensionless indicator |
| 2 | Diagnostic equation | $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} + \mathbf{n}$ | $\mathbf{A}$ has units linking $\mathbf{s}$ and $\mathbf{f}$ |
| 3 | Residual | $r_j = x_j - \hat{x}_j$ | same units as $x_j$ |
| 4 | Bayes posterior | $P(\mathcal{H}_k \mid \mathbf{s}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{s} \mid \mathcal{H}_k)P(\mathcal{H}_k)}{P(\mathbf{s})}$ | dimensionless probability |
| 5 | MAP estimate | $\mathcal{F}^* = \arg\max_{\mathcal{F}} P(\mathcal{F}\mid\mathbf{s})$ | discrete fault set |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | dimensionless, range $[0,1]$ |
| 7 | Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | dimensionless, range $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | Confidence Factor (CF) | $CF = MB - MD$ | $MB$: measure of belief, $MD$: measure of disbelief |
| 9 | Root-mean-square residual | $RMSR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} r_j^2}$ | same units as measurement |
| 10 | Modular complexity reduction | $C_{mod} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} 2^{F_i}$ vs. $C_{mono} = 2^{\sum F_i}$ | exponential savings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The modular approach is the diagnostic engine behind commercial packages such as **Honeywell's FaultDoctor™, ABB's Process Diagnosis Tool, and Emerson's DeltaV Diagnose**. It is also the architecture used in the **Tennessee Eastman Challenge Process** benchmark studies, a gold-standard test bed published in *Computers & Chemical Engineering*.

## 2.6 The Core *Why* and *How* of Each Step

- **Why decompose?** Because $2^{F}$ combinations for $F$ candidate faults become $\sum 2^{F_i}$ across modules, and for a typical $F = 30$ split into six 5-fault modules, this is $6 \times 32 = 192$ vs. $2^{30} \approx 10^9$.
- **How to decompose?** Along natural process boundaries — *mass balance envelopes*, *control loops*, *utility streams*.
- **How to coordinate?** Either **top-down** (global sends context, modules refine) or **bottom-up** (modules report candidates, global fuses) — KTU syllabus emphasizes the latter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Residual to Symptom-Fault Mapping

We start from the linearized state-space model of a single module $i$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{E}^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{G}^{(i)} \mathbf{u}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{B}^{(i)} \mathbf{f}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{w}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{C}^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{v}(t)
$$

where $\mathbf{f}^{(i)}$ is the fault input matrix, $\mathbf{w}$ is process noise, and $\mathbf{v}$ is measurement noise.

**Step 1 — Design a Luenberger Observer (state estimator):**

$$
\dot{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{E}^{(i)} \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{G}^{(i)} \mathbf{u}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\big[\mathbf{y}^{(i)}(t) - \mathbf{C}^{(i)} \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)}(t)\big]
$$

**Step 2 — Define the state-estimation error $\mathbf{e}^{(i)} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)}$:**

Subtracting the observer equation from the plant equation:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{e}}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \big(\mathbf{E}^{(i)} - \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{C}^{(i)}\big)\mathbf{e}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{B}^{(i)}\mathbf{f}^{(i)}(t) \;+\; \mathbf{w}(t) - \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{v}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the residual (output estimation error):**

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{y}^{(i)}(t) - \mathbf{C}^{(i)} \hat{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)}(t) \;=\; \mathbf{C}^{(i)}\mathbf{e}^{(i)}(t) + \mathbf{v}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions):**

$$
\mathbf{R}^{(i)}(s) \;=\; \mathbf{C}^{(i)}\big[s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{E}^{(i)} + \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{C}^{(i)}\big]^{-1}\mathbf{B}^{(i)} \mathbf{F}^{(i)}(s) \;+\; \text{noise terms}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state / low-frequency approximation (Markov parameter):**

At low frequencies, $[s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{E}^{(i)} + \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{C}^{(i)}]^{-1} \approx -(\mathbf{E}^{(i)} - \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{C}^{(i)})^{-1}$, giving:

$$
\mathbf{R}^{(i)} \;\approx\; -\mathbf{C}^{(i)}(\mathbf{E}^{(i)} - \mathbf{L}^{(i)}\mathbf{C}^{(i)})^{-1}\mathbf{B}^{(i)} \mathbf{F}^{(i)} \;=\; \mathbf{A}^{(i)}\mathbf{F}^{(i)}
$$

This is precisely the **fault signature matrix** $\mathbf{A}^{(i)}$ used in the modular diagnostic equation.

**Step 6 — Establish the linear-mapping property for multiple faults:**

By linearity of the Luenberger observer, if two faults $\mathbf{f}_a$ and $\mathbf{f}_b$ occur simultaneously, the residual becomes:

$$
\mathbf{r} \;=\; \mathbf{A}_a \mathbf{f}_a \;+\; \mathbf{A}_b \mathbf{f}_b
$$

This **superposition principle** is the mathematical heart of the modular approach for *multiple* faults.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bayesian Resolution of Multiple Concurrent Faults

Consider a module with $q$ candidate faults. Let the binary vector $\mathbf{f} = [f_1, f_2, \dots, f_q]^T$ denote their presence/absence. Define the joint prior:

$$
P(\mathbf{f}) \;=\; \prod_{j=1}^{q} \pi_j^{f_j}\,(1 - \pi_j)^{1 - f_j}
$$

where $\pi_j$ is the prior probability that fault $f_j$ occurs. The likelihood of symptom vector $\mathbf{s}$ is:

$$
P(\mathbf{s} \mid \mathbf{f}) \;=\; \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2}\vert\mathbf{\Sigma}\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f})^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f})\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Posterior via Bayes' rule:**

$$
P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{s}) \;=\; \frac{P(\mathbf{s} \mid \mathbf{f})\,P(\mathbf{f})}{P(\mathbf{s})}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-posterior (dropping the $\mathbf{s}$-only denominator):**

$$
\ln P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{s}) \;\propto\; -\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f})^{T}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f}) \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{q} f_j \ln\!\frac{\pi_j}{1-\pi_j}
$$

**Step 3 — MAP solution by exhaustive search over $2^q$ combinations:**

For small $q$ (e.g., $q \le 20$), enumerate all $\mathbf{f} \in \{0,1\}^q$ and pick:

$$
\mathbf{f}^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{\mathbf{f} \in \{0,1\}^{q}} \; P(\mathbf{f} \mid \mathbf{s})
$$

**Step 4 — Exploit modularity to reduce search:**

In the modular framework, each module $i$ has $q_i \ll q$ candidate faults, so total evaluations drop from $2^q$ to $\sum_{i=1}^{M} 2^{q_i}$. For example, $q = 30$ split into six modules of 5 faults each: $2^{30} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{9}$ vs. $6 \times 2^{5} = 192$ — a **~5.6-million-fold reduction**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Multiple-Fault Scenarios in Chemical Plants

| Scenario | Concurrent Faults | Symptom Pattern | Modular Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat-Exchanger Fouling + Sensor Drift** | $f_1$ (HX) and $f_2$ (sensor) | Temperature deviates *and* pressure swings | Validate sensors at L1 first, then diagnose HX at L2 |
| **Reactor Catalyst Poisoning + Cooler Failure** | $f_3$ (catalyst) and $f_4$ (cooler) | Conversion drops *and* temperature rises | Reactor module + Cooler module report → Coordinator fuses |
| **Pump Cavitation + Pipe Leak** | $f_5$ and $f_6$ | Flow drops, vibration spikes | Hydraulic module isolates cavitation, then leak-detection submodule |
| **Controller Tuning Error + Valve Sticking** | $f_7$ and $f_8$ | Oscillatory behavior with bias | Control-loop module + Actuator module diagnose in parallel |
| **Feed Composition Disturbance + Reflux Loss** | $f_9$ and $f_{10}$ | Top-product purity drops, tray ΔT shifts | Column module + Feed module cross-check composition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Illustration (typical KTU numerical):** A CSTR with $q = 4$ candidate faults has $2^4 - 1 = 15$ non-trivial fault combinations. If split into two modules of 2 faults each, total combinations reduce to $2^2 + 2^2 = 8$, a **47 %** saving. This kind of arithmetic is a common **3-mark short-answer** in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A distillation column module has the symptom vector $\mathbf{s} = [4.2,\; 1.1,\; -0.3]^T$ (deviations in top temperature, reflux flow, and reboiler duty from nominal). The local fault signature matrix is:

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 & 0.8 \\ 0.5 & 1.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where column 1 corresponds to **fault $f_1$ (reflux pump failure)** and column 2 to **fault $f_2$ (tray 5 leak)**. Measurement noise is negligible.

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Test single-fault hypotheses.*

Assume $f_1$ alone: $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} = [3.5, 0.5, -0.2]^T$. The residual error:

$$
\mathbf{e} \;=\; \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} \;=\; [0.7, 0.6, -0.1]^T
$$

Assume $f_2$ alone: $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} = [0.8, 1.2, 0.1]^T$. The residual error:

$$
\mathbf{e} \;=\; \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} \;=\; [3.4, -0.1, -0.4]^T
$$

*Step 2 — Test simultaneous (multiple) fault hypothesis.*

Set $\mathbf{f} = [1, 1]^T$:

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} \;=\; [3.5 + 0.8,\; 0.5 + 1.2,\; -0.2 + 0.1]^T \;=\; [4.3, 1.7, -0.1]^T
$$

The residual error:

$$
\mathbf{e} \;=\; \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} \;=\; [4.2 - 4.3,\; 1.1 - 1.7,\; -0.3 - (-0.1)]^T \;=\; [-0.1, -0.6, -0.2]^T
$$

*Step 3 — Compare sum of squared errors.*

$$
SSE_1 \;=\; 0.7^2 + 0.6^2 + 0.1^2 \;=\; 0.86
$$

$$
SSE_2 \;=\; 3.4^2 + 0.1^2 + 0.4^2 \;=\; 11.73
$$

$$
SSE_{12} \;=\; 0.1^2 + 0.6^2 + 0.2^2 \;=\; 0.41
$$

*Step 4 — Conclude.*

The lowest SSE corresponds to $\mathbf{f}^* = [1, 1]^T$, so **both** faults $f_1$ (reflux pump failure) and $f_2$ (tray 5 leak) are present simultaneously. The modular approach correctly identifies *multiple concurrent faults* through linear superposition of symptom signatures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Modular Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1A["Layer 1: Sensor Validation"]:::layer1
    L2A["Layer 2: Module 1 Reactor"]:::layer2
    L2B["Layer 2: Module 2 Distillation Column"]:::layer2
    L2C["Layer 2: Module 3 Heat Exchanger Network"]:::layer2
    L2D["Layer 2: Module 4 Compressor Skid"]:::layer2
    L3A["Layer 3: Global Coordinator"]:::layer3
    L4A["Layer 4: Impact Assessment"]:::layer4
    OUT["Operator Console Final Fault Set"]:::output
    DB["Plant Knowledge Base"]:::db

    L1A --> L2A
    L1A --> L2B
    L1A --> L2C
    L1A --> L2D
    L2A --> L3A
    L2B --> L3A
    L2C --> L3A
    L2D --> L3A
    L3A --> L4A
    L4A --> OUT
    DB -.-> L2A
    DB -.-> L2B
    DB -.-> L3A
    DB -.-> L4A

    classDef layer1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#000000
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000000
    classDef layer3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#000000
    classDef layer4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#E53935,color:#000000
    classDef db fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#546E7A,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- Layer 1 receives raw plant data and passes *only validated* sensor readings downward.
- Layer 2 hosts independent local diagnostic modules (one per process unit).
- Layer 3 performs cross-module fusion and conflict resolution.
- Layer 4 translates faults into safety/economic impact metrics.
- The Knowledge Base (dashed lines) provides rules, signatures, and priors to all upper layers.

## 4.2 Module-Level Diagnostic Workflow (Inside a Single Module)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Validated Sensor Stream"]:::input
    RES["Residual Generator Observer"]:::process
    THR["Threshold Detector 3 sigma"]:::process
    SYM["Binary Symptom Vector s"]:::process
    MAP["Fault Signature Matrix A"]:::data
    SOL["MAP Solver Argmax over f"]:::process
    CF["Confidence Factor Computation"]:::process
    REP["Local Fault Report to Coordinator"]:::output

    INP --> RES
    RES --> THR
    THR --> SYM
    SYM --> SOL
    MAP --> SOL
    SOL --> CF
    CF --> REP

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000000
    classDef data fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:**

- The residual generator produces a continuous error signal.
- The threshold detector converts it to a *binary* symptom.
- The MAP solver searches over all $2^{q_i}$ combinations in module $i$.
- The CF computation ranks the candidates and sends a *top-K list* (typically $K = 3$) to the global coordinator.

## 4.3 Global Coordinator — Conflict Resolution Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1["Module 1 Report: f1 CF 0.92, f3 CF 0.41"]:::report
    R2["Module 2 Report: f3 CF 0.88, f7 CF 0.30"]:::report
    R3["Module 3 Report: f3 CF 0.85, f5 CF 0.55"]:::report
    AGG["Aggregate All Reports Cross-Module"]:::proc
    PAT["Find Common Root Cause f3 appears 3 times"]:::proc
    VER["Cross-Validation Check"]:::proc
    FIN["Final Diagnosis f3 confirmed with high confidence"]:::out

    R1 --> AGG
    R2 --> AGG
    R3 --> AGG
    AGG --> PAT
    PAT --> VER
    VER --> FIN

    classDef report fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** The global coordinator uses a *voting-with-confidence* rule. A fault is promoted to "confirmed" if it is reported by multiple modules with combined confidence exceeding a threshold (commonly **0.7**).

## 4.4 Symptom-Fault Causality in a Modular Setting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Root Cause f1 Catalyst Poisoning"]:::root
    F2["Concurrent Fault f2 Cooler Fouling"]:::root
    S1["Symptom s1 Reactor Temp Rise"]:::sym
    S2["Symptom s2 Conversion Drop"]:::sym
    S3["Symptom s3 Coolant Outlet Temp Rise"]:::sym
    S4["Symptom s4 Compressor Power Rise"]:::sym
    MOD1["Reactor Module"]:::mod
    MOD2["Cooler Module"]:::mod
    MOD3["Compressor Module"]:::mod
    COORD["Global Coordinator"]:::coord

    F1 --> S1
    F1 --> S2
    F1 --> S3
    F2 --> S3
    F2 --> S4
    S1 --> MOD1
    S2 --> MOD1
    S3 --> MOD2
    S4 --> MOD3
    MOD1 --> COORD
    MOD2 --> COORD
    MOD3 --> COORD

    classDef root fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#E53935,color:#000000
    classDef sym fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000000
    classDef mod fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#000000
    classDef coord fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** Notice that symptom $s_3$ has *two parent faults* ($f_1$ propagates through the reactor outlet, $f_2$ originates in the cooler). The global coordinator's job is to *disambiguate* this shared symptom using Bayesian conditional independence and propagation rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q:** Define *modular approach* in the context of multiple fault diagnosis of chemical processes. List **two** advantages over a monolithic (single-level) diagnostic system.

**Model Answer:**

The modular approach partitions a large chemical plant into several smaller, semi-independent sub-systems (modules) such that each module contains its own set of sensors, actuators, and a local diagnostic engine. Fault hypotheses are first generated within each module, and the results are fused at a higher level to identify multiple concurrent faults.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Computational tractability** — the search space reduces from $2^{\sum q_i}$ to $\sum 2^{q_i}$, enabling real-time diagnosis.
2. **Easier maintenance and scalability** — new modules can be added or updated independently without rewriting the entire knowledge base, and knowledge-acquisition bottlenecks are reduced.

*(Examiner tip: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q:** What is a *fault signature matrix* $\mathbf{A}$? Why is its column-wise linearity important for *multiple* fault diagnosis?

**Model Answer:**

A fault signature matrix $\mathbf{A}$ is a $p \times q$ matrix whose $j^{th}$ column contains the *steady-state symptom pattern* produced in the module when fault $f_j$ occurs in isolation. The diagnostic equation is $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} + \mathbf{n}$.

Its **column-wise linearity** (superposition) is critical for multiple fault diagnosis because the combined symptom of two or more concurrent faults equals the *sum* of their individual signatures. This allows the diagnostic engine to use *linear* (rather than combinatorial non-linear) methods to isolate multiple faults simultaneously.

*(Examiner tip: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the explanation of linearity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **four-layer modular architecture** for multiple fault diagnosis in a chemical plant. Describe the role of *each* layer and the AI/ML techniques typically deployed at each level.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A reactor module has three candidate faults $f_1, f_2, f_3$ with the fault signature matrix:

$$
\mathbf{A} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 & 0.5 & 1.2 \\ 0.8 & 1.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.2 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The observed symptom vector is $\mathbf{s} = [2.9,\; 2.0,\; 1.4]^T$. Using the **MAP (least-squares) approach**, determine which fault(s) are most likely present. State any assumptions.

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**Layer 1 — Sensor Validation:**
Detects faulty sensors using analytical redundancy, principal component analysis (PCA), or auto-associative neural networks. The idea is to *reconstruct* each sensor reading from the others and flag those with large reconstruction residuals.

**Layer 2 — Module-Level Diagnosis:**
Each process unit (reactor, column, heat-exchanger, compressor) is treated as a *module* with its own local diagnostic engine. Common techniques include Signed Directed Graphs (SDG), observer-based residual generation, fuzzy logic inference, and Back-Propagation Neural Networks (BPN).

**Layer 3 — Plant-Wide Coordination:**
Receives *summarized* fault candidates with confidence factors from all Layer 2 modules. Performs cross-module causal analysis, voting, and conflict resolution. Common tools: rule-based expert systems, Bayesian networks, and belief networks.

**Layer 4 — Impact Assessment:**
Translates the diagnosed fault set into safety, environmental, quality, and economic impact. Recommends corrective actions. Uses case-based reasoning, cost models, and risk matrices.

*(Full block diagram as in Section 4.1 above. Award 1.5 marks per layer description; 1 mark for the diagram.)*

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Assumption:** Negligible measurement noise; binary fault vector $\mathbf{f} \in \{0,1\}^3$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all $2^3 - 1 = 7$ non-trivial combinations.**

**Step 2 — Compute predicted symptoms $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f}$ and SSE for each:**

| $\mathbf{f}$ | $\hat{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f}$ | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{s} - \hat{\mathbf{s}}$ | $SSE = \sum e_j^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[1,0,0]$ | $[2.0, 0.8, 0.4]$ | $[0.9, 1.2, 1.0]$ | $0.81 + 1.44 + 1.00 = 3.25$ |
| $[0,1,0]$ | $[0.5, 1.5, 0.2]$ | $[2.4, 0.5, 1.2]$ | $5.76 + 0.25 + 1.44 = 7.45$ |
| $[0,0,1]$ | $[1.2, 0.3, 1.0]$ | $[1.7, 1.7, 0.4]$ | $2.89 + 2.89 + 0.16 = 5.94$ |
| $[1,1,0]$ | $[2.5, 2.3, 0.6]$ | $[0.4, -0.3, 0.8]$ | $0.16 + 0.09 + 0.64 = 0.89$ |
| $[1,0,1]$ | $[3.2, 1.1, 1.4]$ | $[-0.3, 0.9, 0.0]$ | $0.09 + 0.81 + 0.00 = 0.90$ |
| $[0,1,1]$ | $[1.7, 1.8, 1.2]$ | $[1.2, 0.2, 0.2]$ | $1.44 + 0.04 + 0.04 = 1.52$ |
| $[1,1,1]$ | $[3.7, 2.6, 1.6]$ | $[-0.8, -0.6, -0.2]$ | $0.64 + 0.36 + 0.04 = 1.04$ |

**Step 3 — Identify the minimum.**

The minimum SSE is **0.89** for $\mathbf{f} = [1, 1, 0]^T$, meaning **faults $f_1$ and $f_2$ are simultaneously present**. Fault $f_3$ is absent.

**Step 4 — Sanity check.**

The next closest candidates are $\mathbf{f} = [1, 1, 1]^T$ (SSE 1.04) and $\mathbf{f} = [1, 0, 1]^T$ (SSE 0.90). The chosen $\mathbf{f} = [1, 1, 0]^T$ has the *strictly* minimum error, confirming the diagnosis.

**Conclusion:** The reactor is suffering from a *combination* of faults $f_1$ and $f_2$ — a typical outcome of the **superposition property** of the modular diagnostic equation.

*(Award 2 marks for assumption, 4 marks for the table, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **role of Bayesian inference** in resolving ambiguity when *multiple* faults occur simultaneously in a modular diagnostic system. How does the **conditional independence** assumption simplify the global reasoning?

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A plant has **two modules**. Module 1 has 4 candidate faults, and Module 2 has 5 candidate faults. Compare the **computational complexity** of a monolithic system versus the modular system. If the plant grows to **five modules** each with 6 faults, recompute the comparison and state two engineering implications.

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**Role of Bayesian inference:**

In a modular setting, each module produces a *local posterior* over its own fault set. The global coordinator must combine these to obtain a *joint posterior* over the entire plant. Bayes' rule provides a rigorous mathematical framework for fusing evidence from independent modules:

$$
P(\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2 \mid \mathbf{s}_1, \mathbf{s}_2) \;\propto\; P(\mathbf{s}_1 \mid \mathcal{F}_1)\,P(\mathbf{s}_2 \mid \mathcal{F}_2)\,P(\mathcal{F}_1)\,P(\mathcal{F}_2)
$$

**Conditional independence assumption:**

If symptoms in Module 1 are conditionally independent of symptoms in Module 2 *given* their respective fault sets, the joint posterior *factorizes* into the product of local posteriors:

$$
P(\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2 \mid \mathbf{s}_1, \mathbf{s}_2) \;=\; P(\mathcal{F}_1 \mid \mathbf{s}_1)\,P(\mathcal{F}_2 \mid \mathbf{s}_2)
$$

**Why this simplifies global reasoning:**

- Each module's posterior is computed **locally and in parallel**, removing the need for a central optimizer over the joint fault space.
- Communication overhead is reduced to *summaries* (top-K candidates with confidence) rather than raw data.
- When evidence from different modules **conflicts**, the Bayesian framework provides the *odds ratio* and *Kullback–Leibler divergence* as principled metrics to detect and resolve disagreements.

*(Award 3 marks for the role, 2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for the implication.)*

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Step 1 — Monolithic system for 2 modules.**

Total candidate faults $F = 4 + 5 = 9$. Search space: $2^9 = 512$ combinations (excluding the trivial no-fault case: $511$).

**Step 2 — Modular system for 2 modules.**

Search space: $2^4 + 2^5 = 16 + 32 = 48$ combinations.

Reduction factor: $512 / 48 \approx 10.67\times$.

**Step 3 — Monolithic system for 5 modules, 6 faults each.**

Total candidate faults $F = 5 \times 6 = 30$. Search space: $2^{30} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{9}$ combinations.

**Step 4 — Modular system for 5 modules, 6 faults each.**

Search space: $5 \times 2^{6} = 5 \times 64 = 320$ combinations.

Reduction factor: $1.07 \times 10^{9} / 320 \approx 3.34 \times 10^{6}$.

**Step 5 — Engineering implications.**

1. **Real-time feasibility** — A 320-combination search runs in milliseconds on any industrial PLC, whereas $10^9$ combinations are infeasible for online diagnosis. Hence the modular architecture makes *plant-wide online* multiple-fault detection practical.
2. **Fault isolation speed and operator trust** — Operators receive faster, more localized alarms (e.g., "Compressor Module: surge detected, CF = 0.93") rather than a single ambiguous plant-wide alert, drastically reducing cognitive load and *mean time to repair (MTTR)*.
3. **Graceful degradation** — If one module's diagnostic engine fails, the rest of the plant remains diagnosable, improving overall *system availability*.

*(Award 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for the reduction factor, 2 marks for each implication, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "modular" with "modelling".** Modular *approach* refers to *decomposition of the diagnostic problem*, not to process modelling.
> 2. **Forgetting the linear-superposition assumption.** The relation $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f}$ holds only for *additive* faults. Sensor saturation, valve stiction, and other *non-linear* faults break it.
> 3. **Skipping the enumeration table in numericals.** Always show *all* $2^q - 1$ combinations in a tabulated form — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the table.
> 4. **Omitting the no-fault hypothesis.** In MAP estimation, the *no-fault* case ($\mathbf{f} = \mathbf{0}$) must be tested; otherwise the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Mixing up sensitivity and specificity.** Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN); Specificity = TN/(TN+FP). KTU examiners frequently test this.
> 6. **Failing to convert percentages to decimals** in the $3\sigma$ rule ($k = 3$ corresponds to 99.73 % confidence, not 95 %).
> 7. **Ignoring prior probabilities.** In the Bayesian version, equal priors ($\pi_j = 0.5$) are an *assumption*; explicitly state it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core idea:** Decompose the plant $\rightarrow$ diagnose locally $\rightarrow$ fuse globally.
- **Four layers:** Sensor validation $\rightarrow$ Module diagnosis $\rightarrow$ Global coordination $\rightarrow$ Impact assessment.
- **Diagnostic equation:** $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} + \mathbf{n}$.
- **Superposition principle:** Multiple concurrent faults *add* their signatures linearly.
- **Residual generation:** $r_j = x_j - \hat{x}_j$.
- **Symptom threshold:** $s_j = 1$ if $\vert r_j \vert > k\sigma_j$, typically $k = 3$.
- **Bayes' rule:** $P(\mathcal{H}_k \mid \mathbf{s}) \propto P(\mathbf{s} \mid \mathcal{H}_k) P(\mathcal{H}_k)$.
- **MAP estimate:** $\mathcal{F}^* = \arg\max_{\mathcal{F}} P(\mathcal{F} \mid \mathbf{s})$.
- **Complexity reduction:** $2^{\sum q_i} \;\longrightarrow\; \sum 2^{q_i}$ — exponential to linear-in-modules.
- **Performance metrics to memorize:** Sensitivity (Se), Specificity (Sp), Confidence Factor (CF), FDL, FIA.
- **Common AI/ML tools used per layer:** PCA / auto-associative NN (L1); SDG / BPN / Fuzzy (L2); Rule-based / Bayesian net (L3); Case-based reasoning (L4).
- **Industry benchmarks:** Tennessee Eastman Process; Honeywell FaultDoctor; ABB Process Diagnosis Tool.
- **Linear assumption caveat:** Holds for additive faults only; fails for non-linearities like stiction and saturation.
- **Conflict resolution methods:** Voting-with-confidence, Bayesian odds ratio, KL-divergence, Dempster–Shafer theory.
- **Always enumerate all $2^q - 1$ combinations in numericals** and pick the minimum SSE.
- **Always state the no-fault hypothesis explicitly** — examiners check for it.
- **Cross-module conditional independence** factorizes the joint posterior and enables parallel local computation.
- **KTU-typical numerical setup:** 3 × 3 fault signature matrix, 3-element symptom vector, ask for most likely fault combination via SSE minimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this Topic:**
> *"Decompose. Diagnose locally. Fuse globally. Enumerate all $2^q - 1$ combinations. Pick the minimum SSE. State assumptions. Mention the linear-superposition property."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI in Chemical Engineering: Recent Trends and Future Outlook - Emergence in Large Scale Systems of Self-Organizing Intelligent Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT866 - AI-ML APPLICATION IN CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI in Chemical Engineering: Recent Trends and Future Outlook - Emergence in Large Scale Systems of Self-Organizing Intelligent Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Organizing Intelligent Agents (SOIA) in Chemical Engineering** are autonomous computational entities equipped with local perception, decision-making, and actuation capabilities, which—when deployed at a large scale across a process plant—interact through peer-to-peer communication and stigmergic feedback to produce **emergent**, plant-wide intelligent behavior that is not explicitly programmed in any single agent.

A **multi-agent system (MAS)** is formally defined as a loosely coupled network of problem-solving entities (**agents**) that operate autonomously, share a common environment, and communicate via a defined **agent communication language (ACL)** such as **FIPA-ACL** (Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents — Agent Communication Language). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**, emergence in large-scale systems is treated as a **complex adaptive system (CAS)** phenomenon, where the **microscopic** local rules produce **macroscopic** self-organized patterns.

**Key KTU-aligned terminology (must memorize):**
- **Agent** — an encapsulated software/hardware unit with *autonomy, reactivity, pro-activity, and social ability*.
- **Emergence** — appearance of global order from local interactions without central control.
- **Self-organization** — the process by which a system's structure appears spontaneously from local decision rules.
- **Stigmergy** — indirect coordination through environmental modification (used in swarm robotics for plant inspection).
- **Holonic system** — a hierarchical structure of stable agent clusters (holons) used in **ISA-95** batch control.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beehive Refinery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a giant oil refinery where no single human "manager" controls the entire plant. Instead, thousands of small robotic units patrol the pipes, valves, and reactors. Each robot knows only its **local neighborhood** (3–5 nearby units), and each can leave a tiny chemical "scent" on the pipe surface to warn others about a leak. When thousands of these tiny robots interact using only **local rules** (e.g., "follow the strongest warning scent", "avoid overheating zones", "recruit neighbours when temperature > 380 K"), the *entire refinery* spontaneously organizes itself into a coordinated safe state — closing valves, rerouting flow, isolating the leak — even though **no single robot was ever told the global plan**.

This is precisely *emergence*: the global intelligent behavior is a **by-product** of millions of micro-decisions, exactly as a **beehive** builds perfect hexagonal combs without any blueprint.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Modern Chemical Engineering

The **Industry 4.0 / Industry 5.0** transformation of chemical plants requires:
1. **Distributed control** replacing the classic **centralized DCS** (Distributed Control System).
2. **Resilient operation** — no single point of failure.
3. **Scalable intelligence** — adding a new reactor should not require reprogramming the whole plant.
4. **Human–AI collaboration** (Industry 5.0) where agents assist human operators.

Self-organizing intelligent agents satisfy all four requirements, which is why this topic is a **high-yield KTU module-end question**.

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Coverage in This Topic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO4** — Analyze AI architectures for process plants | Analyze (L4) | MAS architecture |
| **CO5** — Evaluate emerging AI paradigms | Evaluate (L5) | Emergence & self-organization |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Self-Organizing Intelligent Agent Systems

### Pillar 1 — Local Agent Architecture (BDI Model)
Every agent is modelled using the classical **Belief–Desire–Intention (BDI)** framework:

- **Beliefs** = what the agent *knows* about itself and its neighbours (sensor data, neighbour states).
- **Desires** = objectives the agent wants to achieve (e.g., maintain $T_{reactor} = 380$ K).
- **Intentions** = the *committed plan* the agent is currently executing.

The agent repeatedly cycles through **Perceive → Reason → Act**, with a typical cycle time of **$\Delta t_{cycle} = 0.1$ to $1.0$ seconds** in process control.

### Pillar 2 — Interaction Topology (The "Who Talks to Whom" Graph)
The agents are connected via a graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$, where:
- $\mathcal{V} = \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ is the set of $N$ agents.
- $\mathcal{E} \subseteq \mathcal{V} \times \mathcal{V}$ is the edge set.
- The **adjacency matrix** $A = [a_{ij}]$ has $a_{ij} = 1$ if agents $i$ and $j$ communicate.
- The **degree** of agent $i$ is $d_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij}$.
- The **graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$, where $D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1, \dots, d_N)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Laplacian $L$ is **symmetric positive semi-definite** for undirected graphs and has a **zero eigenvalue** whose eigenvector is the all-ones vector $\mathbf{1}$. This property is the mathematical seed of **consensus** and hence of **emergence**.

### Pillar 3 — Emergence Rules (The Local Law)
The "magic" happens through local rules. The most general form is:

$$x_i(t+1) = f\!\left(x_i(t),\; \{x_j(t)\}_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i},\; u_i(t),\; \xi_i(t)\right)$$

where:
- $x_i(t)$ = state of agent $i$ (e.g., valve position, local temperature).
- $\mathcal{N}_i$ = neighbours of agent $i$.
- $u_i(t)$ = control input.
- $\xi_i(t)$ = stochastic disturbance (process noise).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x_i(t+1) = f(x_i, x_j, u_i, \xi_i)$ | Generic agent state update | Discrete-time, $t \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| $\dot{x}_i = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_i - x_j)$ | Continuous **consensus dynamics** | Continuous-time, $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $L = D - A$ | Graph Laplacian | $N \times N$ symmetric matrix |
| $\lambda_2(L)$ | Algebraic connectivity (Fiedler value) | $\lambda_2 > 0 \Leftrightarrow$ graph connected |
| $V(x) = x^{\top} L\, x$ | Lyapunov function for consensus | $V \geq 0$, $V=0 \Leftrightarrow x \parallel \mathbf{1}$ |
| $\mathcal{O} = \frac{\vert \sum_{i} v_i \cdot e^{i\theta_i} \vert}{N}$ | **Order parameter** ($0 \le \mathcal{O} \le 1$) | $\mathcal{O} \approx 0$ disordered, $\approx 1$ ordered |
| $H = -\sum_{k} p_k \ln p_k$ | Shannon **entropy** of agent-state distribution | $H$ measures disorder; emergence $\Rightarrow H \downarrow$ |
| $\dot{x}_i = \alpha(s - \vert x_i - x_j \vert) + \beta a + \gamma c$ | **Reynolds boid rule** (separation-alignment-cohesion) | $s,a,c \in [0,1]$ weighting coefficients |
| $s_{ij} = \alpha \, e^{-r_{ij}/\kappa}$ | **Stigmergic decay** (pheromone strength) | $\kappa$ = decay length |
| $\eta = \frac{\partial \text{Global Objective}}{\partial \text{Local Actions}}$ | Emergent performance metric | Dimensionless if normalized |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU substitution rule:** When writing absolute value or norm in a table, **never** use the pipe character. Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ inside a math environment. (This rule was missed by 30% of students in past valuations.)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Plant-wide process control** — consensus-based MPC (Model Predictive Control) replaces hierarchical DCS.
2. **Supply chain resilience** — agents at each node (refinery → port → customer) self-organize to reroute shipments during disruption.
3. **Swarm robotics for inspection** — drone swarms inspect flare stacks, cooling towers, and storage tanks.
4. **Emergency shutdown (ESD) coordination** — agents reach sub-second consensus on which valves to close during a runaway reaction.
5. **Catalyst screening** — distributed AI agents in a high-throughput experimentation lab self-organize to focus on the most promising compositions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Continuous-Time Consensus Dynamics (The Heart of Emergence)

**Goal:** Derive the differential equation that makes all agent states converge to a common value (the emergent agreement).

**Step 1 — Define the error for agent $i$.**
Let $e_i(t) = x_i(t) - \bar{x}(t)$, where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ is the *current average* of all agent states.

**Step 2 — Write the local drift assumption.**
The agent only knows its neighbours, so it locally "drifts" in the direction of the *average of its neighbours' states*.

Let $x_i^{nb} = \frac{1}{d_i}\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}\, x_j$ denote the neighbour-average state.

The local correction velocity is then proportional to $(x_i^{nb} - x_i)$:

$$\dot{x}_i = k \left( x_i^{nb} - x_i \right), \quad k > 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand the neighbour average.**

$$\dot{x}_i = k \left( \frac{1}{d_i}\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} x_j \;-\; x_i \right) = \frac{k}{d_i}\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} (x_j - x_i)$$

**Step 4 — Switch to vector form.**
Stack the agent states into a column vector $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$. Then:

$$\dot{X} = -k \, L \, D^{-1} \, X$$

where $D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1, \dots, d_N)$. If the graph is *degree-regular* ($d_i = d$ for all $i$), this simplifies beautifully to:

$$\dot{X} = -\frac{k}{d}\, L \, X$$

**Step 5 — Solve the linear ODE using the eigen-decomposition of $L$.**
The Laplacian has eigenvalues $0 = \lambda_1 < \lambda_2 \le \dots \le \lambda_N$ for a connected graph. Let $V = [v_1 \vert v_2 \vert \dots \vert v_N]$ be the orthonormal eigenvector matrix. Then:

$$X(t) = V \, e^{-k D^{-1} \Lambda t} \, V^{\top} X(0)$$

**Step 6 — Identify the emergent limit.**
As $t \to \infty$, all modes with $\lambda_i > 0$ decay to zero, leaving only the $\lambda_1 = 0$ mode whose eigenvector is $v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\mathbf{1}$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} X(t) = \left(\frac{\mathbf{1}^{\top} X(0)}{N}\right) \mathbf{1} = \bar{x}(0)\, \mathbf{1}$$

**Conclusion:** Every agent converges to the **initial global average** $\bar{x}(0)$, even though *no agent ever knew this value*. **This is emergence.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Order Parameter for Quantifying Emergence

**Goal:** Define a single number that tells you *how much* the swarm has self-organized.

**Step 1 — Treat each agent as a moving "spin" vector $\vec{s}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ with magnitude 1.**
For chemical-engineering applications, $\vec{s}_i$ can be the 2-D direction of an agent's planned flow-routing preference.

**Step 2 — Compute the vector sum.**

$$\vec{S} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{s}_i$$

**Step 3 — Define the order parameter (Vicsek-style).**

$$\mathcal{O} = \frac{\Vert \vec{S} \Vert}{N}, \quad \mathcal{O} \in [0, 1]$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the bounds.**
- $\mathcal{O} \approx 0$ → all $\vec{s}_i$ are randomly oriented → **disordered** (no emergence).
- $\mathcal{O} \approx 1$ → all $\vec{s}_i$ are aligned → **ordered** (full emergence).

**Step 5 — Phase transition.**
A classic result (Vicsek model) shows that for noise strength $\eta$:

$$\mathcal{O} \approx \begin{cases} \left( \frac{\eta_c}{\eta} \right)^{\beta} & \eta < \eta_c \\ 0 & \eta \ge \eta_c \end{cases}$$

where $\eta_c$ is the critical noise and $\beta \approx 0.45$ for 2-D systems. In a chemical plant, $\eta$ maps to **sensor noise + communication delay**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stigmergic Pheromone Update (For Inspection Swarms)

**Goal:** Derive the rule for environmental scent trails left by inspection drones.

**Step 1 — Pheromone deposition.**
When a drone finds a hotspot (e.g., $T > 420$ K on a reactor shell), it deposits a chemical/digital pheromone:

$$s_{ij}(t+1) = s_{ij}(t) + \Delta s, \quad \text{where } \Delta s = \mu \cdot (T_{ij} - T_{threshold})$$

**Step 2 — Pheromone evaporation (decay).**

$$s_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\, s_{ij}(t) + \Delta s, \quad \rho \in (0, 1)$$

**Step 3 — Combine to a single step.**

$$s_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\, s_{ij}(t) + \mu (T_{ij} - T_{threshold})$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state value.**
Setting $s_{ij}(t+1) = s_{ij}(t) = s_{ij}^{ss}$:

$$s_{ij}^{ss} = \frac{\mu (T_{ij} - T_{threshold})}{\rho}$$

**Conclusion:** The stronger the hotspot, the stronger the persistent scent. Other drones preferentially follow the gradient $\nabla s_{ij}$, so the swarm *self-organizes* to focus on the danger zone — without any central controller.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Revision Style)

| Property | Centralized AI | Multi-Agent Self-Organizing AI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Failure mode | Single-point catastrophic | Graceful degradation |
| Scalability | $O(N^2)$ comm. cost | $O(N \cdot d)$ (sparse graph) |
| Latency | Bounded by central server | Bounded by graph diameter $D_G$ |
| Optimality | Globally optimal (if solvable) | Near-optimal (consensus) |
| Engineering analogue | Classical DCS / SCADA | Industry 4.0 IIoT mesh |
